narrative:
"Suppose that we make Socrates a party to the argument?"

"If you please."
refashioned:
"What if we bring Socrates into this debate?"

"By all means."
elegant:
"Bring Socrates in?"

"Yes."
summary: Hermogenes proposes inviting Socrates to join a dispute he has been having with Cratylus about the nature of names. Cratylus agrees.
distilled: Hermogenes and Cratylus agree to bring Socrates into their disagreement about names.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Cratylus. -- This brief exchange sets the dramatic frame for the entire dialogue. Hermogenes and Cratylus have reached an impasse over whether names are natural or conventional, and they turn to Socrates as arbiter -- a recurring Platonic device where Socrates enters mid-argument (compare the opening of the Protagoras). The Cratylus is Plato's only dialogue devoted entirely to the philosophy of language, and this opening immediately establishes its central question: do words connect to things by nature or by human agreement?

narrative: "I should explain to you, Socrates, that our friend Cratylus has been arguing about names; he says that they are natural and not conventional; not a portion of the human voice which men agree to use; but that there is a truth or correctness in them, which is the same for Hellenes as for barbarians. Whereupon I ask him, whether his own name of Cratylus is a true name or not, and he answers 'Yes.' And Socrates? 'Yes.' Then every man's name, as I tell him, is that which he is called. To this he replies -- 'If all the world were to call you Hermogenes, that would not be your name.' And when I am anxious to have a further explanation he is ironical and mysterious, and seems to imply that he has a notion of his own about the matter, if he would only tell, and could entirely convince me, if he chose to be intelligible. Tell me, Socrates, what this oracle means; or rather tell me, if you will be so good, what is your own view of the truth or correctness of names, which I would far sooner hear."
refashioned: "Let me fill you in, Socrates. Our friend Cratylus here has been making an argument about names. He claims that names are natural rather than conventional -- that they are not just sounds people agree to use, but that each name has a built-in correctness that holds true for Greeks and foreigners alike. So I tested him: 'Is your own name, Cratylus, a true name?' He said yes. 'And Socrates?' Also yes. 'Then,' I said, 'every person's name is simply whatever they are called.' But he shot back: 'Even if the entire world called you Hermogenes, that still would not be your real name.' When I pressed him for an explanation, he turned cryptic and evasive -- hinting that he has some deeper theory he could share, one that would completely win me over, if only he bothered to make it clear. So tell me, Socrates -- what does this riddle of his mean? Or better yet, tell me your own view on whether names have a natural correctness. That is what I really want to hear."
elegant: "Socrates, Cratylus claims names are natural, not conventional -- that each name has a built-in correctness, universal across all peoples. When I tested this by arguing that everyone's name is simply what they are called, he replied that even if the whole world called me Hermogenes, that would not truly be my name. But he refuses to explain further. What do you think -- do names have a natural correctness?"
summary: Hermogenes explains the dispute to Socrates: Cratylus holds that names are naturally correct, not merely conventional, while Hermogenes believes a name is just whatever people agree to call something. Cratylus has been evasive about his reasoning, so Hermogenes asks Socrates for his view.
distilled: Hermogenes presents the core dispute -- Cratylus claims names are naturally correct while Hermogenes sees them as mere convention -- and asks Socrates to weigh in.
notes: Speaker: Hermogenes. -- This passage lays out the two opposed positions that structure the entire dialogue: Cratylus's naturalism (names have inherent correctness) versus Hermogenes's conventionalism (names are arbitrary agreements). The name 'Hermogenes' means 'born of Hermes,' the god of commerce and gain -- a name Cratylus considers unfitting since Hermogenes is famously poor (as Socrates will note in 1.3). Cratylus's refusal to explain his position echoes his historical association with Heraclitean philosophy and its reputation for obscurity. The naturalism/conventionalism debate about language resurfaces throughout Western philosophy, from Aristotle's De Interpretatione through Saussure's theory of the arbitrary sign.

narrative: "Son of Hipponicus, there is an ancient saying, that 'hard is the knowledge of the good.' And the knowledge of names is a great part of knowledge. If I had not been poor, I might have heard the fifty-drachma course of the great Prodicus, which is a complete education in grammar and language -- these are his own words -- and then I should have been at once able to answer your question about the correctness of names. But, indeed, I have only heard the single-drachma course, and therefore, I do not know the truth about such matters; I will, however, gladly assist you and Cratylus in the investigation of them. When he declares that your name is not really Hermogenes, I suspect that he is only making fun of you; -- he means to say that you are no true son of Hermes, because you are always looking after a fortune and never in luck. But, as I was saying, there is a good deal of difficulty in this sort of knowledge, and therefore we had better leave the question open until we have heard both sides."
refashioned:
"Son of Hipponicus, there is an old saying: 'Hard is the knowledge of the good.' And the knowledge of names is no small part of knowledge itself. If I were not so poor, I might have attended the full fifty-drachma lecture course of the great Prodicus -- which he advertises as a complete education in grammar and language, his own words -- and then I would have been able to answer your question about the correctness of names right away. But as it stands, I only attended his one-drachma course, so I do not possess the truth about these matters. Still, I will gladly join you and Cratylus in investigating the question.

As for his claim that your name is not really Hermogenes -- I suspect he is just teasing you. What he means is that you are no true 'son of Hermes,' since Hermes is the god of profitable gain, and you are always chasing a fortune but never catching one. But as I was saying, this kind of knowledge is genuinely difficult, so we had better keep the question open until we have heard both sides."
elegant: "There is an old saying: 'Hard is the knowledge of the good' -- and the knowledge of names is a great part of knowledge. I am too poor to have taken Prodicus's full course on language, so I cannot answer your question outright. But I will gladly investigate it with you. As for Cratylus denying you the name Hermogenes -- I suspect he is joking that you are no true 'son of Hermes,' since you are always chasing wealth but never finding it. Let us hear both sides before deciding."
summary: Socrates disclaims expertise, joking that he could only afford the cheap version of Prodicus's language course. He suggests Cratylus is teasing Hermogenes about his name (meaning 'son of Hermes,' god of gain) since Hermogenes is always unlucky in money matters. He proposes they investigate the question together.
distilled: Socrates modestly disclaims expertise on names, jokes that Cratylus is teasing Hermogenes about being a poor 'son of Hermes,' and proposes a joint investigation.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- Socrates's characteristic ironic modesty is on full display: claiming ignorance while steering the inquiry. The reference to Prodicus of Ceos is significant -- Prodicus was a sophist famous for distinguishing the precise meanings of near-synonyms, making him a natural authority on 'correctness of names.' The joke about the one-drachma vs. fifty-drachma course also appears in the Protagoras (341a-c) and gently mocks the sophistic practice of selling wisdom at premium prices. The etymological joke about 'Hermogenes' (herma + genos = 'born of Hermes') anticipates the extensive etymological analyses that will dominate Part 2 of the dialogue. Hipponicus was Hermogenes's father and one of the wealthiest Athenians, making Hermogenes's own poverty ironic.

narrative: "I have often talked over this matter, both with Cratylus and others, and cannot convince myself that there is any principle of correctness in names other than convention and agreement; any name which you give, in my opinion, is the right one, and if you change that and give another, the new name is as correct as the old -- we frequently change the names of our slaves, and the newly-imposed name is as good as the old: for there is no name given to anything by nature; all is convention and habit of the users; -- such is my view. But if I am mistaken I shall be happy to hear and learn of Cratylus, or of any one else."
refashioned: "I have discussed this question many times, with Cratylus and with others, and I simply cannot bring myself to believe that there is any standard of correctness for names beyond convention and mutual agreement. In my view, whatever name you assign to something is the right name, and if you change it and assign a different one, the new name is just as correct as the old. We change the names of our slaves all the time, and the newly given name works just as well as the original. No name belongs to anything by nature -- it is all a matter of convention and the habits of those who use the name. That is my position. But if I am wrong, I am perfectly willing to hear the case and learn from Cratylus or anyone else."
elegant: "I remain convinced: there is no correctness in names beyond convention and agreement. Any name you assign is the right one; change it, and the new name is equally correct. We rename our slaves constantly, and the new name serves just as well. Nothing has a name by nature -- all is convention and habit."
summary: Hermogenes states his position clearly: names are purely conventional, with no natural correctness. He cites the practice of renaming slaves as evidence that any name works as well as any other. He remains open to being persuaded otherwise.
distilled: Hermogenes argues that names are entirely conventional, citing the easy renaming of slaves as proof that no name belongs to anything by nature.
notes: Speaker: Hermogenes. -- Hermogenes articulates the conventionalist position on language that anticipates Aristotle's view in De Interpretatione (16a) that words signify 'by convention' (kata syntheken). The example of renaming slaves is both practically grounded in Athenian life and philosophically significant: it suggests names are arbitrary labels imposed by power rather than natural properties. This position aligns loosely with what would later be called the 'arbitrariness of the sign' in Saussurean linguistics. Hermogenes's openness to correction is characteristic of the good-faith interlocutor in Platonic dialogues, contrasting with more combative sophists.

narrative:
"I dare say that you may be right, Hermogenes: let us see; -- Your meaning is, that the name of each thing is only that which anybody agrees to call it?"

"That is my notion."

"Whether the giver of the name be an individual or a city?"

"Yes."

"Well, now, let me take an instance; -- suppose that I call a man a horse or a horse a man, you mean to say that a man will be rightly called a horse by me individually, and rightly called a man by the rest of the world; and a horse again would be rightly called a man by me and a horse by the world: -- that is your meaning?"

"He would, according to my view."

"But how about truth, then? you would acknowledge that there is in words a true and a false?"

"Certainly."

"And there are true and false propositions?"

"To be sure."

"And a true proposition says that which is, and a false proposition says that which is not?"

"Yes; what other answer is possible?"

"Then in a proposition there is a true and false?"

"Certainly."

"But is a proposition true as a whole only, and are the parts untrue?"

"No; the parts are true as well as the whole."

"Would you say the large parts and not the smaller ones, or every part?"

"I should say that every part is true."

"Is a proposition resolvable into any part smaller than a name?"

"No; that is the smallest."

"Then the name is a part of the true proposition?"

"Yes."

"Yes, and a true part, as you say."

"Yes."

"And is not the part of a falsehood also a falsehood?"

"Yes."

"Then, if propositions may be true and false, names may be true and false?"

"So we must infer."

"And the name of anything is that which any one affirms to be the name?"

"Yes."

"And will there be so many names of each thing as everybody says that there are? and will they be true names at the time of uttering them?"

"Yes, Socrates, I can conceive no correctness of names other than this; you give one name, and I another; and in different cities and countries there are different names for the same things; Hellenes differ from barbarians in their use of names, and the several Hellenic tribes from one another."
refashioned:
"Perhaps you are right, Hermogenes. Let us examine this. You are saying that the name of each thing is simply whatever anyone agrees to call it?"

"That is exactly my view."

"Whether the person assigning the name is a single individual or an entire city?"

"Yes."

"Very well, let me test this with an example. Suppose I decide to call a man a 'horse' and a horse a 'man.' You are saying that the man would be correctly called a horse by me personally, and correctly called a man by everyone else; and likewise the horse would be correctly called a man by me and a horse by the rest of the world -- is that right?"

"Yes, that follows from my view."

"But what about truth? You would agree that words can be true or false?"

"Of course."

"And that there are true propositions and false propositions?"

"Certainly."

"And a true proposition states what is the case, while a false proposition states what is not the case?"

"Yes -- what other answer could there be?"

"So a proposition carries truth or falsehood?"

"Yes."

"But does truth belong only to the proposition as a whole, or also to its parts?"

"The parts are true as well as the whole."

"The large parts only, or every part, no matter how small?"

"Every part."

"And is there any component of a proposition smaller than a name?"

"No, a name is the smallest unit."

"So the name is a part of a true proposition?"

"Yes."

"And a true part, as you just said."

"Yes."

"And is not a part of a falsehood also false?"

"Yes."

"Then if propositions can be true or false, names can also be true or false?"

"That does seem to follow."

"And yet you say the name of anything is simply whatever anyone declares it to be?"

"Yes."

"Then will there be as many names for each thing as there are people willing to name it? And will all of those names be equally true at the moment they are spoken?"

"Yes, Socrates. I can think of no standard of correctness for names other than this. You give one name, I give another; different cities and countries have different names for the same things. Greeks differ from foreigners in what they call things, and the various Greek peoples differ from one another."
elegant: Socrates tests Hermogenes's conventionalism with a pointed example: if a name is just whatever anyone agrees to call something, then I could correctly call a man a 'horse' and a horse a 'man,' and both labels would be right -- mine for me, yours for you. Hermogenes accepts this consequence. But Socrates presses further: you admit that propositions can be true or false? Yes. And the parts of a proposition share in its truth or falsehood? Yes. And the smallest part of a proposition is a name? Yes. Then names themselves must be capable of truth and falsehood. Yet you say any name anyone assigns is automatically correct -- how do these claims fit together? Hermogenes holds his ground: he can conceive no other standard of correctness, pointing out that different cities, nations, and peoples use entirely different names for the very same things.
summary: Socrates draws out the implications of Hermogenes's conventionalism through a logical chain: if any name is correct just because someone uses it, then I could call a man a 'horse.' He then argues that since propositions can be true or false, and names are their smallest parts, names too must admit of truth and falsehood -- creating a tension with pure conventionalism. Hermogenes acknowledges the point but holds his position.
distilled: Socrates argues that if propositions can be true or false, and names are their smallest components, then names must also be capable of truth or falsehood -- challenging Hermogenes's view that any name is as good as any other.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Hermogenes. -- This passage contains Socrates's first dialectical move against conventionalism, employing what philosophers call a compositionality argument: the truth-value of a proposition distributes to its parts, including individual names. The definition of true and false propositions here -- 'a true proposition says that which is, a false proposition says that which is not' -- anticipates the fuller treatment of true and false speech in the Sophist (261c-263d), where the Eleatic Stranger similarly analyzes statements into names (onomata) and verbs (rhemata). The argument also foreshadows modern debates in philosophy of language about whether reference is a property of individual words or only of complete sentences. Hermogenes's appeal to cross-cultural variation in naming echoes empirical arguments still made in linguistics today.

narrative: "But would you say, Hermogenes, that the things differ as the names differ? and are they relative to individuals, as Protagoras tells us? For he says that man is the measure of all things, and that things are to me as they appear to me, and that they are to you as they appear to you. Do you agree with him, or would you say that things have a permanent essence of their own?"
refashioned: "But tell me, Hermogenes -- would you go further and say that things themselves differ just as names differ? That reality is relative to the individual, as Protagoras claims? He says that man is the measure of all things -- that things are to me as they appear to me, and to you as they appear to you. Do you agree with that, or would you say that things have a fixed and permanent nature of their own?"
elegant: "If names are relative to individuals, are things themselves also relative -- as Protagoras claims? Or do things have a permanent essence independent of how they appear to us?"
summary: Socrates asks whether Hermogenes's conventionalism about names implies a broader relativism about reality itself, connecting the position to Protagoras's famous doctrine that 'man is the measure of all things.'
distilled: Socrates presses Hermogenes on whether his conventionalism about names commits him to Protagoras's relativism about reality itself.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This is a pivotal move: Socrates links linguistic conventionalism to Protagorean relativism, suggesting they stand or fall together. Protagoras's 'man is the measure' doctrine (homo mensura) is quoted and extensively critiqued in the Theaetetus (152a-172b), where Socrates shows it leads to self-refutation -- if all opinions are equally true, then the opinion that Protagoras is wrong is also true. The same refutational strategy is deployed here in compressed form. The question about whether things have a 'permanent essence' (ousia) points toward the theory of Forms that underlies much of Plato's metaphysics (see Republic V-VII, Phaedo 65d-67b). By framing the debate this way, Socrates transforms a question about language into a question about the nature of reality.

narrative:
"There have been times, Socrates, when I have been driven in my perplexity to take refuge with Protagoras; not that I agree with him at all."

"What! have you ever been driven to admit that there was no such thing as a bad man?"

"No, indeed; but I have often had reason to think that there are very bad men, and a good many of them."

"Well, and have you ever found any very good ones?"

"Not many."

"Still you have found them?"

"Yes."

"And would you hold that the very good were the very wise, and the very evil very foolish? Would that be your view?"

"It would."

"But if Protagoras is right, and the truth is that things are as they appear to any one, how can some of us be wise and some of us foolish?"

"Impossible."

"And if, on the other hand, wisdom and folly are really distinguishable, you will allow, I think, that the assertion of Protagoras can hardly be correct. For if what appears to each man is true to him, one man cannot in reality be wiser than another."

"He cannot."

"Nor will you be disposed to say with Euthydemus, that all things equally belong to all men at the same moment and always; for neither on his view can there be some good and others bad, if virtue and vice are always equally to be attributed to all."

"There cannot."

"But if neither is right, and things are not relative to individuals, and all things do not equally belong to all at the same moment and always, they must be supposed to have their own proper and permanent essence: they are not in relation to us, or influenced by us, fluctuating according to our fancy, but they are independent, and maintain to their own essence the relation prescribed by nature."

"I think, Socrates, that you have said the truth."

"Does what I am saying apply only to the things themselves, or equally to the actions which proceed from them? Are not actions also a class of being?"

"Yes, the actions are real as well as the things."
refashioned:
"I will admit, Socrates, that there have been times when, in my confusion, I have been tempted to fall back on Protagoras's position -- though I do not actually agree with him."

"Really! Have you ever been driven to accept that there is no such thing as a bad person?"

"Certainly not. In fact, I have often had reason to believe there are very bad people -- quite a lot of them."

"And have you ever encountered any truly good ones?"

"Not many."

"But some?"

"Yes."

"And would you say that the truly good are the truly wise, and the truly evil are the truly foolish?"

"I would."

"But if Protagoras is right -- if things really are as they appear to each person -- then how can some people be wise and others foolish?"

"That would be impossible."

"Exactly. And if wisdom and foolishness are genuinely distinct, then Protagoras's claim can hardly be correct. For if whatever appears true to each person is true for that person, then no one can actually be wiser than anyone else."

"No, he cannot."

"Nor, I imagine, would you side with Euthydemus, who claims that all qualities belong equally to all people at all times. On his view too, there could be no distinction between good people and bad, since virtue and vice would be equally attributable to everyone."

"No, that cannot be right."

"Then if neither Protagoras nor Euthydemus is correct -- if things are not relative to individuals, and all qualities do not belong equally to everyone at all times -- then things must have their own fixed and permanent nature. They do not depend on us or shift according to our opinions. They are independent and maintain the character that nature has given them."

"I think you have spoken the truth, Socrates."

"And does this principle apply only to things themselves, or equally to the actions that arise from them? Are not actions also a kind of reality?"

"Yes, actions are real just as things are."
elegant: Hermogenes admits he has been tempted by Protagoras's relativism but does not truly accept it. Socrates presses the point: if everything is as it appears to each person, no one can be wiser or more foolish than another -- yet Hermogenes clearly believes some people are good and wise while others are bad and foolish. The same objection applies to Euthydemus's claim that all qualities belong equally to everyone. If both views fail, then things must have a fixed, independent nature -- not shifting with our opinions but maintaining the character prescribed by nature. Hermogenes agrees, and accepts that this applies to actions as well as to things.
summary: Socrates refutes Protagorean relativism and Euthydemus's universal egalitarianism by showing they eliminate the distinction between wisdom and foolishness, good and evil -- distinctions Hermogenes himself accepts. He concludes that things must have a permanent, independent essence, and extends this principle from things to actions.
distilled: Socrates argues that since we can distinguish wise from foolish and good from evil, things must have fixed natures independent of individual perception -- and this applies to actions as well.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. -- This passage executes the same refutation of Protagoras found at greater length in the Theaetetus (161c-172b), where the 'man is the measure' doctrine is shown to undermine itself: if all beliefs are true, then the belief that Protagoras is wrong is also true. The reference to Euthydemus invokes the eristic sophist of Plato's dialogue by that name (Euthydemus 293b-294a), who argued that all things belong to all people equally -- a position Socrates treats as self-evidently absurd. The conclusion that things have a 'proper and permanent essence' (ousia) independent of human perception is a foundational Platonic thesis that undergirds the theory of Forms (Republic 476a-480a, Phaedo 78c-79d). The extension from things to actions is the crucial bridge to the next argument: if actions have fixed natures, then speech (as an action) must also, and therefore naming (as part of speech) cannot be arbitrary.

narrative: "Then the actions also are done according to their proper nature, and not according to our opinion of them? In cutting, for example, we do not cut as we please, and with any chance instrument; but we cut with the proper instrument only, and according to the natural process of cutting; and the natural process is right and will succeed, but any other will fail and be of no use at all."
refashioned: "Then actions too must be performed according to their own proper nature, not according to whatever we happen to think. Take cutting, for example: we do not cut however we please, with whatever random tool is at hand. We cut with the right instrument, following the natural process of cutting. The natural way succeeds; any other way fails and accomplishes nothing."
elegant: "Actions are performed according to their own nature, not our whims. We cut with the proper instrument and the natural method of cutting -- the natural way succeeds, while any other fails."
summary: Socrates extends the argument about fixed natures to actions: just as things have permanent essences, actions have a proper nature. Cutting, for instance, requires the right instrument and the right method -- you cannot cut however you please and expect success.
distilled: Socrates argues that actions like cutting have a natural, proper way of being done and require the right instruments -- they cannot be performed arbitrarily.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage introduces the craft analogy (techne analogy) that will dominate the rest of Part 1. Socrates reasons from the objectivity of physical actions to the objectivity of naming: just as cutting has a right way and a wrong way, so does the act of naming. The principle that each action has a natural process and requires a proper instrument is central to Plato's broader philosophy of craftsmanship, developed extensively in the Republic (especially the allegory of the allegory of specialization at 370a-c) and the Gorgias (463a-465d, where Socrates distinguishes true arts from mere knacks). The cutting example is chosen for its simplicity and intuitive force -- no one would deny that you need a knife to cut, not a pillow.

narrative:
"I should say that the natural way is the right way."

"Again, in burning, not every way is the right way; but the right way is the natural way, and the right instrument the natural instrument."

"True."

"And this holds good of all actions?"

"Yes."

"And speech is a kind of action?"

"True."

"And will a man speak correctly who speaks as he pleases? Will not the successful speaker rather be he who speaks in the natural way of speaking, and as things ought to be spoken, and with the natural instrument? Any other mode of speaking will result in error and failure."

"I quite agree with you."

"And is not naming a part of speaking? for in giving names men speak."

"That is true."

"And if speaking is a sort of action and has a relation to acts, is not naming also a sort of action?"

"True."

"And we saw that actions were not relative to ourselves, but had a special nature of their own?"

"Precisely."

"Then the argument would lead us to infer that names ought to be given according to a natural process, and with a proper instrument, and not at our pleasure: in this and no other way shall we name with success."

"I agree."

"But again, that which has to be cut has to be cut with something?"

"Yes."

"And that which has to be woven or pierced has to be woven or pierced with something?"

"Certainly."

"And that which has to be named has to be named with something?"

"True."

"What is that with which we pierce?"

"An awl."

"And with which we weave?"

"A shuttle."

"And with which we name?"

"A name."

"Very good: then a name is an instrument?"

"Certainly."

"Suppose that I ask, 'What sort of instrument is a shuttle?' And you answer, 'A weaving instrument.'"

"Well."

"And I ask again, 'What do we do when we weave?' -- The answer is, that we separate or disengage the warp from the woof."

"Very true."

"And may not a similar description be given of an awl, and of instruments in general?"

"To be sure."

"And now suppose that I ask a similar question about names: will you answer me? Regarding the name as an instrument, what do we do when we name?"

"I cannot say."

"Do we not give information to one another, and distinguish things according to their natures?"

"Certainly we do."
refashioned:
"I would say the natural way is the right way."

"And the same goes for burning: not every method is correct, but the right method is the natural one, using the natural instrument."

"True."

"And does this principle hold for all actions?"

"Yes."

"And speech is a kind of action?"

"It is."

"Then will someone speak correctly just by speaking however he pleases? Or will the successful speaker be the one who speaks in the natural way, saying things as they ought to be said, using the natural instrument? Any other way of speaking will lead to error and failure."

"I completely agree."

"And is not naming a part of speaking? When we give names, we speak."

"That is true."

"And if speaking is an action, and naming is part of speaking, then naming too is an action?"

"Yes."

"And we established that actions are not relative to us but have their own fixed nature?"

"Exactly."

"Then our argument leads us to conclude that names should be given according to a natural process, with a proper instrument, and not simply as we please. Only in this way will we name successfully."

"I agree."

"Now, whatever needs to be cut must be cut with something?"

"Yes."

"And whatever needs to be woven or pierced must be woven or pierced with something?"

"Of course."

"And whatever needs to be named must be named with something?"

"True."

"What is the tool we use to pierce?"

"An awl."

"And to weave?"

"A shuttle."

"And to name?"

"A name."

"Excellent -- so a name is a tool?"

"It is."

"Now suppose I ask, 'What kind of tool is a shuttle?' and you answer, 'A weaving tool.'"

"All right."

"And if I ask, 'What do we accomplish when we weave?' the answer is that we separate and disentangle the warp threads from the woof."

"Exactly."

"And a similar functional description could be given of an awl, or any tool?"

"Certainly."

"Then let me ask the same question about names. If a name is a tool, what do we accomplish when we name?"

"I am not sure."

"Do we not communicate information to one another and distinguish things according to their natures?"

"Yes, that is exactly what we do."
elegant: The natural way of doing anything is the right way -- this holds for burning as for cutting, and for all actions without exception. Speech is an action, and naming is part of speech. Therefore naming too has a natural process and requires a proper instrument, and cannot be done however one pleases. Just as we cut with a knife, weave with a shuttle, and pierce with an awl, we name with a name. A name, then, is a tool -- an instrument in its own right. And every tool has a specific function: the shuttle separates warp from woof, the awl pierces leather. What, then, is the function of the name? It communicates information and distinguishes things according to their natures. Naming is not arbitrary self-expression but a skilled action with a definite purpose.
summary: Socrates builds a chain of reasoning: all actions have a natural method; speech is an action; naming is part of speech; therefore naming has a natural method. He then establishes that a name is an instrument, analogous to a shuttle or an awl. The function of this instrument is to communicate information and distinguish things by their natures.
distilled: Socrates argues that since naming is an action, it requires a natural process and proper instrument -- the name itself -- whose function is to communicate and distinguish things according to their natures.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. -- This is the core of Socrates's positive argument in Part 1. The chain of reasoning -- actions have natures, speech is an action, naming is part of speech, therefore naming has a nature -- is a classic Socratic syllogism. The analogy between names and physical tools (shuttle, awl) is developed with characteristic Platonic thoroughness. The conclusion that a name is an 'instrument of teaching and distinguishing natures' (developed further in 1.10) anticipates modern semantic theories about the informational function of language. The shuttle analogy is especially rich because Socrates will later use it to develop the idea of an 'ideal form' of the name (1.10-1.12), connecting language philosophy to the theory of Forms. Compare the Phaedrus (266b) where dialectic is described as the art of 'carving at the joints' -- distinguishing things according to their natures.

narrative:
"Then a name is an instrument of teaching and of distinguishing natures, as the shuttle is of distinguishing the threads of the web."

"Yes."

"And the shuttle is the instrument of the weaver?"

"Assuredly."

"Then the weaver will use the shuttle well -- and well means like a weaver? and the teacher will use the name well -- and well means like a teacher?"

"Yes."

"And when the weaver uses the shuttle, whose work will he be using well?"

"That of the carpenter."

"And is every man a carpenter, or the skilled only?"

"Only the skilled."

"And when the piercer uses the awl, whose work will he be using well?"

"That of the smith."

"And is every man a smith, or only the skilled?"

"The skilled only."

"And when the teacher uses the name, whose work will he be using?"

"There again I am puzzled."

"Cannot you at least say who gives us the names which we use?"

"Indeed I cannot."

"Does not the law seem to you to give us them?"

"Yes, I suppose so."

"Then the teacher, when he gives us a name, uses the work of the legislator?"

"I agree."

"And is every man a legislator, or the skilled only?"

"The skilled only."

"Then, Hermogenes, not every man is able to give a name, but only a maker of names; and this is the legislator, who of all skilled artisans in the world is the rarest."

"True."

"And how does the legislator make names? and to what does he look? Consider this in the light of the previous instances: to what does the carpenter look in making the shuttle? Does he not look to that which is naturally fitted to act as a shuttle?"

"Certainly."

"And suppose the shuttle to be broken in making, will he make another, looking to the broken one? or will he look to the form according to which he made the other?"

"To the latter, I should imagine."

"Might not that be justly called the true or ideal shuttle?"

"I think so."

"And whatever shuttles are wanted, for the manufacture of garments, thin or thick, of flaxen, woollen, or other material, ought all of them to have the true form of the shuttle; and whatever is the shuttle best adapted to each kind of work, that ought to be the form which the maker produces in each case."

"Yes."
refashioned:
"So a name is an instrument for teaching and for distinguishing natures, just as a shuttle is an instrument for separating threads in weaving."

"Yes."

"And the shuttle is the tool of the weaver?"

"Of course."

"Then the weaver uses the shuttle well -- meaning he uses it as a skilled weaver would? And the teacher uses the name well -- meaning he uses it as a skilled teacher would?"

"Yes."

"Now, when the weaver uses the shuttle, whose craftsmanship is he putting to good use?"

"The carpenter's."

"And is every person a carpenter, or only those with the proper skill?"

"Only the skilled."

"And when someone uses an awl for piercing, whose work is he relying on?"

"The smith's."

"And is everyone a smith, or only those with skill?"

"Only those with skill."

"Then when the teacher uses a name, whose craftsmanship is he relying on?"

"Now I am stuck."

"Can you at least say who provides us with the names we use?"

"I really cannot."

"Does it not seem to you that the law gives us our names?"

"I suppose so."

"Then the teacher, in using names, is using the work of the lawgiver?"

"I agree."

"And is every person a lawgiver, or only those with the requisite skill?"

"Only the skilled."

"Then, Hermogenes, not just anyone can give a name. Only a maker of names can do that -- and that maker is the lawgiver, the rarest of all skilled craftsmen."

"True."

"And how does the lawgiver make names? What does he look to? Think of our earlier examples: what does the carpenter look to when making a shuttle? Does he not look to what is naturally suited to function as a shuttle?"

"Certainly."

"And if a shuttle breaks during its making, will he fashion the next one by looking at the broken shuttle? Or will he look to the ideal form according to which he made the first?"

"The ideal form, I would think."

"And would it not be fair to call that the true or ideal shuttle?"

"I think so."

"And whatever shuttles are needed -- for thin cloth or thick, for linen, wool, or any other material -- all of them should embody the true form of the shuttle, while also being specifically adapted to each type of work?"

"Yes."
elegant: A name is an instrument of teaching and distinguishing natures, as a shuttle separates threads in weaving. But every tool is made by a specialist: the carpenter makes the shuttle, the smith makes the awl. Who, then, makes names? The weaver uses the carpenter's shuttle; the teacher uses the lawgiver's name. Just as not everyone can make shuttles -- only a skilled carpenter -- not everyone can make names. Only a lawgiver possesses that rare skill, and he is the rarest of all craftsmen. And what does the lawgiver look to when crafting a name? The same thing the carpenter looks to when crafting a shuttle: not a broken particular, but the ideal form naturally suited to the function. If a shuttle breaks mid-construction, the carpenter does not copy the broken one but returns to the original pattern. Whatever material the shuttle is made from, it must embody the true form of a shuttle, adapted to the specific kind of work it will perform.
summary: Socrates establishes that names are tools crafted by a specialized maker -- the lawgiver -- just as shuttles are crafted by carpenters. The lawgiver, like any craftsman, must look not to imperfect particulars but to the ideal form of the name, naturally suited to its function of teaching and distinguishing natures.
distilled: Names are instruments crafted by a rare skilled artisan -- the lawgiver -- who must look to the ideal form of the name, just as a carpenter looks to the ideal form of the shuttle.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Hermogenes. -- This passage introduces the 'name-maker' or 'legislator' (nomothetēs) -- the skilled craftsman of language -- and connects name-making to the theory of Forms. The carpenter does not look to a broken shuttle but to the 'true or ideal shuttle' (auto ho estin kerkis) -- the Form of the shuttle. By analogy, the name-maker looks to the ideal name, the Form of the name. This is one of the most explicit applications of the theory of Forms to a practical domain in all of Plato's dialogues. The division of labor (carpenter makes the shuttle, weaver uses it; legislator makes the name, teacher uses it) parallels the Republic's principle of specialization (369b-370c). The idea that law (nomos) provides names connects to the Greek root of 'convention' -- nomos itself means both 'law' and 'custom,' making the argument cleverly self-referential.

narrative:
"And the same holds of other instruments: when a man has discovered the instrument which is naturally adapted to each work, he must express this natural form, and not others which he fancies, in the material, whatever it may be, which he employs; for example, he ought to know how to put into iron the forms of awls adapted by nature to their several uses?"

"Certainly."

"And how to put into wood forms of shuttles adapted by nature to their uses?"

"True."

"For the several forms of shuttles naturally answer to the several kinds of webs; and this is true of instruments in general."

"Yes."
refashioned:
"The same principle applies to all instruments. Once a craftsman has discovered the form that is naturally suited to a given task, he must embody that natural form -- not some arbitrary design of his own invention -- in whatever material he works with. For instance, he must know how to put into iron the forms of awls that nature has adapted to their various uses."

"Certainly."

"And how to put into wood the forms of shuttles that nature has adapted to theirs?"

"True."

"Because the different forms of shuttles naturally correspond to the different kinds of fabric to be woven -- and this holds for instruments in general."

"Yes."
elegant: Every craftsman must embody the natural form suited to a task in his chosen material -- putting into iron the awl's form, into wood the shuttle's form. Different shuttle forms correspond to different kinds of weaving. The craftsman's job is to express nature's design, not his own whim.
summary: Socrates generalizes the principle: every craftsman must express the natural form of an instrument in his material, not invent arbitrary designs. Different tools suit different tasks, and the form must match the function.
distilled: A craftsman must embody the natural form suited to each task in his material, not impose arbitrary designs of his own.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage reinforces the Form-to-material relationship central to Plato's metaphysics: the ideal form is prior to and determinative of the physical instantiation. The craftsman is an intermediary who 'expresses' (apodounai) the Form in matter, much as the Demiurge in the Timaeus (28a-29a) looks to the eternal Forms when fashioning the physical world. The principle that different forms of the same tool suit different tasks is significant -- it allows for variation in names (different languages use different sounds) while maintaining that all correct names express the same underlying form. This will become crucial in 1.12 when Socrates argues that different legislators may use different syllables but still produce equally correct names.

narrative: "Then, as to names: ought not our legislator also to know how to put the true natural name of each thing into sounds and syllables, and to make and give all names with a view to the ideal name, if he is to be a namer in any true sense? And we must remember that different legislators will not use the same syllables. For neither does every smith, although he may be making the same instrument for the same purpose, make them all of the same iron. The form must be the same, but the material may vary, and still the instrument may be equally good of whatever iron made, whether in Hellas or in a foreign country; -- there is no difference."
refashioned: "Then the same must apply to names. The lawgiver must know how to express the true natural name of each thing in sounds and syllables, crafting and assigning all names with an eye to the ideal name -- if he is to deserve being called a name-maker at all. And we should keep in mind that different lawgivers will not necessarily use the same syllables. After all, not every smith uses the same iron, even when making the same tool for the same purpose. The form must be the same, but the material can vary -- and the instrument will be equally good regardless of what iron it is made from, whether it is forged in Greece or in a foreign country. It makes no difference."
elegant: The lawgiver must know how to express the natural name of each thing in sounds and syllables, always looking to the ideal name. Different lawgivers may use different syllables -- just as different smiths use different iron for the same tool. The form must be the same, but the material may vary without affecting quality.
summary: Socrates applies the craft analogy directly to naming: the lawgiver must express the ideal name in sounds and syllables, just as a smith expresses the ideal tool-form in iron. Different lawgivers may use different syllables, just as smiths may use different iron, but the names are equally valid so long as the form is preserved.
distilled: The lawgiver must express the ideal name in sounds and syllables; different languages may use different sounds, but the names are equally correct if they preserve the same form.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This is arguably the most philosophically sophisticated passage in Part 1. Socrates draws a crucial distinction between the form of a name (its meaning or natural fitness) and its material (the particular sounds and syllables). This allows him to account for the obvious fact that different languages use completely different words for the same things -- a fact Hermogenes had cited in support of conventionalism (1.5). Socrates's answer is that linguistic variation is like variation in raw material: the underlying form (and thus the correctness) can be the same even when the sounds differ. This anticipates the modern linguistic distinction between sense (meaning) and sound (phonological form), and resonates with the Saussurean concept of the signified vs. the signifier -- though Socrates would reject Saussure's claim that the relationship between them is arbitrary.

narrative:
"Very true."

"And the legislator, whether he be Hellene or barbarian, is not therefore to be deemed by you a worse legislator, provided he gives the true and proper form of the name in whatever syllables; this or that country makes no matter."

"Quite true."

"But who then is to determine whether the proper form is given to the shuttle, whatever sort of wood may be used? the carpenter who makes, or the weaver who is to use them?"

"I should say, he who is to use them, Socrates."

"And who uses the work of the lyre-maker? Will not he be the man who knows how to direct what is being done, and who will know also whether the work is being well done or not?"

"Certainly."

"And who is he?"

"The player of the lyre."

"And who will direct the shipwright?"

"The pilot."

"And who will be best able to direct the legislator in his work, and will know whether the work is well done, in this or any other country? Will not the user be the man?"

"Yes."

"And this is he who knows how to ask questions?"

"Yes."

"And how to answer them?"

"Yes."

"And him who knows how to ask and answer you would call a dialectician?"

"Yes; that would be his name."

"Then the work of the carpenter is to make a rudder, and the pilot has to direct him, if the rudder is to be well made."

"True."

"And the work of the legislator is to give names, and the dialectician must be his director if the names are to be rightly given?"

"That is true."

"Then, Hermogenes, I should say that this giving of names can be no such light matter as you fancy, or the work of light or chance persons; and Cratylus is right in saying that things have names by nature, and that not every man is an artificer of names, but he only who looks to the name which each thing by nature has, and is able to express the true forms of things in letters and syllables."

"I cannot answer you, Socrates; but I find a difficulty in changing my opinion all in a moment, and I think that I should be more readily persuaded, if you would show me what this is which you term the natural fitness of names."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"So a lawgiver, whether Greek or foreign, should not be considered inferior simply because he uses different syllables. What matters is that he gives the name its true and proper form. The country of origin makes no difference."

"Quite true."

"But who determines whether the proper form has been given to a shuttle, regardless of what wood is used -- the carpenter who makes it, or the weaver who will use it?"

"The one who uses it, I would say, Socrates."

"And who judges the work of the lyre-maker? Who knows how to direct the construction and tell whether it has been done well?"

"Certainly."

"And who is that person?"

"The lyre player."

"And who directs the shipwright?"

"The pilot."

"Then who is best qualified to direct the lawgiver and judge whether his work is well done, in any country? Will it not be the person who uses names?"

"Yes."

"And this is the person who knows how to ask the right questions?"

"Yes."

"And how to answer them?"

"Yes."

"And the person who knows how to ask and answer properly -- you would call him a dialectician?"

"Yes, that is the right name."

"So the carpenter's job is to make a rudder, and the pilot must direct him if the rudder is to be well made."

"True."

"And the lawgiver's job is to make names, and the dialectician must direct him if the names are to be rightly made?"

"That is true."

"Then, Hermogenes, the giving of names is no trivial matter -- not something for amateurs or people acting on a whim. Cratylus is right that things have names by nature, and that not just anyone can craft names. Only the person who looks to the name that each thing naturally has, and who can express the true form of things in letters and syllables, deserves to be called a name-maker."

"I cannot refute you, Socrates. But I find it hard to change my mind so suddenly. I think I would be more easily persuaded if you could show me what you mean by this 'natural fitness' of names."
elegant: Who judges the quality of a tool -- the maker or the user? The weaver judges the shuttle, the lyre player judges the lyre, the pilot judges the rudder. Likewise, the dialectician -- the one who knows how to ask and answer questions -- judges and directs the lawgiver's names. Naming is therefore no trivial or arbitrary activity. Cratylus is right: things have names by nature, and only the person who can perceive and express a thing's natural name in letters and syllables is a true name-maker. Hermogenes admits he cannot refute this but asks for a demonstration of what 'natural fitness' actually looks like.
summary: Socrates introduces the principle that the user of a tool is its ultimate judge -- the weaver judges the shuttle, the pilot the rudder. By analogy, the dialectician (the skilled asker and answerer of questions) judges and directs the lawgiver's work of naming. Socrates concludes that Cratylus is right: naming is not arbitrary but requires looking to the natural form of things. Hermogenes is not yet fully persuaded but asks for concrete examples.
distilled: The dialectician -- master of questioning and answering -- directs the lawgiver in crafting names, just as the pilot directs the shipwright; naming is a skilled art, not an arbitrary act.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. -- This passage crowns the argument of Part 1 by placing the dialectician at the top of the craft hierarchy. The maker-user distinction is fundamental to Plato: in the Republic (601c-602a), Socrates similarly distinguishes three levels of knowledge about a thing -- the user, the maker, and the imitator -- with the user possessing the highest knowledge. The dialectician, who asks and answers questions to discover truth, is Plato's philosopher par excellence (see Republic 531d-534e, Sophist 253d). By making the dialectician the judge of names, Socrates subordinates language to philosophy -- words are correct insofar as they serve the philosopher's project of distinguishing natures. Hermogenes's request for concrete examples of 'natural fitness' sets up Part 2's extensive etymological analyses. His response is psychologically realistic: intellectual conviction often lags behind logical demonstration.

narrative:
"My good Hermogenes, I have none to show. Was I not telling you just now (but you have forgotten), that I knew nothing, and proposing to share the enquiry with you? But now that you and I have talked over the matter, a step has been gained; for we have discovered that names have by nature a truth, and that not every man knows how to give a thing a name."

"Very good."

"And what is the nature of this truth or correctness of names? That, if you care to know, is the next question."

"Certainly, I care to know."

"Then reflect."

"How shall I reflect?"
refashioned:
"My dear Hermogenes, I have no concrete examples to show you. Was I not telling you just a moment ago -- though you seem to have forgotten -- that I know nothing about this, and was merely proposing that we investigate it together? But even so, our conversation has made real progress. We have established two things: that names have a natural truth or correctness to them, and that not just anyone has the ability to give a thing its proper name."

"Very good."

"So the next question is: what is the nature of this truth or correctness in names? That is, if you want to pursue the matter further."

"Of course I do."

"Then let us reflect on it."

"But how should I go about reflecting on it?"
elegant: Socrates reminds Hermogenes that he claimed no expertise from the start. Still, their dialogue has yielded two results: names have a natural correctness, and not everyone knows how to assign them. The next question is what this natural correctness consists of -- and Hermogenes is eager to pursue it.
summary: Socrates modestly reminds Hermogenes he never claimed expertise, but notes their conversation has established two key conclusions: names have natural correctness, and name-giving requires special skill. He frames the next question -- what is this natural correctness? -- and Hermogenes eagerly agrees to continue the investigation.
distilled: Socrates summarizes Part 1's conclusions -- names have natural correctness and require skilled makers -- and sets up the next inquiry into what this correctness actually consists of.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Hermogenes. -- This transitional passage recapitulates the findings of Part 1 and sets up Part 2's etymological investigations. Socrates's renewed profession of ignorance echoes the Apology (21d), where he claims his only wisdom is knowing that he does not know -- yet through dialectical questioning he has led Hermogenes to significant philosophical conclusions. The two results -- names have natural truth, and not everyone can assign them -- are the positive contributions of the convention-vs-nature debate. The question 'How shall I reflect?' from Hermogenes is the perfect bridge to Part 2, where Socrates will pursue the nature of correctness through extensive etymological analysis of Greek names, beginning with the names used by gods and heroes in Homer.

narrative: "The true way is to have the assistance of those who know, and you must pay them well both in money and in thanks; these are the Sophists, of whom your brother, Callias, has -- rather dearly -- bought the reputation of wisdom. But you have not yet come into your inheritance, and therefore you had better go to him, and beg and entreat him to tell you what he has learnt from Protagoras about the fitness of names."
refashioned: The proper approach is to consult experts and compensate them generously, both financially and with gratitude. These experts are the Sophists, and your brother Callias has purchased a reputation for wisdom from them -- at quite a steep price, I might add. Since you haven't yet received your inheritance, your best course would be to go to Callias and plead with him to share whatever Protagoras taught him about how names naturally fit the things they describe.
elegant: If you want to understand the natural fitness of names, consult those who claim expertise -- the Sophists. Your brother Callias paid dearly for such wisdom from Protagoras. Go beg him to share it.
summary: Socrates suggests that Hermogenes consult the Sophists, particularly through his brother Callias who studied with Protagoras, to learn about the natural fitness of names.
distilled: Socrates ironically recommends consulting the Sophists -- especially via Hermogenes' brother Callias, who paid handsomely for Protagoras' teachings on names.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. This passage transitions from Part 1's theoretical framework to the etymological investigation. The mention of Callias and Protagoras situates the dialogue socially -- Callias was famously wealthy and a patron of Sophists. The ironic tone toward Sophistic wisdom anticipates Socrates' own playful etymologizing. Cross-references: Protagoras (on the Sophist's teachings), Theaetetus (on Protagoras' relativism).

narrative:
"But how inconsistent should I be, if, whilst repudiating Protagoras and his truth" -- "Truth" was the title of the book of Protagoras; compare Theaetetus -- "I were to attach any value to what he and his book affirm!"

"Then if you despise him, you must learn of Homer and the poets."

"And where does Homer say anything about names, and what does he say?"

"He often speaks of them; notably and nobly in the places where he distinguishes the different names which Gods and men give to the same things. Does he not in these passages make a remarkable statement about the correctness of names? For the Gods must clearly be supposed to call things by their right and natural names; do you not think so?"

"Why, of course they call them rightly, if they call them at all. But to what are you referring?"

"Do you not know what he says about the river in Troy who had a single combat with Hephaestus?"

"I remember."

"Well, and about this river -- to know that he ought to be called Xanthus and not Scamander -- is not that a solemn lesson? Or about the bird which, as he says --"

"I do."

"Let me ask you, then, which did Homer think the more correct of the names given to Hector's son -- Astyanax or Scamandrius?"

"I do not know."

"How would you answer, if you were asked whether the wise or the unwise are more likely to give correct names?"

"I should say the wise, of course."

"And are the men or the women of a city, taken as a class, the wiser?"

"I should say, the men."

"And Homer, as you know, says that the Trojan men called him Astyanax (king of the city); but if the men called him Astyanax, the other name of Scamandrius could only have been given to him by the women."

"That may be inferred."

"And must not Homer have imagined the Trojans to be wiser than their wives?"

"To be sure."

"Then he must have thought Astyanax to be a more correct name for the boy than Scamandrius?"

"Clearly."

"And what is the reason of this? Let us consider: does he not himself suggest a very good reason, when he says --"

"I see."

"Why, Hermogenes, I do not as yet see myself; and do you?"

"No, indeed; not I."

"But tell me, friend, did not Homer himself also give Hector his name?"
refashioned:
Hermogenes objects: "How inconsistent would I be if, after rejecting Protagoras and his doctrine of 'Truth' -- which was the actual title of his book -- I then turned around and valued what he teaches!"

Socrates replies: "If you reject him, then you must turn to Homer and the poets instead."

"But where does Homer discuss names?"

"He does so frequently, and with great distinction, especially where he contrasts the names that gods and mortals give to the same things. Don't these passages make a striking claim about the correctness of names? After all, the gods surely call things by their true and natural names."

"Of course they do, if they name things at all. But what specifically are you referring to?"

"Don't you recall what Homer says about the Trojan river that fought against Hephaestus? The lesson is that it should properly be called Xanthus, not Scamander -- the divine name versus the human one. Now tell me: which name for Hector's son did Homer consider more correct -- Astyanax or Scamandrius?"

Hermogenes doesn't know, so Socrates leads him step by step. Who gives more correct names -- the wise or the foolish? The wise, obviously. And which group in a city is generally wiser -- men or women? The men. Homer tells us the Trojan men called the boy Astyanax, meaning "king of the city," while the name Scamandrius must have come from the women. Since Homer regarded the men as wiser, he must have considered Astyanax the more correct name.

"But what is the reason for this? Homer himself seems to suggest one, though I confess I don't yet see it clearly myself. Tell me though -- didn't Homer also give Hector his name?"
elegant: Hermogenes rejects learning from Protagoras as inconsistent. Socrates redirects him to Homer, who distinguishes divine from human names -- gods naming the Trojan river Xanthus while men call it Scamander. Homer considered Astyanax ("king of the city") the more correct name for Hector's son because the wiser Trojan men used it. Socrates then asks: did Homer himself name Hector?
summary: Socrates turns to Homer as an authority on names, showing that the poet distinguished between divine and human names and implied that wiser namers give more correct names. The example of Hector's son being called Astyanax by the men and Scamandrius by the women illustrates this principle.
distilled: Homer's distinction between divine and human names -- and his preference for the men's name "Astyanax" over the women's "Scamandrius" -- suggests that wiser namers give truer names.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. This passage introduces Homer as an etymological authority, a move that grounds the investigation in poetic tradition. The divine/human name pairs (Xanthus/Scamander) come from Iliad 20.74. Astyanax ("lord of the city") vs. Scamandrius comes from Iliad 6.402-3. The assumption that men are wiser than women reflects conventional Greek attitudes. Cross-references: Theaetetus (on Protagoras' "Truth"), Republic (on Homer's authority).

narrative:
"What of that?"

"The name appears to me to be very nearly the same as the name of Astyanax -- both are Hellenic; and a king (anax) and a holder (ektor) have nearly the same meaning, and are both descriptive of a king; for a man is clearly the holder of that of which he is king; he rules, and owns, and holds it. But, perhaps, you may think that I am talking nonsense; and indeed I believe that I myself did not know what I meant when I imagined that I had found some indication of the opinion of Homer about the correctness of names."

"I assure you that I think otherwise, and I believe you to be on the right track."
refashioned:
Socrates observes that the name Hector is remarkably similar in meaning to Astyanax -- both are Greek words, and "king" (anax) and "holder" (ektor) mean nearly the same thing. Both describe a ruler, since a king is clearly the holder of whatever he rules over -- he governs it, possesses it, grasps it. Socrates adds self-deprecatingly that perhaps he is talking nonsense and didn't really know what he meant when he thought he'd found evidence of Homer's views on the correctness of names. But Hermogenes disagrees and assures him he is on the right track.

The key etymological insight here is that different words with entirely different letters can carry the same essential meaning. "Hector" and "Astyanax" share almost no letters, yet both fundamentally mean "ruler" -- one as "holder" and the other as "lord of the city." This becomes Socrates' first demonstration that natural correctness in names lies in meaning, not in specific syllables.
elegant: Hector ("holder") and Astyanax ("lord of the city") share almost no letters yet mean essentially the same thing -- both describe a ruler. A king is the holder of what he rules. This is the first demonstration that natural correctness lies in meaning, not syllables.
summary: Socrates reveals that Hector and Astyanax share the same meaning despite different syllables -- both signify a ruler. This establishes the principle that a name's correctness depends on its meaning, not its specific letters.
distilled: "Hector" (holder) and "Astyanax" (king) mean the same thing, proving that names can be correct through meaning even when their letters differ entirely.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes responding. Greek words etymologized: Hektor (from echein, to hold) and Astyanax (from anax, lord/king + astu, city). This is Socrates' first formal etymology in the dialogue and establishes the crucial principle that correctness resides in semantic content rather than phonetic form. Socrates' self-deprecation ("perhaps I am talking nonsense") is characteristic irony. Cross-references: This principle of meaning over form recurs throughout the etymological section.

narrative:
"There is reason, I think, in calling the lion's whelp a lion, and the foal of a horse a horse; I am speaking only of the ordinary course of nature, when an animal produces after his kind, and not of extraordinary births; -- if contrary to nature a horse have a calf, then I should not call that a foal but a calf; nor do I call any inhuman birth a man, but only a natural birth. And the same may be said of trees and other things. Do you agree with me?"

"Yes, I agree."
refashioned:
Socrates introduces an analogy from nature. It makes sense to call a lion's cub a lion and a horse's offspring a horse -- this follows the ordinary course of nature, where like produces like. But if something unnatural occurs -- say a horse giving birth to a calf -- we wouldn't call that offspring a foal. We'd call it what it actually is: a calf. Similarly, we wouldn't call an inhuman birth a man, only a natural one. The same principle applies to trees and everything else in nature.

The point is that naming should follow the actual nature of the thing, not merely its parentage or origin. A name belongs to whatever possesses the nature that the name describes.
elegant: We rightly call a lion's cub a lion and a horse's foal a horse -- naming follows nature, not mere parentage. If a horse bore a calf, we'd call it a calf. Names belong to whatever possesses the nature the name describes.
summary: Socrates argues that naming should follow the actual nature of a thing, not its parentage. A lion's offspring is called a lion because it has a lion's nature, not simply because a lion bore it.
distilled: Names should follow the actual nature of a thing -- a lion's cub is called a lion because it has a lion's nature, not merely because of its parentage.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. No specific Greek words are etymologized here; rather, Socrates establishes a biological analogy for natural naming. The principle that names follow nature (phusis) rather than convention or lineage prepares for the etymological analyses to come. The argument that offspring should be named by their nature, not their origin, will be applied to human names -- a pious father's impious son should not bear a name meaning "beloved of god." Cross-references: This nature-over-convention theme echoes the central debate between naturalism and conventionalism in the dialogue.

narrative:
"Very good. But you had better watch me and see that I do not play tricks with you. For on the same principle the son of a king is to be called a king. And whether the syllables of the name are the same or not the same, makes no difference, provided the meaning is retained; nor does the addition or subtraction of a letter make any difference so long as the essence of the thing remains in possession of the name and appears in it."

"What do you mean?"
refashioned:
Socrates warns Hermogenes to watch carefully that he isn't being tricked. By the same natural principle, a king's son should be called a king -- and it doesn't matter whether the exact syllables are the same or different, as long as the meaning is preserved. Adding or removing a letter is irrelevant as long as the essence of the thing still resides in the name and shines through it.

This is a pivotal methodological point: Socrates is establishing that etymological analysis should focus on the essential meaning embedded in a name, not on its surface-level phonetic form. Letters and syllables are the material of names, but meaning is their substance.
elegant: A king's son is rightly called a king regardless of the syllables used. Adding or removing letters doesn't matter so long as the name's essential meaning is preserved. Meaning is the substance of names; letters are merely material.
summary: Socrates establishes that the correctness of a name depends on preserving its essential meaning, not its exact syllables. Letters may be added or removed without consequence, provided the name still captures the nature of the thing.
distilled: A name's correctness lies in preserving essential meaning, not exact syllables -- letters may change so long as the essence remains visible in the name.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. This passage establishes the key methodological principle for all the etymologies that follow: phonetic variation is acceptable if semantic content is preserved. Socrates' playful warning ("watch me and see that I do not play tricks") signals awareness that this principle could be abused -- a concern that resurfaces later when Hermogenes objects to overly free letter manipulation. Cross-references: This principle parallels the Form theory in Phaedo and Republic -- the material instantiation varies while the essence remains constant.

narrative:
"A very simple matter. I may illustrate my meaning by the names of letters, which you know are not the same as the letters themselves with the exception of the four epsilon, upsilon, omicron, omega; the names of the rest, whether vowels or consonants, are made up of other letters which we add to them; but so long as we introduce the meaning, and there can be no mistake, the name of the letter is quite correct. Take, for example, the letter beta -- the addition of eta, tau, alpha, gives no offence, and does not prevent the whole name from having the value which the legislator intended -- so well did he know how to give the letters names."

"I believe you are right."
refashioned:
Socrates illustrates his point with a simple example from the alphabet itself. Most letter-names are not identical to the sounds they represent. Only four -- epsilon, upsilon, omicron, and omega -- are named by their own sound alone. The rest have additional letters attached to form their names: the letter beta, for instance, is named "beta" by adding eta, tau, and alpha to the basic sound. Yet this addition doesn't cause any confusion or make the name incorrect. As long as the intended meaning -- the identity of that particular letter-sound -- comes through clearly, the name works perfectly.

This shows that the original name-maker (the "legislator") understood how to construct names that convey meaning even when their phonetic form includes extra material beyond the bare essence.
elegant: Most Greek letter-names include extra letters beyond the sound they represent -- beta adds eta, tau, and alpha to the basic sound. Yet the name works perfectly because the meaning comes through. This proves names can carry additional phonetic material without losing correctness.
summary: Socrates uses Greek letter-names to illustrate his principle. Most letters (like beta) have names containing additional letters beyond their core sound, yet these names remain correct because the intended meaning is clear.
distilled: The letter beta is named with extra letters (eta, tau, alpha) beyond its core sound, yet the name remains correct because its meaning is unmistakable.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. This passage uses metalinguistic analysis -- the names of letters themselves -- to demonstrate the principle of meaning over form. The four self-naming vowels (epsilon, upsilon, omicron, omega) are contrasted with letters whose names require additional phonetic material. The "legislator" (nomothetēs) is the hypothetical original name-giver discussed in Part 1. Cross-references: The concept of the legislator as name-maker was developed in 1.10-1.12.

narrative: "And may not the same be said of a king? A king will often be the son of a king, the good son or the noble son of a good or noble sire; and similarly the offspring of every kind, in the regular course of nature, is like the parent, and therefore has the same name. Yet the syllables may be disguised until they appear different to the ignorant person, and he may not recognize them, although they are the same, just as any one of us would not recognize the same drugs under different disguises of colour and smell, although to the physician, who regards the power of them, they are the same, and he is not put out by the addition; and in like manner the etymologist is not put out by the addition or transposition or subtraction of a letter or two, or indeed by the change of all the letters, for this need not interfere with the meaning. As was just now said, the names of Hector and Astyanax have only one letter alike, which is tau, and yet they have the same meaning. And how little in common with the letters of their names has Archepolis (ruler of the city) -- and yet the meaning is the same. And there are many other names which just mean 'king.' Again, there are several names for a general, as, for example, Agis (leader) and Polemarchus (chief in war) and Eupolemus (good warrior); and others which denote a physician, as Iatrocles (famous healer) and Acesimbrotus (curer of mortals); and there are many others which might be cited, differing in their syllables and letters, but having the same meaning. Would you not say so?"
refashioned:
Socrates extends the principle: just as a king's son is a king, offspring generally resemble their parents and share the same essential name. But the syllables can be so thoroughly disguised that an untrained person fails to recognize the kinship -- much like how ordinary people can't identify the same medicine when it comes in different colors and smells, while a physician, who understands the active properties, sees through the disguise immediately.

The etymologist works the same way. Transposing, adding, or removing a letter or two -- or even changing all the letters entirely -- need not obscure the meaning. Hector and Astyanax share only a single letter (tau), yet both mean "ruler." Archepolis ("ruler of the city") shares even less in its lettering, yet carries the same meaning. There are many names that simply mean "king" in different phonetic garb.

The same pattern holds across other domains: multiple names mean "general" -- Agis ("leader"), Polemarchus ("chief in war"), Eupolemus ("good warrior"). Multiple names mean "physician" -- Iatrocles ("famous healer"), Acesimbrotus ("curer of mortals"). All differ in syllables yet converge in meaning.
elegant: Like a physician seeing the same medicine in different forms, the etymologist sees the same meaning in different names. Hector, Astyanax, and Archepolis share almost no letters yet all mean "ruler." Agis, Polemarchus, and Eupolemus all mean "general." Names converge in meaning despite diverging in sound.
summary: Socrates compares the etymologist to a physician who recognizes the same drug in different forms. Names like Hector, Astyanax, and Archepolis share the meaning of "ruler" despite entirely different letters, just as Agis, Polemarchus, and Eupolemus all mean "general."
distilled: Like a physician seeing past surface differences to the same medicine, the etymologist sees that names with entirely different letters -- Hector, Astyanax, Archepolis -- can share the same essential meaning.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek names etymologized: Hektor (holder), Astyanax (lord of the city), Archepolis (ruler of the city), Agis (leader, from agein), Polemarchus (chief in war, from polemos + archos), Eupolemus (good warrior, from eu + polemos), Iatrocles (famous healer, from iatros + kleos), Acesimbrotus (curer of mortals, from akesis + brotos). The physician analogy is significant -- it positions etymology as a skilled techne requiring expertise to see through surface variation. Cross-references: The physician analogy recurs in Republic and Gorgias; the legislator-as-craftsman theme connects to 1.10-1.12.

narrative:
"Yes."

"The same names, then, ought to be assigned to those who follow in the course of nature?"

"Yes."

"And what of those who follow out of the course of nature, and are prodigies? For example, when a good and religious man has an irreligious son, he ought to bear the name not of his father, but of the class to which he belongs, just as in the case which was before supposed of a horse foaling a calf."

"Quite true."

"Then the irreligious son of a religious father should be called irreligious?"

"Certainly."

"He should not be called Theophilus (beloved of God) or Mnesitheus (mindful of God), or any of these names: if names are correctly given, his should have an opposite meaning."

"Certainly, Socrates."

"Again, Hermogenes, there is Orestes (the man of the mountains) who appears to be rightly called; whether chance gave the name, or perhaps some poet who meant to express the brutality and fierceness and mountain wildness of his hero's nature."

"That is very likely, Socrates."

"And his father's name is also according to nature."

"Clearly."
refashioned:
Socrates draws out the implication: if a name should match a person's nature, what happens when nature produces something unexpected? When a pious man has an impious son, the son should bear a name reflecting his own character, not his father's -- just as a calf born to a horse should be called a calf, not a foal.

So an irreligious son of a religious father should be called "irreligious" -- certainly not Theophilus ("beloved of God") or Mnesitheus ("mindful of God"). If names are to be correct, his name should carry the opposite meaning.

Then there is the case of Orestes, whose name means "man of the mountains." Whether it was given by chance or deliberately by some poet, it aptly captures the brutality, fierceness, and wild mountainous character of the hero who murdered his own mother. And his father Agamemnon's name likewise fits his nature -- a point Socrates will explore next.
