elegant: When nature deviates, names should follow nature, not lineage. An impious man shouldn't be called Theophilus ("beloved of God"). Orestes ("man of the mountains") aptly captures his fierce, wild nature. Names must match character, not parentage.
summary: Socrates argues that when a person's nature deviates from their family, the name should match the individual's character, not the parent's. Theophilus would be wrong for an impious man. Orestes rightly suggests mountain wildness befitting his fierce nature.
distilled: Names must match individual nature, not lineage -- an impious man should not be called Theophilus, and Orestes rightly evokes the mountain wildness of a fierce character.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek names etymologized: Theophilus (theos + philos, beloved of God), Mnesitheus (mnesis + theos, mindful of God), Orestes (from oros, mountain). The Orestes etymology connects name to character -- his matricide and subsequent madness embody a savage, untamed nature. The argument that names should match nature rather than lineage reinforces the naturalist position against conventionalism. Cross-references: Orestes appears in Aeschylus' Oresteia; the piety theme connects to Euthyphro.

narrative: "Yes, for as his name, so also is his nature; Agamemnon (admirable for remaining) is one who is patient and persevering in the accomplishment of his resolves, and by his virtue crowns them; and his continuance at Troy with all the vast army is a proof of that admirable endurance in him which is signified by the name Agamemnon. I also think that Atreus is rightly called; for his murder of Chrysippus and his exceeding cruelty to Thyestes are damaging and destructive to his reputation -- the name is a little altered and disguised so as not to be intelligible to every one, but to the etymologist there is no difficulty in seeing the meaning, for whether you think of him as ateires the stubborn, or as atrestos the fearless, or as ateros the destructive one, the name is perfectly correct in every point of view. And I think that Pelops is also named appropriately; for, as the name implies, he is rightly called Pelops who sees what is near only (o ta pelas oron)."
refashioned:
Socrates unpacks the name Agamemnon: it means something like "admirable for remaining" or "admirable in perseverance." His nature matches perfectly -- he was patient and resolute in accomplishing his goals, crowning his efforts with virtue. His ten-year siege of Troy with that vast army is living proof of the admirable endurance encoded in his name.

Atreus is equally well-named, though the etymology is slightly disguised. His murder of Chrysippus and horrifying cruelty to Thyestes -- serving him his own children at a feast -- were damaging and destructive. The name can be read several ways: as ateires ("the stubborn"), atrestos ("the fearless"), or ateros ("the destructive one"). Each reading captures something true about his character. The name has been altered enough that ordinary people miss the meaning, but the etymologist sees through the disguise easily.

Pelops, too, is aptly named. His name suggests "he who sees what is near" (ta pelas oron) -- someone with only short-range vision.
elegant: Agamemnon means "admirable for remaining" -- his decade-long siege of Troy proves his persevering nature. Atreus can be read as "stubborn," "fearless," or "destructive" -- all fitting his murderous cruelty. Pelops means "he who sees only what is near," capturing his short-sightedness.
summary: Socrates etymologizes three legendary names: Agamemnon as "admirable for remaining" (proven by his perseverance at Troy), Atreus as "stubborn/fearless/destructive" (fitting his cruelty), and Pelops as "seeing only what is near" (reflecting his lack of foresight).
distilled: Agamemnon ("admirable for persevering"), Atreus ("stubborn" or "destructive"), and Pelops ("seeing only what is near") each reveal their bearers' natures through etymology.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek names etymologized: Agamemnon (from agastos + menein, admirable for remaining), Atreus (from ateires/atrestos/ateros -- stubborn/fearless/destructive), Pelops (from pelas + ops, seeing what is near). The Atreus etymology is notable for offering three possible readings, all negative -- reflecting his role in the curse of the House of Atreus. The Pelops etymology introduces the theme of limited vision vs. foresight. Chrysippus was Pelops' illegitimate son; Thyestes was Atreus' brother. Cross-references: The House of Atreus features prominently in Greek tragedy (Aeschylus' Oresteia).

narrative:
"How so?"

"Because, according to the tradition, he had no forethought or foresight of all the evil which the murder of Myrtilus would entail upon his whole race in remote ages; he saw only what was at hand and immediate, -- or in other words, pelas (near), in his eagerness to win Hippodamia by all means for his bride. Every one would agree that the name of Tantalus is rightly given and in accordance with nature, if the traditions about him are true."

"And what are the traditions?"
refashioned:
Hermogenes asks how Pelops' name reveals short-sightedness. Socrates explains: according to tradition, Pelops had no foresight about the terrible consequences his murder of Myrtilus would bring upon his entire lineage for generations to come. He saw only what was immediate -- pelas, meaning "near" -- consumed by his eagerness to win Hippodamia as his bride at any cost. He traded long-term catastrophe for short-term gain.

Socrates then transitions to Tantalus, declaring that everyone would agree his name fits perfectly if the traditions about him are true. The name connects to talanteia -- to be weighted down or suspended -- foreshadowing the famous punishment of having a stone eternally suspended over his head. Hermogenes, intrigued, asks what these traditions are.
elegant: Pelops saw only what was near (pelas) -- he murdered Myrtilus to win Hippodamia without foreseeing the curse it would bring upon his descendants. His name captures this fatal short-sightedness. Tantalus, too, is perfectly named if tradition is to be believed.
summary: Socrates explains that Pelops' name ("seeing what is near") reflects his lack of foresight in murdering Myrtilus to win Hippodamia, blind to the generational curse this would unleash. He then introduces Tantalus as another perfectly fitted name.
distilled: Pelops murdered Myrtilus to win Hippodamia, seeing only the immediate prize (pelas, "near") and not the curse his act would bring upon generations.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Pelops (continued from 2.9 -- pelas, near + ops, seeing), with introduction to Tantalus. Myrtilus was the charioteer Pelops betrayed after winning the chariot race for Hippodamia's hand. The curse of the House of Pelops -- stemming from this betrayal -- cascades through Atreus, Agamemnon, and Orestes. The theme of foresight vs. short-sightedness has philosophical resonance -- it connects to the Socratic emphasis on examining consequences. Cross-references: Protagoras (on foresight), the House of Atreus in Aeschylus.

narrative: "Many terrible misfortunes are said to have happened to him in his life -- last of all, came the utter ruin of his country; and after his death he had the stone suspended (talanteia) over his head in the world below -- all this agrees wonderfully well with his name. You might imagine that some person who wanted to call him Talantatos (the most weighted down by misfortune), disguised the name by altering it into Tantalus; and into this form, by some accident of tradition, it has actually been transmuted. The name of Zeus, who is his alleged father, has also an excellent meaning, although hard to be understood, because really like a sentence, which is divided into two parts, for some call him Zena, and use the one half, and others who use the other half call him Dia; the two together signify the nature of the God, and the business of a name, as we were saying, is to express the nature. For there is none who is more the author of life to us and to all, than the lord and king of all. Wherefore we are right in calling him Zena and Dia, which are one name, although divided, meaning the God through whom all creatures always have life (di' on zen aei pasi tois zosin uparchei). There is an irreverence, at first sight, in calling him son of Cronos (who is a proverb for stupidity), and we might rather expect Zeus to be the child of a mighty intellect. Which is the fact; for this is the meaning of his father's name: Kronos quasi Koros (Choreo, to sweep), not in the sense of a youth, but signifying to katharon kai akheraton tou nou, the pure and garnished mind (sc. apo tou chorein). He, as we are informed by tradition, was begotten of Uranus, rightly so called (apo tou oran ta ano) from looking upwards; which, as philosophers tell us, is the way to have a pure mind, and the name Uranus is therefore correct. If I could remember the genealogy of Hesiod, I would have gone on and tried more conclusions of the same sort on the remoter ancestors of the Gods, -- then I might have seen whether this wisdom, which has come to me all in an instant, I know not whence, will or will not hold good to the end."
refashioned:
Socrates explains Tantalus: many terrible misfortunes befell him in life, culminating in his country's destruction. After death, he endured the famous punishment of a stone eternally suspended over his head in the underworld. All this fits the name wonderfully. One might imagine someone wanting to call him "Talantatos" -- "the most weighted down by misfortune" -- and disguising the name by reshaping it into Tantalus.

Then Socrates tackles a far greater name: Zeus. The name is difficult because it functions like a sentence split in two. Some Greeks call him Zena, using one half; others call him Dia, using the other. Together they reveal his nature -- for the business of a name is to express nature. Zeus is the supreme author of life for all creatures, and "Zena" combined with "Dia" means "the God through whom all living things always have life" (di' on zen aei).

But isn't it irreverent that Zeus is called son of Kronos, a name proverbially associated with stupidity? Not at all -- Kronos actually derives from koros, meaning the pure and unsullied mind (from chorein, to sweep clean). And Kronos in turn was begotten by Uranus, whose name means "looking upward" (oran ta ano) -- which philosophers say is the path to a pure mind.

Socrates wishes he could remember more of Hesiod's genealogy to test whether this sudden etymological wisdom holds up all the way through.
elegant: Tantalus means "most weighted down" -- fitting for his eternal punishment. Zeus combines Zena and Dia to mean "the God through whom all things live." Kronos means "pure mind," not stupidity. Uranus means "looking upward" -- the path to mental purity. The divine genealogy encodes a philosophical progression from contemplation to intellect to life.
summary: Socrates etymologizes Tantalus as "most weighted down by misfortune," then provides a major analysis of Zeus ("the God through whom all things live"), Kronos ("pure mind"), and Uranus ("looking upward"). The divine genealogy encodes a philosophical progression from contemplation to purity of mind to the gift of life.
distilled: Zeus means "the God through whom all things live," his father Kronos means "pure mind," and grandfather Uranus means "looking upward" -- the divine genealogy encodes a philosophy of life, intellect, and contemplation.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Tantalus (from talanteia, suspension/weighing down, or Talantatos, most burdened), Zeus (from Zena + Dia = di' on zen aei, through whom all live), Kronos (from koros/chorein, pure/sweeping, meaning pure mind), Uranus (from oran ta ano, looking upward). This is one of the most philosophically rich etymological passages. The Zeus etymology connects naming to the life-giving principle. The Kronos etymology reverses the popular association with stupidity (kronos as a term of abuse). Hesiod's Theogony provides the genealogical framework. Cross-references: Theogony (Hesiod), Euthyphro (on piety), Timaeus (on cosmic order).

narrative:
"You seem to me, Socrates, to be quite like a prophet newly inspired, and to be uttering oracles."

"Yes, Hermogenes, and I believe that I caught the inspiration from the great Euthyphro of the Prospaltian deme, who gave me a long lecture which commenced at dawn: he talked and I listened, and his wisdom and enchanting ravishment has not only filled my ears but taken possession of my soul, and to-day I shall let his superhuman power work and finish the investigation of names -- that will be the way; but to-morrow, if you are so disposed, we will conjure him away, and make a purgation of him, if we can only find some priest or sophist who is skilled in purifications of this sort."

"With all my heart; for I am very curious to hear the rest of the enquiry about names."
refashioned:
Hermogenes marvels that Socrates seems like a newly inspired prophet uttering oracles. Socrates attributes his sudden etymological brilliance to the great Euthyphro of Prospaltia, who gave him a long lecture beginning at dawn. Euthyphro's wisdom, Socrates says, has not merely filled his ears but taken possession of his soul. Today he will let this superhuman inspiration run its course and drive the investigation of names to completion.

But tomorrow, he adds with characteristic irony, they should purge themselves of this influence -- if they can find some priest or sophist skilled in such purifications. The joke is multilayered: Socrates is both crediting and mocking the source of his inspiration, suggesting that the etymological frenzy he is about to unleash is a kind of divine madness that will eventually need to be cleansed away.

Hermogenes eagerly agrees, wanting to hear the rest of the inquiry.
elegant: Hermogenes compares Socrates to an inspired prophet. Socrates credits the great Euthyphro, whose dawn lecture has possessed his soul with etymological fervor. He will ride this inspiration today but purge it tomorrow -- the etymological frenzy is a kind of divine madness.
summary: Socrates humorously attributes his etymological inspiration to Euthyphro, treating it as a kind of divine possession that will drive the investigation today but needs to be purged tomorrow.
distilled: Socrates playfully credits his etymological frenzy to Euthyphro's influence -- a divine madness he will indulge today and purge tomorrow.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates (Hermogenes speaks first). Euthyphro of Prospaltia is the same figure from Plato's Euthyphro dialogue, a self-proclaimed expert on divine matters. Socrates' attribution of his wisdom to Euthyphro is deeply ironic -- in the Euthyphro, Socrates systematically dismantles Euthyphro's claims to knowledge. The language of inspiration, possession, and purgation echoes Phaedrus on divine madness. The reference to purification connects to Apollo's role as purifier (discussed later at 2.31-2.32). Cross-references: Euthyphro, Phaedrus (on divine madness), Ion (on poetic inspiration).

narrative: "Then let us proceed; and where would you have us begin, now that we have got a sort of outline of the enquiry? Are there any names which witness of themselves that they are not given arbitrarily, but have a natural fitness? The names of heroes and of men in general are apt to be deceptive because they are often called after ancestors with whose names, as we were saying, they may have no business; or they are the expression of a wish like Eutychides (the son of good fortune), or Sosias (the Saviour), or Theophilus (the beloved of God), and others. But I think that we had better leave these, for there will be more chance of finding correctness in the names of immutable essences; -- there ought to have been more care taken about them when they were named, and perhaps there may have been some more than human power at work occasionally in giving them names."
refashioned:
Socrates proposes a strategy for the investigation. Now that they have an outline, where should they begin? Some names testify to their own natural fitness, but the names of heroes and ordinary people are unreliable evidence. These are often inherited from ancestors whose character they don't share, or they express parental wishes rather than actual nature -- names like Eutychides ("son of good fortune"), Sosias ("savior"), or Theophilus ("beloved of God").

Better to examine the names of immutable essences -- permanent concepts and divine beings. These should have been named with greater care, and perhaps some more-than-human power was at work in their naming. If natural correctness exists anywhere in language, it will be found in names for eternal, unchanging realities rather than in the arbitrary names given to individual mortals.
elegant: Personal names are unreliable -- they're inherited or wishful. Better to examine names of immutable essences, where greater care was taken and perhaps divine power was at work. If natural correctness exists in language, it will be found in names for eternal realities.
summary: Socrates proposes examining names of immutable essences rather than personal names, which are often inherited or wishful. Names for permanent realities were likely given with more care and may reflect more-than-human wisdom.
distilled: Personal names are unreliable evidence for natural correctness; names of immutable essences -- given with greater care, perhaps by divine power -- are the place to look.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek names mentioned: Eutychides (eu + tuche, good fortune), Sosias (from sozein, to save), Theophilus (theos + philos, beloved of God). This passage marks a methodological pivot -- Socrates shifts from analyzing heroic names to investigating the names of abstract essences and divine beings. The distinction between mutable individuals and immutable essences echoes the Theory of Forms. The suggestion of "more than human power" at work in naming anticipates the divine inspiration theme. Cross-references: Phaedo and Republic (on immutable Forms), Euthyphro (on divine knowledge).

narrative:
"I think so, Socrates."

"Ought we not to begin with the consideration of the Gods, and show that they are rightly named Gods?"

"Yes, that will be well."

"My notion would be something of this sort: I suspect that the sun, moon, earth, stars, and heaven, which are still the Gods of many barbarians, were the only Gods known to the aboriginal Hellenes. Seeing that they were always moving and running, from their running nature they were called Gods or runners (Theous, Theontas); and when men became acquainted with the other Gods, they proceeded to apply the same name to them all. Do you think that likely?"

"I think it very likely indeed."

"What shall follow the Gods?"

"Must not demons and heroes and men come next?"

"Demons! And what do you consider to be the meaning of this word? Tell me if my view is right."

"Let me hear."

"You know how Hesiod uses the word?"

"I do not."

"Do you not remember that he speaks of a golden race of men who came first?"

"Yes, I do."

"He says of them --"

"What is the inference?"

"What is the inference! Why, I suppose that he means by the golden men, not men literally made of gold, but good and noble; and I am convinced of this, because he further says that we are the iron race."

"That is true."

"And do you not suppose that good men of our own day would by him be said to be of golden race?"

"Very likely."

"And are not the good wise?"

"Yes, they are wise."
refashioned:
Socrates begins with the word "Gods" itself. He speculates that the earliest Greeks knew only the celestial bodies as gods -- sun, moon, earth, stars, and heaven -- which many non-Greek peoples still worship. These celestial bodies were always moving and running, and from this running nature they were called theoi (gods) or theontes (runners). When the Greeks later learned of other divine beings, they simply extended the same name to all of them.

Moving to the next category, Hermogenes suggests demons, heroes, and men. Socrates takes up "demons" first. He asks whether Hermogenes knows how Hesiod uses the word. Hesiod spoke of a golden race of men who came first -- not literally made of gold, Socrates explains, but good and noble. After all, Hesiod calls us the iron race, which is clearly a metaphor for our inferior quality. Good people of any age would qualify as golden.

And the good are wise -- a crucial step in the etymological argument that follows.
elegant: The word theoi (gods) comes from theontes (runners) -- the earliest Greeks saw the celestial bodies running across the sky and named them accordingly. As for "demons," Hesiod's golden race were not literally golden but good and noble. And the good are wise -- setting up the etymology of daimon.
summary: Socrates etymologizes "gods" (theoi) as derived from "runners" (theontes), reflecting the ceaseless motion of celestial bodies. He then begins analyzing "demons" through Hesiod's golden race, establishing that the golden men were good and noble, not literally golden, and that the good are wise.
distilled: The gods (theoi) were named from their running nature (theontes), and Hesiod's golden race -- the origin of "demons" -- were good and wise, not literally golden.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Theoi (gods, from thein, to run -- theontes, runners). The derivation of theoi from running reflects pre-Socratic cosmological thinking about celestial motion. The reference to barbarian sun-worship suggests awareness of Persian and Egyptian religion. Hesiod's Works and Days (109-201) describes the five races (gold, silver, bronze, heroes, iron). The transition from cosmic gods to Hesiod's daimones sets up 2.15. Cross-references: Works and Days (Hesiod), Laws (on celestial worship), Timaeus (on cosmic motion).

narrative: "And therefore I have the most entire conviction that he called them demons, because they were daemones (knowing or wise), and in our older Attic dialect the word itself occurs. Now he and other poets say truly, that when a good man dies he has honour and a mighty portion among the dead, and becomes a demon; which is a name given to him signifying wisdom. And I say too, that every wise man who happens to be a good man is more than human (daimonion) both in life and death, and is rightly called a demon."
refashioned:
Socrates completes the etymology: Hesiod called the golden race "demons" because they were daemones -- knowing or wise. The word itself appears in the older Attic dialect with precisely this meaning. Hesiod and other poets tell us that when a good man dies, he receives honor and a mighty portion among the dead and becomes a daimon -- a name that signifies wisdom.

Socrates endorses this view with his own conviction: every wise and good man is more than human -- daimonion -- both in life and in death, and is rightly called a daimon. The word "demon" in its original sense has nothing sinister about it; it is a title of wisdom and goodness, bestowed upon those whose knowledge elevates them above ordinary humanity.
elegant: Hesiod called the golden race "demons" (daimones) because they were knowing and wise. When a good man dies, he becomes a daimon -- wisdom itself is what makes him more than human. Every wise and good person deserves this title, in life and death.
summary: Socrates etymologizes "demon" (daimon) as meaning "knowing" or "wise," connecting it to Hesiod's golden race. A good man becomes a daimon after death because of his wisdom, and every wise, good person is rightly called daimonion -- more than human.
distilled: "Demon" (daimon) originally meant "knowing" or "wise" -- Hesiod's golden race earned this title through wisdom, and every good, wise person is rightly called more than human.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Daimon (from daemones, knowing/wise), daimonion (more than human). This etymology carries deep Socratic significance -- Socrates himself claimed to be guided by a daimonion (divine sign). By connecting daimon to wisdom rather than supernatural power, Socrates reframes his own spiritual experience in intellectual terms. The passage also anticipates the Socratic identification of virtue with knowledge. Cross-references: Apology (on Socrates' daimonion), Symposium (Diotima on daimones as intermediaries), Republic (on the philosopher-king as more than human).

narrative:
"Then I rather think that I am of one mind with you; but what is the meaning of the word 'hero'?" (Eros with an eta, in the old writing eros with an epsilon.)

"I think that there is no difficulty in explaining, for the name is not much altered, and signifies that they were born of love."

"What do you mean?"

"Do you not know that the heroes are demigods?"

"What then?"
refashioned:
Hermogenes agrees about demons and asks about the word "hero." Socrates finds this easy to explain: the name is barely altered from its origin, and it signifies that heroes were born of love. The word heros, he notes, is closely connected to Eros -- in the old writing, eros was spelled with an epsilon, making the connection even more transparent.

When Hermogenes asks what he means, Socrates points out the well-known fact that heroes are demigods -- beings born from the union of gods and mortals. The word "hero" thus encodes its own origin story: heroes are the children of eros, of divine love crossing the boundary between immortal and mortal.
elegant: "Hero" (heros) derives from Eros (love) -- heroes were demigods born from the love between gods and mortals. The name barely needs alteration to reveal its origin.
summary: Socrates etymologizes "hero" (heros) as derived from Eros (love), since heroes were demigods born from unions between gods and mortals.
distilled: The word "hero" derives from Eros because heroes were demigods born of love between gods and mortals.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Heros (hero, from Eros, love). The parenthetical note about eta vs. epsilon reflects ancient awareness of dialectal spelling variation. The etymology connects heroism to divine love (eros) rather than to martial valor -- a characteristically Platonic reframing. The identification of heroes as demigods follows Hesiod. Cross-references: Symposium (on Eros), Hesiod's Works and Days and Theogony (on heroes and demigods).

narrative:
"All of them sprang either from the love of a God for a mortal woman, or of a mortal man for a Goddess; think of the word in the old Attic, and you will see better that the name heros is only a slight alteration of Eros, from whom the heroes sprang: either this is the meaning, or, if not this, then they must have been skilful as rhetoricians and dialecticians, and able to put the question (erotan), for eirein is equivalent to legein. And therefore, as I was saying, in the Attic dialect the heroes turn out to be rhetoricians and questioners. All this is easy enough; the noble breed of heroes are a tribe of sophists and rhetors. But can you tell me why men are called anthropoi? -- that is more difficult."

"No, I cannot; and I would not try even if I could, because I think that you are the more likely to succeed."

"That is to say, you trust to the inspiration of Euthyphro."

"Of course."
refashioned:
Socrates elaborates: all heroes sprang from love between a god and a mortal woman, or a mortal man and a goddess. In the old Attic dialect, heros is clearly just a slight alteration of Eros, the god of love from whom they descended.

But Socrates offers an alternative etymology: perhaps heroes were called this because they were skilled rhetoricians and dialecticians, able to pose questions (erotan) -- since eirein means the same as legein (to speak). On this reading, heroes were essentially sophisticated questioners and speakers. Socrates jokes that the noble breed of heroes turns out to be a tribe of sophists and rhetoricians.

Then he asks the harder question: why are human beings called anthropoi? Hermogenes declines to try, trusting in Socrates' Euthyphro-inspired wisdom to work it out.
elegant: Heroes derive either from Eros (born of divine love) or from erotan (to question) -- making them rhetoricians and dialecticians. Either way, the noble heroes turn out to be a tribe of sophists. But why are humans called anthropoi? That is harder.
summary: Socrates offers two etymologies for "hero": from Eros (love), since heroes were born of divine-mortal unions, or from erotan (to question), making them skilled rhetoricians. He then pivots to the harder question of why humans are called anthropoi.
distilled: Heroes are either children of Eros (love) or masters of erotan (questioning) -- either born of divine passion or skilled in rhetoric and dialectic.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes responding. Greek words etymologized: Heros (from Eros, love, or from erotan, to question; eirein = legein, to speak). The double etymology is characteristic of Socrates' method -- offering multiple derivations and letting them coexist. The joke about heroes being sophists has an ironic edge, given Plato's hostility toward sophistry. The transition to anthropos sets up a major etymology. Cross-references: Symposium (on Eros), Gorgias and Protagoras (on rhetoric vs. dialectic).

narrative: "Your faith is not vain; for at this very moment a new and ingenious thought strikes me, and, if I am not careful, before to-morrow's dawn I shall be wiser than I ought to be. Now, attend to me; and first, remember that we often put in and pull out letters in words, and give names as we please and change the accents. Take, for example, the word Dii Philos; in order to convert this from a sentence into a noun, we omit one of the iotas and sound the middle syllable grave instead of acute; as, on the other hand, letters are sometimes inserted in words instead of being omitted, and the acute takes the place of the grave."
refashioned:
Socrates says Hermogenes' faith in his inspiration is justified -- a new and ingenious thought has just struck him. If he's not careful, he'll become wiser than is proper before tomorrow's dawn.

But first, a methodological reminder: the ancient name-makers frequently inserted and removed letters from words, changed names at will, and shifted accents. Consider the phrase Dii Philos ("dear to Zeus") -- to convert this from a sentence into a single noun (Diphilos), they removed one iota and changed the middle syllable's accent from acute to grave. The reverse also happens: letters get inserted rather than removed, and acute accents replace grave ones.

This principle of phonetic transformation -- addition, subtraction, and accent shifts -- will be essential for the etymology of anthropos that follows.
elegant: Names are frequently transformed through adding, removing, or shifting letters and accents. Dii Philos ("dear to Zeus") becomes the noun Diphilos by dropping a letter and changing an accent. This principle of phonetic transformation is key to understanding how words evolve.
summary: Socrates reminds Hermogenes that ancient name-makers frequently transformed words by adding, removing, or shifting letters and accents, illustrating with how the phrase Dii Philos became the noun Diphilos.
distilled: Words undergo constant transformation -- letters added, removed, accents shifted -- as illustrated by Dii Philos becoming Diphilos.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words analyzed: Dii Philos (dear to Zeus) transformed into Diphilos through letter removal and accent change. This passage establishes the linguistic mechanics that will justify the more creative etymologies to follow. The self-deprecating joke about becoming "wiser than I ought to be" maintains Socratic irony about the limits of human wisdom. Cross-references: This methodological principle will be explicitly challenged later (2.51-2.52) when Hermogenes objects to overly free letter manipulation.

narrative:
"That is true."

"The name anthropos, which was once a sentence, and is now a noun, appears to be a case just of this sort, for one letter, which is the alpha, has been omitted, and the acute on the last syllable has been changed to a grave."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean to say that the word 'man' implies that other animals never examine, or consider, or look up at what they see, but that man not only sees (opope) but considers and looks up at that which he sees, and hence he alone of all animals is rightly anthropos, meaning anathron a opopen."

"May I ask you to examine another word about which I am curious?"

"Certainly."

"I will take that which appears to me to follow next in order. You know the distinction of soul and body?"

"Of course."

"Let us endeavour to analyze them like the previous words."

"You want me first of all to examine the natural fitness of the word psuche (soul), and then of the word soma (body)?"

"Yes."
refashioned:
Socrates reveals the etymology of anthropos (human being). The word was originally a sentence that has been compressed into a noun -- one letter (alpha) was removed and the accent on the last syllable changed from acute to grave.

The meaning: other animals simply see things but never examine or reflect on what they perceive. Humans, uniquely, not only see (opope) but look upward and contemplate what they have seen. The word anthropos is really anathron ha opopen -- "the one who looks up at and examines what he has seen." Human beings are the only creatures that reflect on their experience, and this reflective capacity is encoded in our very name.

Hermogenes is satisfied and asks to move on to soul and body. He wants Socrates to examine the natural fitness of psuche (soul) and soma (body) using the same method.
elegant: Anthropos (human) is really anathron ha opopen -- "the one who looks up at and examines what he has seen." Unlike other animals who merely see, humans reflect on their perceptions. Our name encodes our unique capacity for contemplation.
summary: Socrates etymologizes anthropos (human) as a compressed form of anathron ha opopen -- "the one who examines what he has seen." Humans are uniquely named for their capacity to reflect on experience, unlike other animals who merely perceive.
distilled: "Human" (anthropos) means "the one who looks up at and examines what he has seen" -- our name encodes the uniquely human capacity for reflective contemplation.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Anthropos (from anathron ha opopen -- examining what one has seen; or anathrein + ops, looking upward). This etymology defines humanity by the capacity for reflective thought -- a deeply Socratic and Platonic idea. The upward gaze connects to the Uranus etymology (2.11) and to the philosophical life of contemplation. The transition to soul (psuche) and body (soma) marks a shift from proper names to fundamental metaphysical concepts. Cross-references: Republic (the cave allegory -- looking upward toward truth), Theaetetus (on wonder and contemplation), Phaedo (on the soul's contemplative nature).

narrative: "If I am to say what occurs to me at the moment, I should imagine that those who first used the name psuche meant to express that the soul when in the body is the source of life, and gives the power of breath and revival (anapsuchon), and when this reviving power fails then the body perishes and dies, and this, if I am not mistaken, they called psyche. But please stay a moment; I fancy that I can discover something which will be more acceptable to the disciples of Euthyphro, for I am afraid that they will scorn this explanation. What do you say to another?"
refashioned:
Socrates offers his first attempt at the etymology of psuche (soul). Off the top of his head, he imagines the original name-givers meant to express that the soul, while in the body, is the source of life. It provides the power of breath and revival -- anapsuchon, the reviving or cooling force -- and when this power fails, the body perishes and dies. From this life-giving, reviving function, they called it psyche.

But Socrates pauses, dissatisfied. He suspects he can find a derivation more acceptable to the disciples of Euthyphro -- those philosophically sophisticated enough to scorn a merely physiological explanation. The breath-based etymology is too materialistic; something more elevated is needed.
elegant: The first etymology of psuche (soul): it gives the body breath and revival (anapsuchon), and when this power fails, the body dies. But Socrates finds this too materialistic and searches for something more philosophically sophisticated.
summary: Socrates first derives psuche (soul) from anapsuchon -- the power of breath and revival that keeps the body alive. Finding this too materialistic, he promises a more philosophically sophisticated alternative.
distilled: The soul (psuche) was first named for its life-giving breath (anapsuchon), but Socrates finds this materialistic etymology insufficient.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Psuche (soul, from anapsuchon -- reviving, breathing, cooling). The breath-based etymology connects soul to biological life -- a view Socrates will upgrade in the next entry. The reference to "disciples of Euthyphro" maintains the ironic framing of Socrates' inspiration. The two-stage etymology (materialistic then philosophical) mirrors Socrates' general method of moving from appearance to reality. Cross-references: Phaedo (on the soul as life-principle), Philebus (on the soul and body relationship).

narrative:
"Let me hear."

"What is that which holds and carries and gives life and motion to the entire nature of the body? What else but the soul?"

"Just that."

"And do you not believe with Anaxagoras, that mind or soul is the ordering and containing principle of all things?"

"Yes; I do."

"Then you may well call that power phuseche which carries and holds nature (e phusin ochei, kai echei), and this may be refined away into psuche."

"Certainly; and this derivation is, I think, more scientific than the other."

"It is so; but I cannot help laughing, if I am to suppose that this was the true meaning of the name."

"But what shall we say of the next word?"

"You mean soma (the body)."

"Yes."
refashioned:
Socrates offers his improved etymology for psuche. What holds, carries, and gives life and motion to the body's entire nature? Nothing but the soul. And following Anaxagoras, who held that mind or soul is the ordering and containing principle of all things, we can see that psuche really derives from phuseche -- "that which carries and holds nature" (phusin ochei kai echei). This has been refined and compressed over time into the word psuche.

Hermogenes finds this derivation more scientific than the breath-based one. Socrates agrees it's better but can't help laughing at the thought that this was truly the original intention behind the name. The laughter signals his awareness that these etymologies, however clever, may be more playful than truthful.

They then turn to soma -- the body.
elegant: The soul (psuche) is really phuseche -- "that which carries and holds nature." Following Anaxagoras, the soul is the ordering principle of all things. This is more scientific than the breath etymology, though Socrates laughs at the idea it was the true original meaning.
summary: Socrates offers an improved etymology: psuche derives from phuseche, meaning "that which carries and holds nature," aligning with Anaxagoras' view of mind as the ordering principle of all things. Even Socrates finds this derivation somewhat laughable.
distilled: The soul (psuche) is phuseche -- "that which carries and holds nature" -- reflecting Anaxagoras' doctrine that mind orders all things.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Psuche (second etymology -- from phuseche, phusin ochei kai echei, carrying and holding nature). The invocation of Anaxagoras is significant -- his doctrine that nous (mind) orders all things was deeply influential on Socrates (as reported in Phaedo 97b-99d). Socrates' laughter at his own etymology signals the self-aware playfulness of the entire etymological exercise. Cross-references: Phaedo 97b-99d (Socrates on Anaxagoras), Philebus (on mind as cosmic principle).

narrative:
"That may be variously interpreted; and yet more variously if a little permutation is allowed. For some say that the body is the grave (sema) of the soul which may be thought to be buried in our present life; or again the index of the soul, because the soul gives indications to (semainei) the body; probably the Orphic poets were the inventors of the name, and they were under the impression that the soul is suffering the punishment of sin, and that the body is an enclosure or prison in which the soul is incarcerated, kept safe (soma, sozetai), as the name soma implies, until the penalty is paid; according to this view, not even a letter of the word need be changed."

"I think, Socrates, that we have said enough of this class of words. But have we any more explanations of the names of the Gods, like that which you were giving of Zeus? I should like to know whether any similar principle of correctness is to be applied to them."
refashioned:
Socrates offers multiple etymologies for soma (body), noting that even more interpretations emerge if letter permutation is allowed.

First: the body is the grave (sema) of the soul -- the soul is buried in our present bodily life. Second: the body is the sign or index (sema) of the soul, because the soul expresses itself through and gives indications (semainei) to the body.

Third, and most striking: the Orphic poets probably invented this name. They believed the soul is suffering punishment for sin, and the body is an enclosure or prison in which the soul is incarcerated and kept safe (soma from sozetai, to be preserved) until the penalty is fully paid. On this reading, not a single letter needs to be changed -- soma naturally means "that which preserves" the imprisoned soul.

Hermogenes, satisfied with these analyses of soul and body, asks to return to the names of the gods.
elegant: The body (soma) has three etymologies: the grave (sema) where the soul is buried; the sign (sema) through which the soul expresses itself; or, per the Orphics, the prison that keeps the soul safe (sozetai) until its punishment is complete. The last requires no letter changes at all.
summary: Socrates provides three etymologies for soma (body): as the soul's grave (sema), as the soul's sign or index (semainei), and as the Orphic prison that keeps the soul safe (sozetai) during its punishment. Hermogenes then redirects to divine names.
distilled: The body (soma) is the soul's grave (sema), its sign (semainei), or its Orphic prison that preserves it (sozetai) until punishment is complete.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Soma (body, from sema -- grave/sign, semainei -- to signify, sozetai -- to be kept safe/preserved). The soma-sema doctrine (body as tomb) is one of the most famous Orphic-Pythagorean ideas in Plato. The three etymologies represent three philosophical frameworks: pessimistic dualism (body as grave), expressivist psychology (body as sign), and Orphic soteriology (body as prison). Cross-references: Phaedo 62b (on the body as prison), Gorgias 493a (soma-sema doctrine), Republic (on the soul's imprisonment), Phaedrus (on the soul's fall).

narrative: "Yes, indeed, Hermogenes; and there is one excellent principle which, as men of sense, we must acknowledge, -- that of the Gods we know nothing, either of their natures or of the names which they give themselves; but we are sure that the names by which they call themselves, whatever they may be, are true. And this is the best of all principles; and the next best is to say, as in prayers, that we will call them by any sort or kind of names or patronymics which they like, because we do not know of any other. That also, I think, is a very good custom, and one which I should much wish to observe. Let us, then, if you please, in the first place announce to them that we are not enquiring about them; we do not presume that we are able to do so; but we are enquiring about the meaning of men in giving them these names, -- in this there can be small blame."
refashioned:
Before diving into divine names, Socrates establishes an important caveat. As rational people, they must acknowledge a fundamental limitation: we know nothing about the gods -- neither their true natures nor the names they give themselves. Whatever names the gods use for themselves are certainly true, but we have no access to them.

The best principle, then, is to admit this ignorance. The next best is to follow the custom of prayer, where we address the gods by whatever names and titles they prefer, since we know no others.

Socrates proposes a diplomatic announcement to the gods: we are not investigating you or presuming to understand your nature. We are merely examining what humans meant when they gave you these names. In that, there can be little blame.

This pious disclaimer is both genuinely reverent and strategically clever -- it frees Socrates to etymologize divine names without committing impiety.
elegant: We know nothing of the gods' true natures or self-given names. Our investigation concerns only what humans meant by the names they assigned. This pious disclaimer frees the etymological inquiry from the charge of impiety.
summary: Socrates establishes a pious disclaimer before analyzing divine names: we know nothing about the gods or their true names. The investigation concerns only what humans intended when they named the gods, which cannot be blameworthy.
distilled: We know nothing of the gods' true names -- our investigation concerns only what humans meant by the names they assigned, which frees the inquiry from impiety.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. No specific etymologies here; this is a methodological and theological disclaimer. The passage reflects genuine Socratic piety -- the acknowledgment of human ignorance about divine matters. It also serves a practical purpose: protecting Socrates from charges of impiety (a real danger, given his historical trial and execution). The prayer convention Socrates references is the Greek practice of addressing gods with multiple epithets to avoid offense. Cross-references: Apology (on Socrates' piety), Euthyphro (on knowledge of divine things), Laws (on proper worship).

narrative:
"I think, Socrates, that you are quite right, and I would like to do as you say."

"Shall we begin, then, with Hestia, according to custom?"

"Yes, that will be very proper."

"What may we suppose him to have meant who gave the name Hestia?"

"That is another and certainly a most difficult question."

"My dear Hermogenes, the first imposers of names must surely have been considerable persons; they were philosophers, and had a good deal to say."

"Well, and what of them?"
refashioned:
With the disclaimer in place, Socrates asks where to begin. Following Greek religious custom, which always invokes Hestia first in sacrifices, they start with her name.

Hermogenes finds this a particularly difficult question. Socrates responds with a general observation: the original name-givers must have been considerable people -- they were philosophers with a great deal to say about the nature of things. The names they created were not arbitrary labels but encoded philosophical understanding.

This remark elevates the entire etymological project. If the first namers were philosophers, then etymological analysis is a form of intellectual archaeology -- recovering ancient philosophical insights buried within ordinary words.
elegant: Following religious custom, they begin with Hestia. Socrates observes that the first name-givers were philosophers -- their names encode philosophical understanding, making etymology a form of intellectual archaeology.
summary: Following Greek custom of invoking Hestia first, Socrates begins the divine name analysis. He asserts that the original name-givers were philosophers, suggesting that etymology can recover philosophical insights embedded in language.
distilled: The original name-givers were philosophers, making etymology a form of intellectual archaeology that recovers ancient wisdom embedded in words.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Hestia, goddess of the hearth, was traditionally invoked first in Greek religious ritual ("beginning from Hestia" was proverbial). Hermogenes' remark that she is a difficult case is significant -- Socrates will connect her name to ousia (essence/being) in the next entry, making her the most philosophically fundamental deity. The characterization of early name-givers as philosophers reflects the pre-Socratic tradition and anticipates the Heraclitean interpretation. Cross-references: Laws (on Hestia worship), Phaedrus 247a (Hestia in the procession of the gods).

narrative: "They are the men to whom I should attribute the imposition of names. Even in foreign names, if you analyze them, a meaning is still discernible. For example, that which we term ousia is by some called esia, and by others again osia. Now that the essence of things should be called estia, which is akin to the first of these (esia = estia), is rational enough. And there is reason in the Athenians calling that estia which participates in ousia. For in ancient times we too seem to have said esia for ousia, and this you may note to have been the idea of those who appointed that sacrifices should be first offered to estia, which was natural enough if they meant that estia was the essence of things. Those again who read osia seem to have inclined to the opinion of Heracleitus, that all things flow and nothing stands; with them the pushing principle (othoun) is the cause and ruling power of all things, and is therefore rightly called osia. Enough of this, which is all that we who know nothing can affirm. Next in order after Hestia we ought to consider Rhea and Cronos, although the name of Cronos has been already discussed. But I dare say that I am talking great nonsense."
refashioned:
Socrates reveals the philosophical depth hidden in Hestia's name. The word ousia (essence or being) appears in different dialects as esia and osia. Now, estia is closely related to esia -- and if the essence of things is called estia, that makes Hestia the goddess of essence itself. This explains why the Athenians always sacrificed to Hestia first: they recognized that she represents ousia, the essence of all things.

But Socrates offers an alternative reading for those who spell it osia. This form suggests the Heraclitean view that all things are in flux and nothing stands still. In this interpretation, the pushing or driving principle (othoun) is the cause and ruling power behind everything, and this force is rightly called osia -- being as motion.

So Hestia's name encodes a fundamental philosophical divide: is being (ousia) stable essence or perpetual flux? Socrates modestly adds that this is all they can say from a position of acknowledged ignorance. He then proposes moving to Rhea and Kronos, noting that Kronos has already been discussed.

He adds, self-deprecatingly, that he is probably talking great nonsense.
elegant: Hestia's name connects to ousia (essence): esia/estia links the goddess to stable being, while the variant osia suggests Heraclitean flux -- the pushing principle (othoun) driving all things. Hestia thus encodes the fundamental question: is being stable or in motion?
summary: Socrates connects Hestia to ousia (essence/being) through dialectal variants. The form esia/estia links her to stable essence, while osia suggests Heraclitean flux. Hestia's name thus encodes the deepest philosophical question about the nature of being.
distilled: Hestia's name connects to ousia (essence) -- either as stable being (estia/esia) or as Heraclitean flux (osia from othoun, pushing), encoding the fundamental question of whether reality is permanent or in motion.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Hestia (from esia/estia, connected to ousia -- essence/being; alternatively from osia, connected to othoun -- pushing, reflecting Heraclitean flux). This is philosophically the most significant etymology so far. By connecting Hestia to ousia, Socrates makes the humble hearth goddess into the embodiment of Being itself. The two readings (stable essence vs. flux) map onto the Parmenides-Heraclitus debate that structures much of pre-Socratic philosophy. Cross-references: Phaedrus 247a (Hestia remaining in the house of the gods while others process), Heraclitus (fragments on flux), Parmenides (on stable being).

narrative:
"Why, Socrates?"

"My good friend, I have discovered a hive of wisdom."

"Of what nature?"

"Well, rather ridiculous, and yet plausible."

"How plausible?"

"I fancy to myself Heracleitus repeating wise traditions of antiquity as old as the days of Cronos and Rhea, and of which Homer also spoke."

"How do you mean?"

"Heracleitus is supposed to say that all things are in motion and nothing at rest; he compares them to the stream of a river, and says that you cannot go into the same water twice."

"That is true."

"Well, then, how can we avoid inferring that he who gave the names of Cronos and Rhea to the ancestors of the Gods, agreed pretty much in the doctrine of Heracleitus? Is the giving of the names of streams to both of them purely accidental? Compare the line in which Homer, and, as I believe, Hesiod also, tells of --"

"I think that there is something in what you say, Socrates; but I do not understand the meaning of the name Tethys."

"Well, that is almost self-explained, being only the name of a spring, a little disguised; for that which is strained and filtered (diattomenon, ethoumenon) may be likened to a spring, and the name Tethys is made up of these two words."

"The idea is ingenious, Socrates."

"To be sure. But what comes next? -- of Zeus we have spoken."

"Yes."

"Then let us next take his two brothers, Poseidon and Pluto, whether the latter is called by that or by his other name."

"By all means."
refashioned:
Socrates announces he has discovered a "hive of wisdom" -- ridiculous yet plausible. He imagines Heraclitus drawing on ancient traditions as old as Kronos and Rhea themselves. Heraclitus taught that all things are in motion, nothing is at rest; you cannot step into the same river twice.

But here is the insight: whoever named Kronos and Rhea gave them names associated with streams and flowing. Can this be purely accidental? The original namers must have shared Heraclitus' doctrine of universal flux. Homer and Hesiod also connected these deities to flowing water.

Hermogenes asks about Tethys. Socrates finds this nearly self-explanatory: her name is just a slightly disguised word for "spring." Tethys combines diattomenon (strained) and ethoumenon (filtered) -- the qualities of a spring's water.

With Zeus already discussed, they move on to his brothers Poseidon and Pluto.
elegant: Kronos and Rhea have names connected to streams and flowing -- suggesting their namers shared Heraclitus' doctrine that everything is in motion. Tethys is simply a disguised word for "spring" (from diattomenon + ethoumenon, strained and filtered). The ancient divine genealogy encodes Heraclitean flux.
summary: Socrates discovers that Kronos and Rhea both have stream-related names, suggesting the original namers held Heraclitean views about universal flux. Tethys is etymologized as a spring (from strained + filtered). The divine genealogy encodes pre-Socratic philosophy.
distilled: Kronos, Rhea, and Tethys all have names connected to flowing water, suggesting the ancient name-givers shared Heraclitus' doctrine that all things are in perpetual motion.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Kronos and Rhea (connected to streams/flow -- rhein, to flow; Kronos already analyzed in 2.11), Tethys (from diattomenon + ethoumenon, strained and filtered, i.e., a spring). The Heraclitean interpretation of divine names is a major theme of Part 2 -- Socrates reads the entire mythological tradition as encoding the doctrine of flux. The famous Heraclitean fragment about the river appears here. Cross-references: Heraclitus fr. 91 (river fragment), Theaetetus 152e-153a (on Heraclitean flux), Hesiod's Theogony (on Tethys as ocean goddess).

narrative: "Poseidon is Posidesmos, the chain of the feet; the original inventor of the name had been stopped by the watery element in his walks, and not allowed to go on, and therefore he called the ruler of this element Poseidon; the epsilon was probably inserted as an ornament. Yet, perhaps, not so; but the name may have been originally written with a double lambda and not with a sigma, meaning that the God knew many things (Polla eidos). And perhaps also he being the shaker of the earth, has been named from shaking (seiein), and then pi and delta have been added. Pluto gives wealth (Ploutos), and his name means the giver of wealth, which comes out of the earth beneath. People in general appear to imagine that the term Hades is connected with the invisible (aeides) and so they are led by their fears to call the God Pluto instead."
refashioned:
Socrates offers three etymologies for Poseidon. First: Posidesmos, meaning "chain of the feet" -- the original namer was stopped by water during a walk and couldn't continue, so he named the ruler of this obstructing element accordingly. The epsilon was probably just an ornamental addition.

Second: the name may originally have been written with a double lambda instead of sigma, yielding Polla eidos -- "he who knows many things," a god of vast knowledge.

Third: since Poseidon is the earth-shaker, his name may derive from seiein (to shake), with pi and delta added.

As for Pluto, the name straightforwardly means "wealth-giver" (from ploutos, wealth) -- the god who bestows riches from beneath the earth. The common people, however, think the name Hades is connected to aeides (invisible), and out of fear of the unseen, they prefer to call him Pluto instead.
elegant: Poseidon has three possible etymologies: "chain of the feet" (water stops walkers), "knower of many things" (polla eidos), or "the shaker" (seiein). Pluto simply means "wealth-giver." People call him Pluto rather than Hades out of fear, wrongly connecting Hades to the invisible (aeides).
summary: Socrates gives Poseidon three etymologies: chain of the feet, knower of many things, or the shaker. Pluto means wealth-giver. The common people mistakenly connect Hades to invisibility and use Pluto as a euphemism born of fear.
distilled: Poseidon means either "chain of the feet," "knower of many things," or "the shaker," while Pluto (wealth-giver) is a fearful euphemism for Hades, which people wrongly connect to invisibility.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Poseidon (from Posidesmos -- posi + desmos, chain of feet; or Polla eidos, knowing many things; or seiein, to shake), Pluto/Ploutos (from ploutos, wealth), Hades (popularly but incorrectly from aeides, invisible). The triple etymology for Poseidon -- physical obstruction, knowledge, seismic power -- reflects the god's multiple domains (sea, earthquakes, horses). The Hades/Pluto distinction anticipates the extended analysis in 2.28-2.29. Cross-references: Republic 363c-d (on Hades and fear of death), Gorgias (on death and the underworld).

narrative:
"And what is the true derivation?"

"In spite of the mistakes which are made about the power of this deity, and the foolish fears which people have of him, such as the fear of always being with him after death, and of the soul denuded of the body going to him (compare Rep.), my belief is that all is quite consistent, and that the office and name of the God really correspond."

"Why, how is that?"

"I will tell you my own opinion; but first, I should like to ask you which chain does any animal feel to be the stronger? and which confines him more to the same spot, -- desire or necessity?"

"Desire, Socrates, is stronger far."

"And do you not think that many a one would escape from Hades, if he did not bind those who depart to him by the strongest of chains?"

"Assuredly they would."

"And if by the greatest of chains, then by some desire, as I should certainly infer, and not by necessity?"

"That is clear."

"And there are many desires?"

"Yes."

"And therefore by the greatest desire, if the chain is to be the greatest?"

"Yes."

"And is any desire stronger than the thought that you will be made better by associating with another?"

"Certainly not."
refashioned:
Hermogenes asks for the true derivation of Hades. Socrates says that despite the common mistakes and foolish fears surrounding this deity -- the dread of being with him forever after death, of the soul stripped of the body going to him -- the god's name and office are actually perfectly consistent.

He builds the argument through questions. Which binds any creature more powerfully to one spot -- desire or necessity? Hermogenes answers without hesitation: desire is far stronger. Then if the dead don't flee from Hades, he must bind them with the strongest possible chain. And the strongest chain must be the greatest desire, not compulsion. Among all desires, the most powerful is the conviction that associating with someone will make you better.

The implication is revolutionary: Hades holds the dead not through fear or force, but through the desire for self-improvement. The underworld becomes a place of philosophical education.
elegant: Desire is a stronger chain than necessity. Hades binds the dead with the greatest desire of all -- the conviction that associating with him will make them better. The underworld is not a prison but a school; the dead stay willingly.
summary: Socrates argues that Hades binds the dead not through force but through desire -- specifically the desire for self-improvement through association with a wiser being. The strongest chain is not necessity but the longing to become better.
distilled: Hades binds the dead with the strongest chain of all: the desire to become better through association with him -- not force but the longing for self-improvement.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. This passage reinterprets Hades as a philosophical benefactor rather than a fearsome captor. The argument that desire is stronger than necessity is characteristically Socratic -- it prioritizes internal motivation over external compulsion. The "strongest chain" being the desire for improvement reflects Socrates' own practice: he drew people to him through the promise of becoming better. Cross-references: Republic (on Hades, referenced in the text), Symposium (on desire and the good), Phaedo (on the soul's liberation from the body).

narrative:
"And is not that the reason, Hermogenes, why no one, who has been to him, is willing to come back to us? Even the Sirens, like all the rest of the world, have been laid under his spells. Such a charm, as I imagine, is the God able to infuse into his words. And, according to this view, he is the perfect and accomplished Sophist, and the great benefactor of the inhabitants of the other world; and even to us who are upon earth he sends from below exceeding blessings. For he has much more than he wants down there; wherefore he is called Pluto (or the rich). Note also, that he will have nothing to do with men while they are in the body, but only when the soul is liberated from the desires and evils of the body. Now there is a great deal of philosophy and reflection in that; for in their liberated state he can bind them with the desire of virtue, but while they are flustered and maddened by the body, not even father Cronos himself would suffice to keep them with him in his own far-famed chains."

"There is a deal of truth in what you say."

"Yes, Hermogenes, and the legislator called him Hades, not from the unseen (aeides) -- far otherwise, but from his knowledge (eidenai) of all noble things."

"Very good; and what do we say of Demeter, and Here, and Apollo, and Athene, and Hephaestus, and Ares, and the other deities?"
refashioned:
This is why no one who has gone to Hades willingly returns to us, Socrates explains. Even the Sirens have fallen under his spell. The god infuses such charm into his words that he is, in effect, the perfect and accomplished Sophist -- not in the pejorative sense, but as the supreme teacher and benefactor of the dead. He even sends exceeding blessings up to us on earth, for he has more wealth than he needs below -- hence the name Pluto, "the rich."

Crucially, Hades has nothing to do with people while they're still in their bodies. He waits until the soul is liberated from bodily desires and evils. There's deep philosophy in this: only in the liberated state can he bind souls with the desire for virtue. While people are flustered and maddened by bodily passions, not even Kronos with his famous chains could hold them.

So the true etymology of Hades is not from aeides (invisible) but from eidenai (to know). He is the god who knows all noble things -- wisdom, not darkness, defines his realm.

Hermogenes, satisfied, asks about the remaining major deities.
elegant: No one returns from Hades because he binds souls with the desire for virtue -- he is the perfect teacher. He waits until souls are freed from bodily passions. His true name comes not from aeides (invisible) but from eidenai (to know) -- he is the god of knowledge, not darkness.
summary: Socrates reinterprets Hades as the perfect philosopher-teacher who binds liberated souls with the desire for virtue. The true etymology is from eidenai (to know), not aeides (invisible). Hades is the god of knowledge, and his realm is one of philosophical fulfillment.
distilled: Hades derives from eidenai (to know), not aeides (invisible) -- he is the philosopher-god who binds liberated souls with the desire for virtue and knowledge of noble things.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes responding. Greek words etymologized: Hades (from eidenai, to know, not aeides, invisible), Pluto (from ploutos, wealth -- he has surplus riches). This is one of the most philosophically ambitious etymologies in the dialogue. Hades becomes a figure for the examined life: the soul, freed from bodily distraction, discovers that knowledge of noble things is the most binding desire. Calling Hades the "perfect Sophist" is striking -- Socrates reclaims the term from its pejorative associations. Cross-references: Phaedo (on the soul's liberation), Republic (on Hades myths), Gorgias 523a-527a (on judgment in Hades), Symposium (on desire for the good).

narrative: "Demeter is e didousa meter, who gives food like a mother; Here is the lovely one (erate) -- for Zeus, according to tradition, loved and married her; possibly also the name may have been given when the legislator was thinking of the heavens, and may be only a disguise of the air (aer), putting the end in the place of the beginning. You will recognize the truth of this if you repeat the letters of Here several times over. People dread the name of Pherephatta as they dread the name of Apollo, -- and with as little reason; the fear, if I am not mistaken, only arises from their ignorance of the nature of names. But they go changing the name into Phersephone, and they are terrified at this; whereas the new name means only that the Goddess is wise (sophe); for seeing that all things in the world are in motion (pheromenon), that principle which embraces and touches and is able to follow them, is wisdom. And therefore the Goddess may be truly called Pherepaphe (Pherepapha), or some name like it, because she touches that which is in motion (tou pheromenon ephaptomene), herein showing her wisdom. And Hades, who is wise, consorts with her, because she is wise. They alter her name into Pherephatta now-a-days, because the present generation care for euphony more than truth. There is the other name, Apollo, which, as I was saying, is generally supposed to have some terrible signification. Have you remarked this fact?"
refashioned:
Socrates rapidly etymologizes several major deities.

Demeter is "the giving mother" (e didousa meter) -- she who provides food like a mother nourishes her children.

Hera is "the lovely one" (erate), since Zeus loved and married her. Alternatively, her name may disguise aer (air) -- if you rearrange the letters of Hera, you get something close. The legislator may have been thinking of the heavens.

Persephone (Pherephatta/Pherepapha) terrifies people, just as Apollo's name does -- but in both cases the fear comes from ignorance of what the names actually mean. Her true name, Pherepapha, means "she who touches that which is in motion" (tou pheromenon ephaptomene). Since all things are in motion and wisdom is the capacity to embrace, touch, and follow moving things, Persephone is fundamentally the goddess of wisdom. That's why the wise Hades consorts with her -- wisdom attracts wisdom.

The modern form Pherephatta is simply a euphonic corruption. People today care more about how words sound than what they mean.

Socrates then turns to Apollo, noting that his name is generally thought to carry some terrible meaning.
elegant: Demeter is "the giving mother." Hera is "the lovely one" or a disguise of aer (air). Persephone -- properly Pherepapha -- means "she who touches what is in motion," i.e., wisdom itself. Hades consorts with her because wisdom attracts wisdom. Modern forms sacrifice truth for euphony.
summary: Socrates etymologizes Demeter (giving mother), Hera (lovely one or disguised air), and Persephone (she who touches what moves -- wisdom). Persephone's association with the wise Hades reflects wisdom seeking wisdom. Modern name-forms prioritize euphony over truth.
distilled: Demeter is the giving mother, Hera the lovely one (or air), and Persephone the wise -- she who touches all things in motion, which is why the knowledge-god Hades consorts with her.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Demeter (e didousa meter, the giving mother), Hera/Here (erate, lovely, or aer, air rearranged), Pherephatta/Pherepapha/Persephone (tou pheromenon ephaptomene, touching what is in motion = wisdom/sophe). The Persephone etymology is notable for reinterpreting the terrifying queen of the underworld as a figure of wisdom. The complaint about euphony over truth reflects a broader Platonic concern about surface appearances vs. reality. Cross-references: Homeric Hymn to Demeter (on Persephone), Republic (on appearance vs. reality), Phaedrus (on beauty and wisdom).

narrative:
"To be sure I have, and what you say is true."

"But the name, in my opinion, is really most expressive of the power of the God."

"How so?"

"I will endeavour to explain, for I do not believe that any single name could have been better adapted to express the attributes of the God, embracing and in a manner signifying all four of them, -- music, and prophecy, and medicine, and archery."

"That must be a strange name, and I should like to hear the explanation."

"Say rather an harmonious name, as beseems the God of Harmony. In the first place, the purgations and purifications which doctors and diviners use, and their fumigations with drugs magical or medicinal, as well as their washings and lustral sprinklings, have all one and the same object, which is to make a man pure both in body and soul."

"Very true."

"And is not Apollo the purifier, and the washer, and the absolver from all impurities?"

"Very true."
refashioned:
Hermogenes confirms that Apollo's name is popularly feared. Socrates disagrees with the fear -- the name is actually the most expressive possible, capturing all four of the god's domains: music, prophecy, medicine, and archery.

Hermogenes calls this a strange claim, but Socrates corrects him: it's a harmonious name, as befits the God of Harmony.

He begins with the purification aspect. Doctors and diviners use purgations, purifications, fumigations (magical and medicinal), washings, and lustral sprinklings, all with one object: to make a person pure in both body and soul. Apollo is the purifier, the washer, the absolver from all impurities. This is the first of four etymological threads that Socrates will weave together.
elegant: Apollo's name captures all four of his domains: music, prophecy, medicine, and archery. The first thread is purification -- Apollo is the purifier who makes people pure in body and soul through washing and absolution.
summary: Socrates begins his analysis of Apollo by arguing the name encompasses all four divine attributes: music, prophecy, medicine, and archery. He starts with purification, establishing Apollo as the great purifier of body and soul.
distilled: Apollo's name harmoniously unifies four domains -- music, prophecy, medicine, and archery -- beginning with his role as the great purifier of body and soul.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. This begins the extended Apollo etymology (2.31-2.32), one of the most elaborate in the dialogue. Apollo's four domains -- music, prophecy, medicine, archery -- map onto four etymological derivations. The purification theme connects to the purgation of Euthyphro's influence mentioned in 2.12 and to the broader Greek concept of katharsis. Cross-references: Phaedo (on philosophical purification), Republic (on Apollo as patron of the state), Ion (on Apollo and prophetic inspiration).

narrative:
"Then in reference to his ablutions and absolutions, as being the physician who orders them, he may be rightly called Apolouon (purifier); or in respect of his powers of divination, and his truth and sincerity, which is the same as truth, he may be most fitly called Aplos, from aplous (sincere), as in the Thessalian dialect, for all the Thessalians call him Aplos; also he is aei Ballon (always shooting), because he is a master archer who never misses; or again, the name may refer to his musical attributes, and then, as in akolouthos, and akoitis, and in many other words the alpha is supposed to mean 'together,' so the meaning of the name Apollo will be 'moving together,' whether in the poles of heaven as they are called, or in the harmony of song, which is termed concord, because he moves all together by an harmonious power, as astronomers and musicians ingeniously declare. And he is the God who presides over harmony, and makes all things move together, both among Gods and among men. And as in the words akolouthos and akoitis the alpha is substituted for an omicron, so the name Apollon is equivalent to omopolon; only the second lambda is added in order to avoid the ill-omened sound of destruction (apolon). Now the suspicion of this destructive power still haunts the minds of some who do not consider the true value of the name, which, as I was saying just now, has reference to all the powers of the God, who is the single one, the everdarting, the purifier, the mover together (aplous, aei Ballon, apolouon, omopolon). The name of the Muses and of music would seem to be derived from their making philosophical enquiries (mosthai); and Leto is called by this name, because she is such a gentle Goddess, and so willing (ethelemon) to grant our requests; or her name may be Letho, as she is often called by strangers -- they seem to imply by it her amiability, and her smooth and easy-going way of behaving. Artemis is named from her healthy (artemes), well-ordered nature, and because of her love of virginity, perhaps because she is a proficient in virtue (arete), and perhaps also as hating intercourse of the sexes (ton aroton misesasa). He who gave the Goddess her name may have had any or all of these reasons."

"What is the meaning of Dionysus and Aphrodite?"
refashioned:
Socrates completes his four-fold etymology of Apollo. As the physician who prescribes purifications, he is Apolouon -- the purifier. In his prophetic aspect, embodying truth and sincerity, he is Aplos (from aplous, sincere) -- which is what the Thessalians actually call him. As master archer who never misses, he is Aei Ballon -- always shooting. And as god of music and cosmic harmony, the alpha in Apollo means "together" (as in akolouthos and akoitis), making him Omopolon -- "moving all together." He presides over the harmony of celestial motion and musical concord alike, making all things move in unison among gods and men. The second lambda was added to avoid the ominous sound of apolon (destruction).

So Apollo's name captures four attributes in one: the single one (aplous), the ever-darting (aei Ballon), the purifier (apolouon), the mover-together (omopolon). People who fear the name are simply ignorant of its true value.

Socrates then quickly handles several more deities. The Muses and music derive from mosthai -- making philosophical inquiries. Leto is the gentle goddess, named from ethelemon (willing) or from her smooth, easy-going nature. Artemis comes from artemes (healthy, well-ordered), her love of virginity connecting to arete (virtue) and perhaps aroton misesasa (hating sexual intercourse).

Hermogenes then asks about Dionysus and Aphrodite.
elegant: Apollo unifies four etymologies: Apolouon (purifier), Aplos (sincere/truthful), Aei Ballon (ever-shooting), Omopolon (moving all together in harmony). The Muses derive from philosophical inquiry (mosthai). Leto means willing and gentle. Artemis means healthy, virtuous, and virginal.
summary: Socrates completes Apollo's four-fold etymology: purifier, truth-teller, master archer, and cosmic harmonizer. He then derives the Muses from philosophical inquiry, Leto from gentleness and willingness, and Artemis from health, virtue, and virginity.
distilled: Apollo's name encodes four powers -- purification, sincerity, archery, and cosmic harmony -- while the Muses embody philosophical inquiry, Leto gentleness, and Artemis healthy virtue.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Apollo/Apollon (from apolouon -- purifier; aplous -- sincere; aei Ballon -- always shooting; omopolon -- moving together; second lambda added to avoid apolon, destruction), Muses/Music (from mosthai, philosophical inquiry), Leto (from ethelemon, willing/gentle, or letho, smooth), Artemis (from artemes -- healthy; arete -- virtue; aroton misesasa -- hating intercourse). The Apollo etymology is the most virtuosic in the dialogue -- four derivations unified in a single name. The idea that Apollo governs cosmic harmony anticipates the Timaeus. Cross-references: Timaeus (cosmic harmony), Republic (Apollo as divine authority), Phaedrus (on the Muses and philosophy).

narrative: "Son of Hipponicus, you ask a solemn question; there is a serious and also a facetious explanation of both these names; the serious explanation is not to be had from me, but there is no objection to your hearing the facetious one; for the Gods too love a joke. Dionusos is simply didous oinon (giver of wine), Didoinusos, as he might be called in fun, -- and oinos is properly oionous, because wine makes those who drink, think (oiesthai) that they have a mind (noun) when they have none. The derivation of Aphrodite, born of the foam (aphros), may be fairly accepted on the authority of Hesiod."
refashioned:
Socrates addresses Hermogenes formally as "Son of Hipponicus" -- a solemn form of address for a solemn question. He distinguishes between a serious and a facetious explanation of Dionysus and Aphrodite, claiming he can only offer the facetious one. After all, the gods themselves love a joke.

Dionysus (Dionusos) is simply didous oinon -- "the giver of wine." In a playful form he might be called Didoinusos.
