And wine itself (oinos) is properly oionous, because it makes drinkers think (oiesthai) they have intelligence (nous) when they actually have none. Wine creates the illusion of wisdom.

As for Aphrodite, Socrates defers to Hesiod's authority: she was born from the foam (aphros) of the sea, and her name derives straightforwardly from this origin.
elegant: Dionysus is the "giver of wine" (didous oinon), and wine (oinos) is so named because it makes people think (oiesthai) they have minds (nous) when they don't. Aphrodite comes from aphros (foam), per Hesiod. The facetious etymology reveals wine's illusory wisdom.
summary: Socrates offers playful etymologies: Dionysus means "giver of wine," and wine itself is named for creating the illusion of intelligence. Aphrodite derives from aphros (foam), following Hesiod's account of her birth.
distilled: Dionysus is the giver of wine, which creates the illusion of wisdom (oinos from oiesthai nous), while Aphrodite is simply born of sea-foam (aphros).
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Dionysus/Dionusos (from didous oinon, giver of wine; oinos from oiesthai + nous, thinking one has mind), Aphrodite (from aphros, foam). The facetious disclaimer is notable -- Socrates reserves the "serious" etymology, perhaps suggesting deeper mysteries of Dionysus he won't discuss. The wine-as-false-wisdom etymology aligns with Socratic/Platonic suspicion of intoxication as opposed to genuine knowledge. The Aphrodite etymology follows Hesiod's Theogony 195-198. Cross-references: Symposium (on Aphrodite and Eros), Laws (on wine and education), Hesiod's Theogony (Aphrodite's birth).

narrative:
"Still there remains Athene, whom you, Socrates, as an Athenian, will surely not forget; there are also Hephaestus and Ares."

"I am not likely to forget them."

"No, indeed."

"There is no difficulty in explaining the other appellation of Athene."

"What other appellation?"

"We call her Pallas."

"To be sure."

"And we cannot be wrong in supposing that this is derived from armed dances. For the elevation of oneself or anything else above the earth, or by the use of the hands, we call shaking (pallein), or dancing."

"That is quite true."

"Then that is the explanation of the name Pallas?"

"Yes; but what do you say of the other name?"

"Athene?"

"Yes."
refashioned:
Hermogenes reminds Socrates that Athena remains, along with Hephaestus and Ares. Socrates, as an Athenian, is naturally unlikely to forget his city's patron goddess.

He begins with her secondary name, Pallas, which he finds straightforward. Pallas derives from pallein -- to shake, to brandish, to dance in armor. The elevation of oneself or anything above the earth, whether by the body or the hands, is called pallein. Pallas is thus the goddess of the armed dance, the warrior who shakes her weapons.

Hermogenes accepts this and asks about the primary name, Athene, which proves to be a harder and more philosophically significant question.
elegant: Pallas derives from pallein -- to shake or brandish weapons, to perform armed dances. The goddess shakes and elevates. The harder question is Athene itself.
summary: Socrates etymologizes Pallas from pallein (to shake/brandish/dance in armor), connecting the name to the armed dance. He then turns to the more difficult primary name, Athene.
distilled: Pallas derives from pallein (to shake or dance in armor) -- the goddess who brandishes weapons and performs the armed dance.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Pallas (from pallein, to shake/brandish/dance). The armed dance (pyrrhiche) was an important part of Athenian military culture and religious festival. Athena as warrior-dancer connects to her birth fully armed from Zeus' head. The separate treatment of Pallas and Athene allows Socrates to move from the martial/physical meaning to the intellectual/philosophical one. Cross-references: Timaeus 24d (on Athena and Athens), Laws (on armed dances), Republic (on Athena as patron of wisdom).

narrative: "That is a graver matter, and there, my friend, the modern interpreters of Homer may, I think, assist in explaining the view of the ancients. For most of these in their explanations of the poet, assert that he meant by Athene 'mind' (nous) and 'intelligence' (dianoia), and the maker of names appears to have had a singular notion about her; and indeed calls her by a still higher title, 'divine intelligence' (Theou noesis), as though he would say: This is she who has the mind of God (Theonoa); -- using alpha as a dialectical variety for eta, and taking away iota and sigma (There seems to be some error in the MSS. The meaning is that the word theonoa = theounoa is a curtailed form of theou noesis, but the omitted letters do not agree.). Perhaps, however, the name Theonoe may mean 'she who knows divine things' (Theia noousa) better than others. Nor shall we be far wrong in supposing that the author of it wished to identify this Goddess with moral intelligence (en ethei noesin), and therefore gave her the name ethonoe; which, however, either he or his successors have altered into what they thought a nicer form, and called her Athene."
refashioned:
The name Athene is more philosophically significant. Modern interpreters of Homer agree that the poet meant Athena to represent nous (mind) and dianoia (intelligence). The original name-giver went even further, calling her by the exalted title Theou noesis -- "divine intelligence" or "the mind of God" (Theonoa). The alpha was substituted for eta as a dialectal variant, and certain letters were dropped.

Alternatively, Theonoe might mean "she who knows divine things" (Theia noousa) -- a knower of the sacred rather than divine intelligence itself.

A third possibility: the name-giver wanted to identify Athena with moral intelligence -- thinking within the ethical realm (en ethei noesin). He originally called her Ethonoe, but this was later smoothed into the more pleasant-sounding Athene.

All three readings converge on the same idea: Athena is intelligence -- divine, epistemological, or moral.
elegant: Athene means mind and intelligence -- either Theou noesis (divine intelligence), Theia noousa (knower of divine things), or en ethei noesin (moral intelligence). All readings converge: Athena embodies intelligence in its highest forms.
summary: Socrates offers three etymologies for Athene: divine intelligence (Theou noesis), knower of divine things (Theia noousa), or moral intelligence (en ethei noesin). All three identify the goddess with the highest forms of mind and understanding.
distilled: Athene embodies intelligence itself -- whether as divine mind (Theou noesis), knowledge of sacred things (Theia noousa), or moral understanding (en ethei noesin).
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Athene (from Theonoa/Theou noesis -- divine intelligence; Theia noousa -- knowing divine things; Ethonoe/en ethei noesin -- moral intelligence). The manuscript note about textual corruption acknowledges genuine scholarly difficulty. The three-fold etymology moves from cosmic to epistemological to ethical intelligence, mirroring Plato's own philosophical range. Making Athena the embodiment of nous connects to her role in the Republic as patron of the philosopher's city. Cross-references: Republic (Athena and the just city), Symposium (Athena's wisdom vs. Aphrodite's love), Protagoras (on Athena giving wisdom to mankind).

narrative:
"But what do you say of Hephaestus?"

"Speak you of the princely lord of light (Phaeos istora)?"

"Surely."

"Ephaistos is Phaistos, and has added the eta by attraction; that is obvious to anybody."

"That is very probable, until some more probable notion gets into your head."

"To prevent that, you had better ask what is the derivation of Ares."

"What is Ares?"

"Ares may be called, if you will, from his manhood (arren) and manliness, or if you please, from his hard and unchangeable nature, which is the meaning of arratos: the latter is a derivation in every way appropriate to the God of war."

"Very true."

"And now, by the Gods, let us have no more of the Gods, for I am afraid of them; ask about anything but them, and thou shalt see how the steeds of Euthyphro can prance."

"Only one more God! I should like to know about Hermes, of whom I am said not to be a true son. Let us make him out, and then I shall know whether there is any meaning in what Cratylus says."
refashioned:
Hephaestus receives a quick treatment. Socrates identifies him as Phaeos istor -- "the princely lord of light." The name Ephaistos is really Phaistos with an eta added by phonetic attraction. This is obvious enough.

Ares is equally straightforward: his name comes either from arren (male, manly) -- reflecting masculine warrior nature -- or from arratos (hard, unchangeable, unyielding), perfectly fitting the god of war's rigid, uncompromising character.

Socrates then begs to stop analyzing gods -- he's afraid of them. Ask about anything else, he says, and the steeds of Euthyphro's inspiration will really prance.

But Hermogenes insists on one more: Hermes. This is personal for him -- Cratylus has said that Hermogenes ("son of Hermes") is not truly his name, implying he is no true son of Hermes. He wants to discover whether Cratylus' taunt has any etymological justification.
elegant: Hephaestus is the "lord of light" (Phaeos istor). Ares derives from arren (manly) or arratos (hard, unyielding). Socrates begs to stop with gods but Hermogenes insists on Hermes -- it's personal, since Cratylus said "Hermogenes" is not his true name.
summary: Hephaestus is etymologized as "lord of light" and Ares as "manly" or "unyielding." Socrates wants to stop analyzing gods, but Hermogenes insists on Hermes for personal reasons -- Cratylus claimed his name is not truly his.
distilled: Hephaestus is the lord of light, Ares the manly or unyielding -- and Hermogenes insists on analyzing Hermes because Cratylus questioned whether he truly deserves his name.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Hephaestus/Ephaistos (from Phaeos istor, lord/knower of light), Ares (from arren, male/manly, or arratos, hard/unchangeable). Socrates' fear of the gods is partly comic, partly genuine piety. The transition to Hermes is dramatically motivated -- Hermogenes' name means "son of Hermes" (god of commerce and speech), but Cratylus has declared this name incorrect (see 1.2). This personal stake gives emotional weight to the etymology. Cross-references: Cratylus 383b (Cratylus' challenge to Hermogenes' name), Iliad (Ares as god of war).

narrative: "I should imagine that the name Hermes has to do with speech, and signifies that he is the interpreter (ermeneus), or messenger, or thief, or liar, or bargainer; all that sort of thing has a great deal to do with language; as I was telling you, the word eirein is expressive of the use of speech, and there is an often-recurring Homeric word emesato, which means 'he contrived' -- out of these two words, eirein and mesasthai, the legislator formed the name of the God who invented language and speech; and we may imagine him dictating to us the use of this name: 'O my friends,' says he to us, 'seeing that he is the contriver of tales or speeches, you may rightly call him Eirhemes.' And this has been improved by us, as we think, into Hermes. Iris also appears to have been called from the verb 'to tell' (eirein), because she was a messenger."
refashioned:
Socrates derives Hermes from speech. The name connects to the interpreter (hermeneus), messenger, thief, liar, and bargainer -- all activities deeply intertwined with language. The key roots are eirein (to speak, to use speech) and emesato (a Homeric word meaning "he contrived"). From these two -- eirein and mesasthai -- the legislator formed the name of the god who invented language itself.

Socrates imagines the original name-giver speaking: "My friends, since this god is the contriver of tales and speeches, you should call him Eirhemes." Over time, this was smoothed into the more familiar Hermes.

Iris, the other divine messenger, gets her name from the same root -- eirein (to tell). She is named for her function of telling or reporting the gods' messages.
elegant: Hermes combines eirein (to speak) and mesasthai (to contrive) -- he is the contriver of speech, the god who invented language. Originally Eirhemes, the name was smoothed into Hermes. Iris similarly derives from eirein (to tell), as the gods' messenger.
summary: Hermes' name combines eirein (to speak) and mesasthai (to contrive), making him the divine inventor and contriver of speech. The original form Eirhemes was smoothed into Hermes. Iris derives from the same root as a divine messenger.
distilled: Hermes is the contriver of speech -- his name combines eirein (to speak) and mesasthai (to contrive), originally Eirhemes, identifying him as the inventor of language.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Hermes (from eirein + mesasthai -- speaking + contriving = Eirhemes, contriver of speech; also connected to hermeneus, interpreter), Iris (from eirein, to tell). The Hermes etymology is particularly rich because it connects the god to multiple linguistic functions: interpretation, contrivance, deception, commerce -- all mediated through language. The word hermeneutics derives from Hermes/hermeneus. This etymology is especially relevant to Hermogenes' personal question about his name. Cross-references: Ion (on interpretation), Phaedrus (on writing as Hermes' gift), the hermeneutic tradition in philosophy.

narrative:
"Then I am very sure that Cratylus was quite right in saying that I was no true son of Hermes (Ermogenes), for I am not a good hand at speeches."

"There is also reason, my friend, in Pan being the double-formed son of Hermes."

"How do you make that out?"

"You are aware that speech signifies all things (pan), and is always turning them round and round, and has two forms, true and false?"

"Certainly."

"Is not the truth that is in him the smooth or sacred form which dwells above among the Gods, whereas falsehood dwells among men below, and is rough like the goat of tragedy; for tales and falsehoods have generally to do with the tragic or goatish life, and tragedy is the place of them?"

"Very true."
refashioned:
Hermogenes draws the personal conclusion: if Hermes is the god of speech, then Cratylus was right that Hermogenes is no true son of Hermes -- he admits he is not skilled with words.

Socrates pivots to Pan, Hermes' son, whose double form is philosophically significant. Speech (logos) signifies all things (pan) and constantly turns them over and over. Speech has two forms: true and false.

The true part of Pan is the smooth, sacred form that dwells above among the gods. The false part is rough and goat-like, dwelling below among mortals. Falsehood is connected to the "goatish" -- tragedy itself (tragoidia) derives from tragos (goat), and tragedy is the realm of dramatic tales and fabrications.

Pan's dual nature -- smooth above, rough and goatlike below -- embodies speech's dual nature: divine truth and mortal falsehood.
elegant: Hermogenes concedes he is no true son of the speech-god. Pan, Hermes' son, embodies speech's dual nature: smooth and true above (among the gods), rough and goatlike below (among mortals). Tragedy (from tragos, goat) is the realm of falsehood.
summary: Hermogenes acknowledges Cratylus' point about his name. Socrates explains Pan's double form as embodying speech's duality: truth (smooth, divine, above) and falsehood (rough, goatish, below), connecting to tragedy's etymology from tragos (goat).
distilled: Pan's dual form -- smooth above, goatlike below -- embodies the dual nature of speech itself: divine truth above and rough mortal falsehood below.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Pan (from pan, all -- speech signifies all things), with etymology of tragedy (tragoidia from tragos, goat). Pan as Hermes' son connects speech (Hermes) to totality (Pan). The smooth/rough duality maps onto a vertical cosmology: divine truth above, mortal falsehood below. The tragedy-goat connection was an ancient etymology (goat-song). Cross-references: Republic (on mimesis and falsehood in poetry), Phaedrus (on Pan and the countryside), Symposium (on truth and appearance).

narrative: "Then surely Pan, who is the declarer of all things (pan) and the perpetual mover (aei polon) of all things, is rightly called aipolos (goat-herd), he being the two-formed son of Hermes, smooth in his upper part, and rough and goatlike in his lower regions. And, as the son of Hermes, he is speech or the brother of speech, and that brother should be like brother is no marvel. But, as I was saying, my dear Hermogenes, let us get away from the Gods."
refashioned:
Socrates completes the Pan etymology. Pan declares all things (pan) and perpetually moves all things (aei polon). He is therefore rightly called aipolos (goat-herd) -- the word combining his dual nature. As the two-formed son of Hermes, he is smooth in his upper part (representing truth) and rough and goatlike below (representing falsehood).

As Hermes' son, Pan is speech itself, or at least speech's brother. That brother should resemble brother is only natural.

With this, Socrates firmly insists on leaving the gods behind. The divine etymologies have been daring enough -- it's time to move to safer territory.
elegant: Pan declares all things (pan), perpetually moves them (aei polon), and is rightly called the goat-herd (aipolos) -- smooth truth above, rough falsehood below. As Hermes' son, he is speech's brother. Socrates insists on leaving the gods.
summary: Socrates concludes that Pan, who declares and moves all things, is rightly named the goat-herd (aipolos). His dual form represents truth and falsehood in speech. As Hermes' child, he is speech's sibling. Socrates then insists on leaving divine names behind.
distilled: Pan the goat-herd (aipolos) declares all things (pan) and perpetually moves them -- speech's brother, smooth truth above and rough falsehood below.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Pan (from pan, all things; aei polon, perpetually moving), Aipolos (goat-herd, combining Pan's dual nature). The Pan etymology completes the speech-related divine sequence: Hermes (inventor of speech) begets Pan (totality of speech). Socrates' desire to "get away from the Gods" is partly comic exhaustion, partly genuine piety about the limits of human inquiry into divine matters. Cross-references: Phaedrus 263d (Pan's prayer at the end), Laws (on Pan worship).

narrative:
"From these sort of Gods, by all means, Socrates. But why should we not discuss another kind of Gods -- the sun, moon, stars, earth, aether, air, fire, water, the seasons, and the year?"

"You impose a great many tasks upon me. Still, if you wish, I will not refuse."

"You will oblige me."

"How would you have me begin? Shall I take first of all him whom you mentioned first -- the sun?"

"Very good."

"The origin of the sun will probably be clearer in the Doric form, for the Dorians call him alios, and this name is given to him because when he rises he gathers (alizoi) men together or because he is always rolling in his course (aei eilein ion) about the earth; or from aiolein, of which the meaning is the same as poikillein (to variegate), because he variegates the productions of the earth."

"But what is selene (the moon)?"

"That name is rather unfortunate for Anaxagoras."

"How so?"

"The word seems to forestall his recent discovery, that the moon receives her light from the sun."

"Why do you say so?"

"The two words selas (brightness) and phos (light) have much the same meaning?"

"Yes."

"This light about the moon is always new (neon) and always old (enon), if the disciples of Anaxagoras say truly. For the sun in his revolution always adds new light, and there is the old light of the previous month."

"Very true."

"The moon is not unfrequently called selanaia."

"True."

"And as she has a light which is always old and always new (enon neon aei) she may very properly have the name selaenoneoaeia; and this when hammered into shape becomes selanaia."

"A real dithyrambic sort of name that, Socrates. But what do you say of the month and the stars?"

"Meis (month) is called from meiousthai (to lessen), because suffering diminution; the name of astra (stars) seems to be derived from astrape, which is an improvement on anastrope, signifying the upsetting of the eyes (anastrephein opa)."

"What do you say of pur (fire) and udor (water)?"

"I am at a loss how to explain pur; either the muse of Euthyphro has deserted me, or there is some very great difficulty in the word. Please, however, to note the contrivance which I adopt whenever I am in a difficulty of this sort."

"What is it?"
refashioned:
Hermogenes redirects from the Olympians to the cosmic gods -- sun, moon, stars, earth, aether, air, fire, water, seasons, and year. It's an enormous list, but Socrates obliges.

The sun (helios) is clearer in its Doric form, alios. Three possible etymologies: alizoi (gathering people together when it rises), aei eilein ion (always rolling in its course around the earth), or aiolein (to variegate -- the sun variegates everything the earth produces).

The moon (selene) has a name that inadvertently anticipates Anaxagoras' discovery that the moon receives its light from the sun. Since selas (brightness) and phos (light) are near-synonyms, and the moon's light is always simultaneously new (neon, from the current month's sun) and old (enon, from last month), her full name would be selaenoneoaeia -- "she of the old-and-new light." This tongue-twister was hammered down into selanaia. Hermogenes calls it a "dithyrambic" name.

The month (meis) comes from meiousthai (to lessen) -- the moon diminishes. Stars (astra) derive from astrape, itself from anastrope -- "upsetting of the eyes" (anastrephein opa), because stars make us look upward.

Fire (pur) stumps Socrates completely -- either Euthyphro's muse has deserted him or the word is genuinely intractable. He promises a special contrivance for such difficulties.
elegant: The sun gathers people (alizoi), rolls across the sky (aei eilein ion), or variegates earth's productions (aiolein). The moon has always old and new light (selaenoneoaeia, compressed to selanaia). Month means diminishing (meiousthai). Stars upset the eyes upward (anastrephein opa). Fire defeats Socrates entirely.
summary: Socrates etymologizes cosmic bodies: the sun from gathering, rolling, or variegating; the moon from its always old-and-new light; month from diminishing; stars from upsetting the eyes upward. Fire stumps him completely.
distilled: The sun gathers, rolls, and variegates; the moon carries always-old-and-new light; the month diminishes; stars upset the eyes upward -- but fire defeats even Socrates' etymological powers.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Helios/Alios (from alizoi -- gathering; aei eilein ion -- always rolling; aiolein -- variegating), Selene/Selanaia (from selas + enon + neon + aei -- brightness always old and new), Meis (from meiousthai, to diminish), Astra (from astrape/anastrope -- anastrephein opa, upsetting the eyes), Pur (fire -- unetymologized). The Anaxagoras reference dates the dialogue's dramatic setting -- his theory of lunar light was considered a recent discovery. The dithyrambic quality of selaenoneoaeia is a self-aware joke about the method's tendency toward baroque constructions. Cross-references: Anaxagoras (on lunar light), Timaeus (on celestial bodies), Apology 26d (on Anaxagoras' cosmology).

narrative:
"I will tell you; but I should like to know first whether you can tell me what is the meaning of the pur?"

"Indeed I cannot."

"Shall I tell you what I suspect to be the true explanation of this and several other words? -- My belief is that they are of foreign origin. For the Hellenes, especially those who were under the dominion of the barbarians, often borrowed from them."

"What is the inference?"

"Why, you know that any one who seeks to demonstrate the fitness of these names according to the Hellenic language, and not according to the language from which the words are derived, is rather likely to be at fault."

"Yes, certainly."

"Well then, consider whether this pur is not foreign; for the word is not easily brought into relation with the Hellenic tongue, and the Phrygians may be observed to have the same word slightly changed, just as they have udor (water) and kunes (dogs), and many other words."

"That is true."
refashioned:
Socrates reveals his escape hatch for difficult etymologies: foreign origin. When a Greek word resists analysis within the Greek language, it may simply be a loanword.

The Greeks, especially those who lived under foreign rule, frequently borrowed words from other languages. Anyone trying to demonstrate the natural fitness of such words using Greek etymology alone will inevitably fail -- you can't find Greek roots in a non-Greek word.

Fire (pur) is a prime example. It doesn't fit naturally into the Greek linguistic system. The Phrygians have the same word with slight modification, just as they share udor (water) and kunes (dogs) with Greek. These shared words likely indicate borrowing rather than independent Greek coinage.

This "foreign origin" device is methodologically important: it allows Socrates to maintain the theory that names have natural fitness while accounting for words that resist etymological analysis.
elegant: When a Greek word resists etymology, it may be a foreign loanword. Fire (pur) appears in Phrygian too, suggesting borrowing. You can't find Greek roots in non-Greek words. This device preserves the natural-fitness theory while accounting for recalcitrant cases.
summary: Socrates introduces the foreign-origin escape valve: words like pur (fire) that resist Greek etymology may be loanwords from Phrygian or other languages. This preserves the natural-fitness theory while explaining intractable cases.
distilled: Words that resist Greek etymology -- like pur (fire), shared with Phrygian -- may be foreign loanwords, a device that preserves the natural-fitness theory for otherwise intractable cases.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Hermogenes. Greek words discussed: Pur (fire), Udor (water), Kunes (dogs) -- all shared with Phrygian. The foreign-origin hypothesis is both a genuine linguistic insight (Indo-European cognates do exist) and a convenient escape when etymology fails. Phrygian was an Indo-European language spoken in Anatolia. The acknowledgment of linguistic borrowing shows sophisticated awareness of language contact. Cross-references: This device recurs at 2.54 and 2.65. The mention of Greeks under barbarian dominion may refer to Ionian Greeks under Persian rule.

narrative:
"Any violent interpretations of the words should be avoided; for something to say about them may easily be found. And thus I get rid of pur and udor. Aer (air), Hermogenes, may be explained as the element which raises (airei) things from the earth, or as ever flowing (aei rei), or because the flux of the air is wind, and the poets call the winds 'air-blasts,' (aetai); he who uses the term may mean, so to speak, air-flux (aetorroun), in the sense of wind-flux (pneumatorroun); and because this moving wind may be expressed by either term he employs the word air (aer = aetes rheo). Aither (aether) I should interpret as aeitheer; this may be correctly said, because this element is always running in a flux about the air (aei thei peri tou aera reon). The meaning of the word ge (earth) comes out better when in the form of gaia, for the earth may be truly called 'mother' (gaia, genneteira), as in the language of Homer (Od.) gegaasi means gegennesthai."

"Good."

"What shall we take next?"

"There are orai (the seasons), and the two names of the year, eniautos and etos."
refashioned:
Socrates moves on, having dispatched fire and water as foreign words. Violent or forced interpretations should be avoided.

Air (aer) receives three etymologies: it raises things from the earth (airei), it ever flows (aei rei), or its flux is wind -- the poets call winds aetai (air-blasts). The term might mean aetorroun (air-flux) or pneumatorroun (wind-flux). Since moving wind can be expressed either way, aer captures the essence.

Aether (aither) is aeitheer -- the element that is always running in flux around the air (aei thei peri tou aera reon). It's a perpetually circulating, higher element.

Earth (ge) is better understood in the form gaia, which connects to genneteira (mother, generator). Homer uses gegaasi to mean gegennesthai (to be born). Earth is the universal mother who gives birth to all things.

Hermogenes then asks about the seasons and the year.
elegant: Air (aer) raises things (airei), ever flows (aei rei), or is wind-flux (aetorroun). Aether always runs around the air (aeitheer). Earth (gaia) is the mother (genneteira) who generates all things. The elements are named for their essential motions.
summary: Socrates etymologizes air as raising/flowing/wind-flux, aether as perpetually running around the air, and earth (gaia) as the generating mother. Each element is named for its essential motion or function.
distilled: Air raises and flows (aer from airei/aei rei), aether perpetually circulates (aeitheer), and earth is the generating mother (gaia from genneteira).
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Aer (air -- from airei, to raise; aei rei, ever flowing; aetai/aetorroun, wind-flux), Aither (aether -- from aei thei, always running), Ge/Gaia (earth -- from genneteira, mother/generator; Homeric gegaasi = gegennesthai, to be born). The Heraclitean flux theme continues -- air and aether are defined by their constant motion. Earth as mother (Gaia/genneteira) connects to widespread Greek mythology. The Homeric reference is to Odyssey usage. Cross-references: Timaeus (on the elements), Hesiod's Theogony (Gaia as primordial mother), pre-Socratic cosmology.

narrative: "The orai should be spelt in the old Attic way, if you desire to know the probable truth about them; they are rightly called the orai because they divide (orizousin) the summers and winters and winds and the fruits of the earth. The words eniautos and etos appear to be the same, -- 'that which brings to light the plants and growths of the earth in their turn, and passes them in review within itself (en eauto exetazei)': this is broken up into two words, eniautos from en eauto, and etos from etazei, just as the original name of Zeus was divided into Zena and Dia; and the whole proposition means that his power of reviewing from within is one, but has two names, two words etos and eniautos being thus formed out of a single proposition."
refashioned:
The seasons (orai) get their name from orizousin -- to divide or mark boundaries. The seasons divide the summers and winters, winds and fruits of the earth into their proper periods. The old Attic spelling makes this derivation clearer.

The year has two names -- eniautos and etos -- which appear to be the same concept split into two words. Both derive from the idea of "that which brings to light the plants and growths of the earth in their turn, and passes them in review within itself" (en eauto exetazei). Eniautos comes from en eauto (within itself), while etos comes from etazei (examining/reviewing).

This is exactly like Zeus, whose single name was divided into Zena and Dia. The year's power of internal self-review is one, but it has two names -- two words formed from a single proposition.
elegant: Seasons (orai) divide (orizousin) the year's periods. The year's two names -- eniautos and etos -- split a single idea: "that which reviews all growths within itself." Eniautos from en eauto (within itself), etos from etazei (examining) -- one concept, two names, like Zeus becoming Zena and Dia.
summary: Socrates etymologizes the seasons (orai) from orizousin (to divide/bound), and the year's two names -- eniautos and etos -- as a single concept split in two: the power that reviews all earthly growth within itself.
distilled: Seasons divide (orizousin) the year's periods, while the year's two names -- eniautos (within itself) and etos (examining) -- express a single idea split in two, like Zeus into Zena and Dia.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Orai (seasons, from orizousin -- to divide/bound), Eniautos (year, from en eauto -- within itself), Etos (year, from etazei -- examining/reviewing). The parallel with Zeus' split name reinforces the principle that a single essence can be expressed through multiple phonetic forms. The concept of the year as self-reviewing nature connects to cyclical cosmology. Cross-references: Timaeus (on cosmic cycles), the Zeus etymology at 2.11.

narrative:
"Indeed, Socrates, you make surprising progress."

"I am run away with."

"Very true."

"But am not yet at my utmost speed."

"I should like very much to know, in the next place, how you would explain the virtues. What principle of correctness is there in those charming words -- wisdom, understanding, justice, and the rest of them?"

"That is a tremendous class of names which you are disinterring; still, as I have put on the lion's skin, I must not be faint of heart; and I suppose that I must consider the meaning of wisdom (phronesis) and understanding (sunesis), and judgment (gnome), and knowledge (episteme), and all those other charming words, as you call them?"

"Surely, we must not leave off until we find out their meaning."
refashioned:
Hermogenes marvels at Socrates' pace. Socrates says he's been carried away -- run off with by the etymological inspiration -- but hasn't reached top speed yet.

Hermogenes then asks the pivotal question: what about the virtues? What principle of correctness governs those charming words -- wisdom, understanding, justice, and the rest?

Socrates acknowledges this is a tremendous class of names to unearth. But having donned the lion's skin (the metaphor of Heracles' labor), he cannot be faint-hearted now. He must tackle phronesis (wisdom), sunesis (understanding), gnome (judgment), episteme (knowledge), and all the other noble terms.

Hermogenes insists they must press on until the meaning of each is found. This marks the transition from divine and cosmic names to the most philosophically important category: the names of virtues and concepts.
elegant: Hermogenes asks about the virtues -- wisdom, understanding, justice, knowledge. Socrates, having donned the lion's skin, cannot turn back. This marks the crucial transition from divine names to the philosophical vocabulary itself.
summary: Hermogenes requests etymologies of the virtues -- wisdom, understanding, justice, knowledge. Socrates accepts the challenge, comparing himself to Heracles donning the lion's skin. The investigation now shifts to philosophy's most important terms.
distilled: The investigation pivots from divine names to the virtues -- wisdom, understanding, justice, knowledge -- the most philosophically significant class of names.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. No specific etymologies yet -- this is a transitional passage. The lion's skin metaphor references Heracles (who wore the Nemean lion's skin), signaling that this next phase requires heroic intellectual effort. The list of virtues -- phronesis, sunesis, gnome, episteme -- maps onto key Platonic and Socratic concepts. The shift from mythological to philosophical vocabulary is the dialogue's most important turn. Cross-references: Republic (on the virtues), Meno (on knowledge and virtue), Theaetetus (on episteme).

narrative:
"By the dog of Egypt I have a not bad notion which came into my head only this moment: I believe that the primeval givers of names were undoubtedly like too many of our modern philosophers, who, in their search after the nature of things, are always getting dizzy from constantly going round and round, and then they imagine that the world is going round and round and moving in all directions; and this appearance, which arises out of their own internal condition, they suppose to be a reality of nature; they think that there is nothing stable or permanent, but only flux and motion, and that the world is always full of every sort of motion and change. The consideration of the names which I mentioned has led me into making this reflection."

"How is that, Socrates?"

"Perhaps you did not observe that in the names which have been just cited, the motion or flux or generation of things is most surely indicated."

"No, indeed, I never thought of it."

"Take the first of those which you mentioned; clearly that is a name indicative of motion."

"What was the name?"
refashioned:
Socrates swears by the dog of Egypt -- his characteristic oath -- and announces a sudden insight. The ancient name-givers were like many modern philosophers who, in their relentless search for the nature of things, spin themselves dizzy going round and round. In their vertigo, they project their own dizziness onto reality itself, imagining that the whole world is spinning and moving in every direction. What is actually an artifact of their own confused state, they mistake for the truth about nature. They conclude that nothing is stable or permanent -- only flux, motion, and change.

This realization has been triggered by the names of the virtues. Socrates notices that every name they've been analyzing encodes motion, flux, or generation. This is a startling meta-observation: the entire etymological framework so far has been Heraclitean, interpreting everything in terms of movement. Perhaps this says more about the name-givers' philosophical biases than about reality itself.
elegant: The ancient name-givers were Heracliteans -- they spun themselves dizzy and projected their vertigo onto reality, seeing flux everywhere. Every name encodes motion. This may reveal their philosophical bias more than the truth about nature.
summary: Socrates realizes the ancient name-givers were Heracliteans who projected their own intellectual dizziness onto reality, seeing flux everywhere. Every name analyzed so far encodes motion -- a pattern that may reflect philosophical bias rather than truth.
distilled: The ancient name-givers saw flux everywhere because they were Heraclitean philosophers dizzy from their own spinning -- their etymologies encode motion, which may reflect bias rather than reality.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. The oath "by the dog of Egypt" (a reference to the dog-headed god Anubis) is distinctively Socratic -- he uses it in several dialogues to avoid swearing by the Greek gods. This passage is a crucial meta-commentary: Socrates steps back from the etymological exercise to question its Heraclitean presuppositions. The image of philosophers getting dizzy from going round and round is both comic and philosophically pointed -- it anticipates the dialogue's eventual critique of the flux doctrine. Cross-references: Theaetetus 152-160 (on Heraclitean flux), Phaedo (critique of purely physical explanations), Republic (the allegory of prisoners mistaking shadows for reality).

narrative: "Phronesis (wisdom), which may signify phoras kai rhou noesis (perception of motion and flux), or perhaps phoras onesis (the blessing of motion), but is at any rate connected with pheresthai (motion); gnome (judgment), again, certainly implies the ponderation or consideration (nomesis) of generation, for to ponder is the same as to consider; or, if you would rather, here is noesis, the very word just now mentioned, which is neou esis (the desire of the new); the word neos implies that the world is always in process of creation. The giver of the name wanted to express this longing of the soul, for the original name was neoesis, and not noesis; but eta took the place of a double epsilon. The word sophrosune is the salvation (soteria) of that wisdom (phronesis) which we were just now considering. Epioteme (knowledge) is akin to this, and indicates that the soul which is good for anything follows (epetai) the motion of things, neither anticipating them nor falling behind them; wherefore the word should rather be read as epistemene, inserting epsilon nu. Sunesis (understanding) may be regarded in like manner as a kind of conclusion; the word is derived from sunienai (to go along with), and, like epistasthai (to know), implies the progression of the soul in company with the nature of things. Sophia (wisdom) is very dark, and appears not to be of native growth; the meaning is, touching the motion or stream of things. You must remember that the poets, when they speak of the commencement of any rapid motion, often use the word esuthe (he rushed); and there was a famous Lacedaemonian who was named Sous (Rush), for by this word the Lacedaemonians signify rapid motion, and the touching (epaphe) of motion is expressed by sophia, for all things are supposed to be in motion. Good (agathon) is the name which is given to the admirable (agasto) in nature; for, although all things move, still there are degrees of motion; some are swifter, some slower; but there are some things which are admirable for their swiftness, and this admirable part of nature is called agathon. Dikaiosune (justice) is clearly dikaiou sunesis (understanding of the just); but the actual word dikaion is more difficult: men are only agreed to a certain extent about justice, and then they begin to disagree."
refashioned:
Socrates launches into a rapid-fire series of etymologies for the intellectual virtues, all revealing Heraclitean flux:

Phronesis (wisdom) is phoras kai rhou noesis -- "perception of motion and flux" -- or phoras onesis, "the blessing of motion." Either way, it's connected to pheresthai (being carried along in motion).

Gnome (judgment) implies nomesis -- the consideration or pondering of generation. To judge is to consider what is coming into being.

Noesis (thinking) is really neoesis -- "desire of the new" (neou esis). The world is always in process of creation, and thinking is the soul's longing for what is perpetually new. The original form was neoesis, but eta replaced a double epsilon.

Sophrosune (temperance/moderation) is the salvation (soteria) of phronesis -- it preserves wisdom.

Episteme (knowledge) indicates that the worthy soul follows (epetai) the motion of things, neither running ahead nor falling behind. It should properly be epistemene.

Sunesis (understanding) is from sunienai -- "going along with." Understanding means the soul progressing in company with the nature of things.

Sophia (wisdom in the deeper sense) is obscure and possibly foreign. It means "touching the motion or stream of things." The poets use esuthe ("he rushed") for rapid motion, and there was a Spartan named Sous ("Rush"). Sophia expresses the touching (epaphe) of motion.

Agathon (good) is from agasto -- admirable. Among all things in motion, some move more swiftly than others; the admirably swift part of nature is called good.

Dikaiosune (justice) is clearly dikaiou sunesis -- "understanding of the just." But the word dikaion itself is harder -- people agree about justice only to a point before disagreement begins.
elegant: Every intellectual virtue encodes motion: phronesis perceives flux, gnome ponders generation, noesis desires the new, episteme follows motion, sunesis progresses alongside nature, sophia touches the stream of things. Even "good" means admirably swift. All virtue names are secretly Heraclitean.
summary: Socrates etymologizes the intellectual virtues as encoding Heraclitean flux: wisdom perceives motion, judgment ponders generation, thinking desires the new, knowledge follows motion, understanding progresses with nature, and even goodness means admirable swiftness.
distilled: Every virtue name encodes Heraclitean flux -- wisdom perceives motion, knowledge follows it, understanding progresses with it, and goodness is admirable swiftness.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Phronesis (phoras noesis/onesis -- perception/blessing of motion), Gnome (nomesis -- considering generation), Noesis (neoesis -- desire of the new), Sophrosune (soteria phroneseos -- salvation of wisdom), Episteme (epetai -- following motion; read as epistemene), Sunesis (sunienai -- going along with), Sophia (touching motion; connected to esuthe/Sous -- rushing), Agathon (agasto -- admirable in swiftness), Dikaiosune (dikaiou sunesis -- understanding of the just). This is the densest etymological passage in the dialogue, connecting every philosophical term to motion. The meta-argument from 2.45 is confirmed: the name-givers were thoroughgoing Heracliteans. Cross-references: Theaetetus (on flux), Republic (on the virtues), Meno (on the nature of virtue).

narrative: "For those who suppose all things to be in motion conceive the greater part of nature to be a mere receptacle; and they say that there is a penetrating power which passes through all this, and is the instrument of creation in all, and is the subtlest and swiftest element; for if it were not the subtlest, and a power which none can keep out, and also the swiftest, passing by other things as if they were standing still, it could not penetrate through the moving universe. And this element, which superintends all things and pierces (diaion) all, is rightly called dikaion; the letter k is only added for the sake of euphony. Thus far, as I was saying, there is a general agreement about the nature of justice; but I, Hermogenes, being an enthusiastic disciple, have been told in a mystery that the justice of which I am speaking is also the cause of the world: now a cause is that because of which anything is created; and some one comes and whispers in my ear that justice is rightly so called because partaking of the nature of the cause, and I begin, after hearing what he has said, to interrogate him gently: 'Well, my excellent friend,' say I, 'but if all this be true, I still want to know what is justice.' Thereupon they think that I ask tiresome questions, and am leaping over the barriers, and have been already sufficiently answered, and they try to satisfy me with one derivation after another, and at length they quarrel."
refashioned:
Socrates explains the deeper etymology of dikaion (the just). For Heraclitean thinkers, most of nature is a mere receptacle through which a penetrating power passes -- the subtlest, swiftest creative force. It must be the subtlest (nothing can block it) and the swiftest (passing through everything else as if it stood still) to penetrate the entire moving universe. This all-piercing, all-superintending element is called dikaion -- from diaion (piercing through). The letter kappa was added merely for euphony.

But Socrates goes further. He reports being told in confidence that justice is also the cause of the world -- the creative principle (aitia) that makes everything exist. Justice is rightly named because it partakes of the nature of causation itself.

Socrates then describes the characteristic frustration of Socratic questioning: after hearing such answers, he gently asks, "But what IS justice?" His interlocutors think he's being tiresome, leaping over barriers, and already sufficiently answered. They pile on derivation after derivation, and eventually quarrel among themselves.

This is vintage Socratic method: etymological explanations, however clever, don't answer the fundamental philosophical question.
elegant: Justice (dikaion) is the all-piercing element (diaion) -- the subtlest, swiftest force that penetrates all moving things. Some say it is the world's very cause. But when Socrates asks "What IS justice?," people grow impatient, pile on derivations, and quarrel. Etymology doesn't answer the real question.
summary: Socrates etymologizes dikaion (just) as the all-piercing element (diaion) that penetrates the moving universe. Some identify it with causation itself. But Socrates notes that when he asks what justice actually IS, his questioners grow impatient -- etymological derivations don't answer the philosophical question.
distilled: Justice (dikaion) is the all-piercing force (diaion) penetrating the moving universe, perhaps even the world's cause -- but etymology still cannot answer what justice fundamentally IS.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Dikaion (just, from diaion -- piercing through all things; kappa added for euphony). This passage is philosophically pivotal. Socrates embeds a critique of his own etymological method: even if we determine what the name-givers MEANT by "justice," we still haven't determined what justice IS. The "mysterious" informant and the quarreling interlocutors satirize competing philosophical schools. Cross-references: Republic (the central question "What is justice?"), Gorgias (on justice and power), Euthyphro (the frustration of Socratic questioning), Theaetetus (the nature of knowledge).

narrative:
"For one of them says that justice is the sun, and that he only is the piercing (diaionta) and burning (kaonta) element which is the guardian of nature. And when I joyfully repeat this beautiful notion, I am answered by the satirical remark, 'What, is there no justice in the world when the sun is down?' And when I earnestly beg my questioner to tell me his own honest opinion, he says, 'Fire in the abstract'; but this is not very intelligible. Another says, 'No, not fire in the abstract, but the abstraction of heat in the fire.' Another man professes to laugh at all this, and says, as Anaxagoras says, that justice is mind, for mind, as they say, has absolute power, and mixes with nothing, and orders all things, and passes through all things. At last, my friend, I find myself in far greater perplexity about the nature of justice than I was before I began to learn. But still I am of opinion that the name, which has led me into this digression, was given to justice for the reasons which I have mentioned."

"I think, Socrates, that you are not improvising now; you must have heard this from some one else."

"And not the rest?"

"Hardly."
refashioned:
Socrates recounts the bewildering variety of opinions about justice's nature. One philosopher says justice is the sun -- the piercing (diaionta) and burning (kaonta) element that guards nature. When Socrates joyfully repeats this, he's met with the sarcastic objection: "Is there no justice when the sun goes down?" Pressed for his own view, the critic says "fire in the abstract" -- not very helpful. Another refines this to "the abstraction of heat in fire." Yet another laughs at all of them and, following Anaxagoras, declares that justice is mind -- mind has absolute power, mixes with nothing, orders everything, and passes through everything.

Socrates confesses he ends up more confused about justice than before he started asking. Still, he maintains that the name dikaion was given for the reasons he described -- the all-penetrating principle.

Hermogenes suspects Socrates didn't improvise this part but heard it from someone else. Socrates asks if the same applies to everything else. "Hardly," Hermogenes replies -- implying the rest was indeed Socrates' own invention.
elegant: Justice has been identified with the sun, fire, abstract heat, and Anaxagoras' mind -- each theory refuted or mocked. Socrates ends more perplexed than before. Etymological derivations proliferate but the fundamental question remains unanswered.
summary: Socrates describes competing theories of justice -- the sun, fire, heat, and Anaxagoras' mind -- each criticized in turn. He ends more perplexed than before, illustrating that etymology cannot resolve fundamental philosophical questions. Hermogenes suspects this section was not improvised.
distilled: Competing identifications of justice with sun, fire, heat, and mind each fail -- Socrates ends more confused than before, proving that etymological derivation cannot substitute for philosophical understanding.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes responding. This passage surveys pre-Socratic theories of justice as a cosmic principle: solar theology, Heraclitean fire, and Anaxagorean nous. Each is met with objection. The Anaxagoras reference (mind ordering all things) reprises a theme from 2.21. Hermogenes' suspicion that Socrates isn't improvising is dramatically interesting -- it suggests real philosophical debates Socrates has witnessed. Cross-references: Phaedo 97-99 (Socrates on Anaxagoras), Republic (the sun analogy for the Good), Heraclitus fragments (on fire and logos).

narrative: "Well, then, let me go on in the hope of making you believe in the originality of the rest. What remains after justice? I do not think that we have as yet discussed courage (andreia), -- injustice (adikia), which is obviously nothing more than a hindrance to the penetrating principle (diaiontos), need not be considered. Well, then, the name of andreia seems to imply a battle; -- this battle is in the world of existence, and according to the doctrine of flux is only the counterflux (enantia rhon): if you extract the delta from andreia, the name at once signifies the thing, and you may clearly understand that andreia is not the stream opposed to every stream, but only to that which is contrary to justice, for otherwise courage would not have been praised. The words arren (male) and aner (man) also contain a similar allusion to the same principle of the upward flux (te ano rhon). Gune (woman) I suspect to be the same word as goun (birth): thelu (female) appears to be partly derived from thele (the teat), because the teat is like rain, and makes things flourish (tethelenai)."
refashioned:
Socrates moves to courage (andreia), noting that injustice (adikia) needs no separate treatment -- it's simply the hindrance of the penetrating principle (diaiontos) already discussed.

Andreia implies a battle -- specifically, the counterflux (enantia rhon) within the world of flux. If you remove the delta from andreia, you get anreia, which reveals the word's meaning: the stream (rhon) that flows against (enantia) unjust currents. Crucially, courage is not opposition to every stream, but only to what opposes justice. Otherwise courage wouldn't be praised -- blind opposition to everything would be mere stubbornness.

Arren (male) and aner (man) encode the upward flux (ano rhon) -- the masculine principle is the stream that flows upward.

Gune (woman) is related to goun (birth) -- the feminine principle is generative. And thelu (female) derives from thele (the teat, nipple), because the teat is like rain, making things flourish (tethelenai). The female principle nourishes and causes growth.
elegant: Courage (andreia) is the counterflux -- the stream opposing injustice, not all streams. Male (arren/aner) encodes the upward flux. Woman (gune) relates to birth (goun), and female (thelu) to the teat (thele) that nourishes growth. Gender names encode cosmic principles of opposition and generation.
summary: Socrates etymologizes courage as the counterflux opposing injustice, male/man as upward flux, woman as birth, and female as the nourishing teat. Each gendered term encodes a principle within the Heraclitean system of flux.
distilled: Courage (andreia) is the counterflux opposing injustice; male (arren) is upward flux; woman (gune) is birth; female (thelu) is the nourishing teat that makes things flourish.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Andreia (courage -- from enantia rhon, counterflux; delta removed reveals anreia), Adikia (injustice -- hindrance of diaiontos), Arren/Aner (male/man -- from ano rhon, upward flux), Gune (woman -- from goun, birth/generation), Thelu (female -- from thele, teat; tethelenai, to flourish). The distinction between opposing all streams and opposing only unjust ones is philosophically important -- true courage requires discrimination. The gendered etymologies encode cosmological principles: masculine as active/upward, feminine as generative/nourishing. Cross-references: Republic (on courage as one of the four virtues), Laches (on the nature of courage), Symposium (on masculine and feminine principles).

narrative:
"That is surely probable."

"Yes; and the very word thallein (to flourish) seems to figure the growth of youth, which is swift and sudden ever. And this is expressed by the legislator in the name, which is a compound of thein (running), and allesthai (leaping). Pray observe how I gallop away when I get on smooth ground. There are a good many names generally thought to be of importance, which have still to be explained."

"True."

"There is the meaning of the word techne (art), for example."

"Very true."

"That may be identified with echonoe, and expresses the possession of mind: you have only to take away the tau and insert two omichrons, one between the chi and nu, and another between the nu and eta."

"That is a very shabby etymology."
refashioned:
Socrates continues at full gallop. The word thallein (to flourish) captures the swift, sudden growth of youth. The legislator composed it from thein (running) and allesthai (leaping) -- youth's growth is a running leap.

Socrates notices himself accelerating: "See how I gallop when I hit smooth ground!" There are still many important names to explain.

He takes up techne (art/craft/skill). Its etymology: techne can be identified with echonoe -- "possession of mind." Remove the tau, insert two omicrons (one between chi and nu, another between nu and eta), and the word reveals its meaning: art is the having (echein) of nous (mind).

Hermogenes is unimpressed: "That is a very shabby etymology." The letter manipulation is too violent -- too many insertions and deletions to be convincing. This objection will prompt Socrates' defense of etymological liberties.
elegant: Thallein (flourishing) combines running (thein) and leaping (allesthai) -- youth's swift growth. Techne (art) is echonoe -- "possession of mind." But Hermogenes protests: the letter manipulation is too free. This is a shabby etymology.
summary: Socrates derives flourishing (thallein) from running and leaping, and art (techne) from echonoe (possession of mind). Hermogenes objects that the techne etymology requires too many letter changes, calling it shabby.
distilled: Flourishing (thallein) is running and leaping combined; art (techne) is possession of mind (echonoe) -- but Hermogenes protests the etymology as too liberal with letter changes.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Thallein (to flourish -- from thein + allesthai, running + leaping), Techne (art -- from echonoe, having mind; echein + nous). Hermogenes' protest ("shabby etymology") is dramatically important -- it raises the methodological objection that has been building throughout. If you can freely add, remove, and rearrange letters, any word can mean anything. This challenge forces Socrates to address the limits of etymological analysis. Cross-references: Ion (on techne and inspiration), Gorgias (on rhetoric as a techne), Phaedrus (on dialectic as the true techne).

narrative: "Yes, my dear friend; but then you know that the original names have been long ago buried and disguised by people sticking on and stripping off letters for the sake of euphony, and twisting and bedizening them in all sorts of ways: and time too may have had a share in the change. Take, for example, the word katoptron; why is the letter rho inserted? This must surely be the addition of some one who cares nothing about the truth, but thinks only of putting the mouth into shape. And the additions are often such that at last no human being can possibly make out the original meaning of the word. Another example is the word sphigx, sphiggos, which ought properly to be phigx, phiggos, and there are other examples."
refashioned:
Socrates defends his method against Hermogenes' objection. Original names, he argues, have been buried and disguised over time. People stuck on extra letters and stripped others off for the sake of euphony -- making words sound pleasant rather than preserving their meaning. They twisted and ornamented words in every way imaginable. Time itself has contributed to the corruption.

Consider katoptron (mirror): why is the rho inserted? Someone who cared nothing for truth and only wanted the word to feel right in the mouth added it. Such additions accumulate until no human being can recover the original meaning.

Or take sphigx (sphinx): the original form should be phigx, phiggos, but letters have been added to dress it up.

The methodological point is clear: the etymologist must work backward through layers of phonetic corruption to recover buried meaning. The fact that letters have changed doesn't prove the meaning was never there -- it proves the difficulty of the recovery.
elegant: Original names have been buried by centuries of phonetic corruption -- letters added for euphony, stripped for convenience, twisted by time. Katoptron has a needless rho; sphigx should be phigx. The etymologist must dig through layers of corruption to recover buried meaning.
summary: Socrates defends etymological liberties by arguing that original names have been corrupted through euphonic additions, letter-stripping, and time. Katoptron and sphigx show how words accumulate meaningless phonetic material that obscures their origins.
distilled: Original word-forms have been buried under centuries of euphonic additions and phonetic corruption -- the etymologist must dig through these layers to recover the meaning beneath.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words discussed: Katoptron (mirror -- rho inserted for euphony), Sphigx/Sphinx (should be phigx -- sigma added). This is Socrates' defense of his etymological method against the charge of arbitrariness. The argument -- that words undergo phonetic corruption over time -- is actually linguistically sound (modern historical linguistics confirms this). However, Socrates may be using a valid principle to justify invalid specific etymologies. The tension between methodological soundness and specific application runs throughout. Cross-references: This defense responds to Hermogenes' objection at 2.50 and anticipates the further methodological discussion at 2.52.

narrative:
"That is quite true, Socrates."

"And yet, if you are permitted to put in and pull out any letters which you please, names will be too easily made, and any name may be adapted to any object."

"True."

"Yes, that is true. And therefore a wise dictator, like yourself, should observe the laws of moderation and probability."

"Such is my desire."
refashioned:
Hermogenes accepts Socrates' point about phonetic corruption but presses the methodological concern. If you're free to insert and remove whatever letters you want, names become too easy to construct -- you could adapt any name to mean anything about any object.

Socrates agrees this is a real danger. A wise practitioner must observe the laws of moderation and probability. You can't just rearrange letters at will; the changes must be plausible, the derivations moderate rather than forced.

This is a crucial moment of self-regulation in the etymological method. Both speakers acknowledge the tension between necessary phonetic reconstruction and arbitrary manipulation. The line between the two is judgment, not algorithm.
elegant: If any letters can be freely added or removed, any name can mean anything. The wise etymologist must observe moderation and probability -- plausible reconstruction, not arbitrary manipulation.
summary: Hermogenes warns that unrestricted letter manipulation lets any name mean anything. Socrates agrees: the etymologist must observe moderation and probability, maintaining plausible rather than forced derivations.
distilled: Unrestricted letter manipulation would make any name adaptable to any meaning -- the etymologist must observe moderation and probability.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this is a methodological interlude. The principle of "moderation and probability" (metriotes kai eikos) is Socrates' answer to the arbitrariness objection. It admits that the method has no strict rules but relies on judgment. This is both a strength (acknowledging complexity) and a weakness (providing no clear criterion for distinguishing good from bad etymologies). Cross-references: Phaedrus (on probability in rhetoric), Republic (on moderation as a virtue).

narrative: "And mine, too, Hermogenes. But do not be too much of a precisian, or 'you will unnerve me of my strength (Iliad.).' When you have allowed me to add mechane (contrivance) to techne (art) I shall be at the top of my bent, for I conceive mechane to be a sign of great accomplishment -- anein; for mekos has the meaning of greatness, and these two, mekos and anein, make up the word mechane. But, as I was saying, being now at the top of my bent, I should like to consider the meaning of the two words arete (virtue) and kakia (vice); arete I do not as yet understand, but kakia is transparent, and agrees with the principles which preceded, for all things being in a flux (ionton), kakia is kakos ion (going badly); and this evil motion when existing in the soul has the general name of kakia, or vice, specially appropriated to it. The meaning of kakos ienai may be further illustrated by the use of deilia (cowardice), which ought to have come after andreia, but was forgotten, and, as I fear, is not the only word which has been passed over. Deilia signifies that the soul is bound with a strong chain (desmos), for lian means strength, and therefore deilia expresses the greatest and strongest bond of the soul; and aporia (difficulty) is an evil of the same nature (from a (alpha) not, and poreuesthai to go), like anything else which is an impediment to motion and movement. Then the word kakia appears to mean kakos ienai, or going badly, or limping and halting; of which the consequence is, that the soul becomes filled with vice. And if kakia is the name of this sort of thing, arete will be the opposite of it, signifying in the first place ease of motion, then that the stream of the good soul is unimpeded, and has therefore the attribute of ever flowing without let or hindrance, and is therefore called arete, or, more correctly, aeireite (ever-flowing), and may perhaps have had another form, airete (eligible), indicating that nothing is more eligible than virtue, and this has been hammered into arete. I daresay that you will deem this to be another invention of mine, but I think that if the previous word kakia was right, then arete is also right."
refashioned:
Socrates asks for indulgence -- too much precision will sap his strength, as Homer warns. He quickly derives mechane (contrivance) from mekos (greatness) and anein (accomplishing) -- contrivance means great accomplishment.

Now at full power, he tackles the great pair: arete (virtue) and kakia (vice).

Kakia is transparent within the flux framework: it means kakos ion -- "going badly." When the soul moves badly, limping and halting, this evil motion is called vice. Cowardice (deilia) illustrates the same principle: from desmos (chain) and lian (strength), it means the soul is bound by the strongest possible chain. Aporia (difficulty/perplexity) similarly means "not going" -- alpha (not) plus poreuesthai (to go), an impediment to motion.

Arete is kakia's opposite. If vice is impeded motion, virtue is unimpeded flow. The good soul streams freely without hindrance. Arete is properly aeireite -- "ever-flowing" -- or perhaps airete ("eligible"), since nothing is more worthy of choice than virtue. These forms were hammered over time into the familiar arete.

Socrates anticipates the objection that this is his own invention, but argues that if the kakia etymology is correct, the arete etymology must follow logically.
elegant: Vice (kakia) is kakos ion -- going badly, the soul limping and halted. Cowardice (deilia) is the soul's strongest chain. Difficulty (aporia) is inability to go. Virtue (arete) is the opposite: aeireite, ever-flowing -- the good soul streaming without impediment. Contrivance (mechane) means great accomplishment.
summary: Socrates etymologizes vice (kakia) as going badly, cowardice (deilia) as the soul's strongest chain, difficulty (aporia) as inability to move, and virtue (arete) as ever-flowing unimpeded motion. Vice and virtue are defined as impeded and free motion of the soul.
distilled: Vice (kakia) is the soul going badly; virtue (arete) is the soul flowing freely -- cowardice chains the soul, difficulty blocks it, but virtue is ever-flowing, unimpeded motion.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Mechane (from mekos + anein, great accomplishment), Kakia (vice -- kakos ion, going badly), Deilia (cowardice -- from desmos + lian, strongest chain of the soul), Aporia (difficulty -- a + poreuesthai, not going), Arete (virtue -- from aeireite, ever-flowing, or airete, eligible/choiceworthy). The Iliad quotation signals Socrates' awareness of pushing limits. The vice/virtue pair as impeded/free motion is the climax of the Heraclitean etymological system. The aporia etymology is ironic -- Socratic method deliberately produces aporia. Cross-references: Meno (on virtue and aporia), Republic (on virtue as the soul's proper function), Laches (on courage).

narrative:
"But what is the meaning of kakon, which has played so great a part in your previous discourse?"

"That is a very singular word about which I can hardly form an opinion, and therefore I must have recourse to my ingenious device."

"What device?"

"The device of a foreign origin, which I shall give to this word also."

"Very likely you are right; but suppose that we leave these words and endeavour to see the rationale of kalon and aischron."

"The meaning of aischron is evident, being only aei ischon roes (always preventing from flowing), and this is in accordance with our former derivations. For the name-giver was a great enemy to stagnation of all sorts, and hence he gave the name aeischoroun to that which hindered the flux (aei ischon roun), and that is now beaten together into aischron."

"But what do you say of kalon?"

"That is more obscure; yet the form is only due to the quantity, and has been changed by altering omicron upsilon into omicron."

"What do you mean?"

"This name appears to denote mind."

"How so?"

"Let me ask you what is the cause why anything has a name; is not the principle which imposes the name the cause?"

"Certainly."

"And must not this be the mind of Gods, or of men, or of both?"

"Yes."

"Is not mind that which called (kalesan) things by their names, and is not mind the beautiful (kalon)?"

"That is evident."

"And are not the works of intelligence and mind worthy of praise, and are not other works worthy of blame?"

"Certainly."

"Physic does the work of a physician, and carpentering does the works of a carpenter?"

"Exactly."

"And the principle of beauty does the works of beauty?"

"Of course."

"And that principle we affirm to be mind?"

"Very true."

"Then mind is rightly called beauty because she does the works which we recognize and speak of as the beautiful?"

"That is evident."

"What more names remain to us?"

"There are the words which are connected with agathon and kalon, such as sumpheron and lusiteloun, ophelimon, kerdaleon, and their opposites."
refashioned:
Hermogenes asks about kakon (bad/evil), which has appeared constantly. Socrates admits defeat -- he can't form an opinion about this word and must use his foreign-origin escape hatch.

They turn instead to kalon (beautiful) and aischron (ugly/shameful). Aischron is straightforward within the flux system: aei ischon roes -- "always preventing from flowing." The name-giver despised stagnation, so he called whatever hindered the flux aeischoroun, which was beaten down into aischron. Ugliness and shame are stagnation.

Kalon is harder but ultimately connects to mind itself. Through a series of Socratic questions, the argument unfolds: What causes things to have names? The principle that imposes names -- which is mind (nous). Mind is what called (kalesan) things by their names. And is not mind the beautiful (kalon)? The works of intelligence are praiseworthy; the works of non-intelligence deserve blame. Mind produces beauty; therefore mind is rightly called beauty because it does the works we recognize as beautiful.

Hermogenes asks what remains. The answer: words connected to goodness and beauty -- sumpheron (expedient), lusiteloun (profitable), ophelimon (advantageous), kerdaleon (gainful), and their opposites.
elegant: Kakon is foreign and intractable. Aischron means "always preventing flow" -- ugliness is stagnation. Kalon (beauty) derives from kalesan (calling/naming) -- mind calls things by their names, and mind IS the beautiful. Beauty is the work of intelligence.
summary: Socrates declares kakon foreign, derives aischron (ugly) from perpetual hindrance of flow, and connects kalon (beautiful) to mind through kalesan (naming). Mind names all things and produces beauty; therefore beauty and intelligence are one.
distilled: Ugliness (aischron) is perpetual stagnation; beauty (kalon) is mind itself -- the naming principle (kalesan) that calls all things and produces everything praiseworthy.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Kakon (evil -- declared foreign), Aischron (ugly/shameful -- from aei ischon roes, always hindering flow), Kalon (beautiful -- from kalesan, to call/name; identified with nous, mind). The identification of beauty with mind is profoundly Platonic -- it anticipates the Symposium's ascent from physical beauty to the Form of Beauty, and the Republic's identification of the Good with intelligibility. The foreign-origin device for kakon (2.41's strategy) is applied again. Cross-references: Symposium 210-212 (ascent to Beauty), Republic (the Good and intelligibility), Hippias Major (what is the beautiful?).

narrative:
"The meaning of sumpheron (expedient) I think that you may discover for yourself by the light of the previous examples, -- for it is a sister word to episteme, meaning just the motion (pora) of the soul accompanying the world, and things which are done upon this principle are called sumphora or sumpheronta, because they are carried round with the world."

"That is probable."

"Again, cherdaleon (gainful) is called from cherdos (gain), but you must alter the delta into nu if you want to get at the meaning; for this word also signifies good, but in another way; he who gave the name intended to express the power of admixture (kerannumenon) and universal penetration in the good; in forming the word, however, he inserted a delta instead of a nu, and so made kerdos."

"Well, but what is lusiteloun (profitable)?"
refashioned:
Socrates handles several economic and practical terms quickly.

Sumpheron (expedient/beneficial) is a sister word to episteme. It means the motion of the soul accompanying the world. Things done on this principle are called sumphora or sumpheronta because they are carried round (sumpheresthai) with the world's motion. What is expedient is what moves in harmony with the cosmic flux.

Kerdaleon (gainful) comes from kerdos (gain), but you need to change the delta to a nu to see the real meaning. The original intent was to express the power of admixture (kerannumenon) and universal penetration -- the good mingles with and penetrates everything. The name-giver inserted a delta instead of a nu, producing kerdos.

Hermogenes asks about lusiteloun (profitable).
elegant: Sumpheron (expedient) means the soul's motion accompanying the world. Kerdaleon (gainful) really expresses universal penetration and admixture of the good (kerannumenon) -- delta was substituted for nu. The practical vocabulary encodes cosmic flux.
summary: Socrates etymologizes sumpheron (expedient) as the soul's motion accompanying the world, and kerdaleon (gainful) as expressing the universal penetration and admixture of the good, with a delta substituted for the original nu.
distilled: The expedient (sumpheron) is what moves with the world; the gainful (kerdaleon) expresses the good's universal penetration and admixture (kerannumenon).
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Hermogenes. Greek words etymologized: Sumpheron (expedient -- from sumpheresthai, being carried along with), Kerdaleon/Kerdos (gainful/gain -- from kerannumenon, being mixed/penetrating, with delta for nu). The economic vocabulary is absorbed into the Heraclitean framework: what is profitable or expedient is what moves in harmony with the cosmic flux. The kerannumenon (mixing/penetrating) etymology for gain echoes the penetrating principle of justice (dikaion as diaion). Cross-references: Republic (on the expedient and the just), Protagoras (on the profitable and the good).

narrative: "I suppose, Hermogenes, that people do not mean by the profitable the gainful or that which pays (luei) the retailer, but they use the word in the sense of swift. You regard the profitable (lusiteloun), as that which being the swiftest thing in existence, allows of no stay in things and no pause or end of motion, but always, if there begins to be any end, lets things go again (luei), and makes motion immortal and unceasing: and in this point of view, as appears to me, the good is happily denominated lusiteloun -- being that which looses (luon) the end (telos) of motion. Ophelimon (the advantageous) is derived from ophellein, meaning that which creates and increases; this latter is a common Homeric word, and has a foreign character."
refashioned:
Socrates corrects a common misunderstanding. The profitable (lusiteloun) doesn't mean what pays (luei) the retailer in the commercial sense. In the philosophical sense, lusiteloun describes the swiftest thing in existence -- that which permits no stasis, no pause, no end to motion. Whenever motion threatens to stop, the profitable loosens (luei) the endpoint (telos), releasing things back into motion. It makes motion immortal and unceasing.

So lusiteloun means luon telos -- "loosing the end of motion." What is profitable prevents the cessation of flux.

Ophelimon (advantageous) derives from ophellein -- to create and increase. This is a common Homeric word with a foreign character, suggesting it was borrowed from another language.

Both terms, like everything in this section, are reinterpreted through the Heraclitean lens: profit and advantage are defined by their relationship to perpetual motion.
elegant: The profitable (lusiteloun) loosens the end (telos) of motion -- it prevents flux from stopping, making motion immortal. The advantageous (ophelimon) creates and increases (ophellein). Both terms encode the Heraclitean imperative of perpetual motion.
summary: Socrates derives the profitable (lusiteloun) from loosening the end of motion (luon telos), preventing flux from ceasing. The advantageous (ophelimon) from ophellein means creating and increasing. Both are Heraclitean in spirit.
distilled: The profitable (lusiteloun) loosens the end of motion, making flux perpetual; the advantageous (ophelimon) creates and increases -- both encode the imperative of ceaseless motion.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Lusiteloun (profitable -- from luon + telos, loosening the end of motion), Ophelimon (advantageous -- from ophellein, to create/increase, a Homeric and possibly foreign word). The lusiteloun etymology is characteristically Heraclitean -- even commercial vocabulary is reinterpreted as cosmic physics. The ophellein connection to Homer suggests awareness of archaic vocabulary layers. Cross-references: Homer (ophellein as creation/increase), the general Heraclitean framework established in 2.45-2.46.

narrative:
