"And what do you say of their opposites?"

"Of such as are mere negatives I hardly think that I need speak."

"Which are they?"

"The words axumphoron (inexpedient), anopheles (unprofitable), alusiteles (unadvantageous), akerdes (ungainful)."

"True."

"I would rather take the words blaberon (harmful), zemiodes (hurtful)."

"Good."

"The word blaberon is that which is said to hinder or harm (blaptein) the stream (roun); blapton is boulomenon aptein (seeking to hold or bind); for aptein is the same as dein, and dein is always a term of censure; boulomenon aptein roun (wanting to bind the stream) would properly be boulapteroun, and this, as I imagine, is improved into blaberon."

"You bring out curious results, Socrates, in the use of names; and when I hear the word boulapteroun I cannot help imagining that you are making your mouth into a flute, and puffing away at some prelude to Athene."

"That is the fault of the makers of the name, Hermogenes; not mine."

"Very true; but what is the derivation of zemiodes?"
refashioned:
Hermogenes asks about the opposites of the beneficial terms. Socrates dismisses the simple negatives -- axumphoron (inexpedient), anopheles (unprofitable), alusiteles (unadvantageous), akerdes (ungainful) -- as mere privative prefixes that need no explanation.

Instead he tackles blaberon (harmful) and zemiodes (hurtful). Blaberon derives from blaptein (to hinder or harm the stream). Breaking it down further: blapton means boulomenon aptein -- "wanting to bind or hold." Since aptein equals dein (to bind), and binding is always negative, the full form would be boulapteroun -- "wanting to bind the stream." This was polished into blaberon.

Hermogenes can't help laughing. When he hears the unwieldy boulapteroun, he imagines Socrates puffing into a flute, playing some prelude to Athena. Socrates deflects: that's the fault of the original name-makers, not his.

Hermogenes then asks about zemiodes.
elegant: Harmful (blaberon) means "wanting to bind the stream" (boulomenon aptein roun) -- compressed from boulapteroun. Simple negatives like "inexpedient" need no analysis. Hermogenes jokes that the uncompressed form sounds like flute-playing.
summary: Socrates derives harmful (blaberon) from boulomenon aptein roun -- wanting to bind the stream -- compressed from the unwieldy boulapteroun. Hermogenes humorously compares the original form to flute-playing. Simple negative terms are dismissed as mere prefixes.
distilled: Harmful (blaberon) means "wanting to bind the stream" (boulapteroun compressed) -- while simple negatives like inexpedient are mere prefixes needing no analysis.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Blaberon (harmful -- from boulomenon aptein roun, wanting to bind the stream; compressed from boulapteroun), plus the negatives axumphoron, anopheles, alusiteles, akerdes (dismissed as simple alpha-privative formations). Hermogenes' flute joke provides comic relief and implicitly critiques the method's tendency toward baroque reconstructions. The distinction between mere negatives and substantive opposites shows methodological discrimination. Cross-references: The binding/flow opposition echoes the vice/virtue pair (2.53) and anticipates the discussion of deon (2.58-2.60).

narrative: "What is the meaning of zemiodes? -- let me remark, Hermogenes, how right I was in saying that great changes are made in the meaning of words by putting in and pulling out letters; even a very slight permutation will sometimes give an entirely opposite sense; I may instance the word deon, which occurs to me at the moment, and reminds me of what I was going to say to you, that the fine fashionable language of modern times has twisted and disguised and entirely altered the original meaning both of deon, and also of zemiodes, which in the old language is clearly indicated."
refashioned:
Socrates pauses before answering about zemiodes (hurtful) to make a methodological point. He reminds Hermogenes how right he was about the transformative power of letter changes -- even a very slight permutation can reverse a word's meaning entirely.

The word deon (duty/obligation) comes to mind as a perfect illustration. Modern fashionable Greek has twisted, disguised, and completely altered the original meanings of both deon and zemiodes. In the ancient language, their meanings were transparent.

This sets up a crucial argument: contemporary language can be misleading because phonetic changes have obscured original meanings. The etymologist must look past modern forms to recover ancient clarity. Sometimes what a word means NOW is the opposite of what it originally meant.
elegant: Even slight letter changes can reverse a word's meaning. Modern Greek has disguised the original senses of deon (obligation) and zemiodes (hurtful). The etymologist must look past contemporary corruption to recover ancient clarity.
summary: Socrates emphasizes that small letter changes can invert meaning, and modern language has disguised the original senses of words like deon and zemiodes. Ancient forms reveal what modern usage conceals.
distilled: Even slight letter permutations can reverse a word's meaning -- modern language has disguised the original senses of deon and zemiodes, which were transparent in the ancient tongue.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words discussed: Deon (obligation/duty) and Zemiodes (hurtful) -- both flagged as having modern meanings that differ from their original senses. This passage introduces the concept of semantic inversion through phonetic change, which will be fully developed in 2.59-2.60. The complaint about "fine fashionable language" reflects a conservative linguistic attitude found elsewhere in Plato. Cross-references: This theme of linguistic corruption connects to the complaint about euphony over truth at 2.30 and 2.51.

narrative:
"What do you mean?"

"I will try to explain. You are aware that our forefathers loved the sounds iota and delta, especially the women, who are most conservative of the ancient language, but now they change iota into eta or epsilon, and delta into zeta; this is supposed to increase the grandeur of the sound."

"How do you mean?"

"For example, in very ancient times they called the day either imera or emera (short e), which is called by us emera (long e)."

"That is true."

"Do you observe that only the ancient form shows the intention of the giver of the name? of which the reason is, that men long for (imeirousi) and love the light which comes after the darkness, and is therefore called imera, from imeros, desire."

"Clearly."

"But now the name is so travestied that you cannot tell the meaning, although there are some who imagine the day to be called emera because it makes things gentle (emera different accents)."

"Such is my view."

"And do you know that the ancients said duogon and not zugon?"

"They did so."

"And zugon (yoke) has no meaning, -- it ought to be duogon, which word expresses the binding of two together (duein agoge) for the purpose of drawing; -- this has been changed into zugon, and there are many other examples of similar changes."

"There are."

"Proceeding in the same train of thought I may remark that the word deon (obligation) has a meaning which is the opposite of all the other appellations of good; for deon is here a species of good, and is, nevertheless, the chain (desmos) or hinderer of motion, and therefore own brother of blaberon."

"Yes, Socrates; that is quite plain."
refashioned:
Socrates explains the mechanics of linguistic change. The ancient Greeks loved the sounds iota and delta -- especially women, who are the most conservative preservers of archaic language. Modern speakers have replaced iota with eta or epsilon, and delta with zeta, supposedly to make words sound grander.

Example: the ancients called the day imera (from imeros, desire/longing), because people long for and love the light that follows darkness. The name beautifully encoded its meaning. But modern Greek changed it to hemera (with a long eta), disguising the etymology. Some now imagine hemera means "gentle" (from a different word hemera with different accentuation), but this is a false modern interpretation.

Another example: the ancient word duogon (yoke) transparently means "drawing two together" (duein agoge). Modern Greek changed it to zugon, which has no discernible meaning at all.

This brings Socrates to deon (obligation). In its modern form, deon appears paradoxical: it's classified as a good thing, yet its meaning is actually a chain (desmos) or hinderer of motion -- making it the brother of blaberon (harmful). Obligation binds and restricts. How can a species of the good be a form of binding?
elegant: Ancient imera (day, from imeros, desire for light) became hemera, losing its meaning. Ancient duogon (yoke, binding two together) became meaningless zugon. Similarly, deon (obligation) paradoxically combines goodness with binding -- it chains motion, making it the brother of harm.
summary: Socrates shows how phonetic shifts (iota to eta, delta to zeta) corrupted meanings: imera (desire for light) became hemera, duogon (binding two) became zugon. This reveals that deon (obligation) paradoxically combines goodness with binding, making it kin to the harmful.
distilled: Ancient words like imera (desire for light) and duogon (binding two together) were corrupted into hemera and zugon -- and deon (obligation) paradoxically combines goodness with the binding that opposes flux.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Imera/Hemera (day -- from imeros, desire/longing for light), Duogon/Zugon (yoke -- from duein agoge, drawing two together), Deon (obligation -- connected to desmos, chain/binding). The observation about women preserving archaic language is a genuine sociolinguistic insight -- many cultures show conservative female speech patterns. The sound changes described (iota to eta, delta to zeta) are historically attested in Greek dialectal development. The deon paradox (obligation as both good and binding) sets up the resolution in 2.60. Cross-references: Republic (on obligation and justice), Laws (on linguistic conservatism).

narrative:
"Not if you restore the ancient form, which is more likely to be the correct one, and read dion instead of deon; if you convert the epsilon into an iota after the old fashion, this word will then agree with other words meaning good; for dion, not deon, signifies the good, and is a term of praise; and the author of names has not contradicted himself, but in all these various appellations, deon (obligatory), ophelimon (advantageous), lusiteloun (profitable), kerdaleon (gainful), agathon (good), sumpheron (expedient), euporon (plenteous), the same conception is implied of the ordering or all-pervading principle which is praised, and the restraining and binding principle which is censured. And this is further illustrated by the word zemiodes (hurtful), which if the zeta is only changed into delta as in the ancient language, becomes demiodes; and this name, as you will perceive, is given to that which binds motion (dounti ion)."

"What do you say of edone (pleasure), lupe (pain), epithumia (desire), and the like, Socrates?"
refashioned:
The paradox dissolves when you restore the ancient form. Read dion instead of deon -- convert the epsilon back to an iota in the old way. Dion harmonizes perfectly with all the other words meaning good: it signifies the good and is a term of praise, not of binding.

The original name-giver was consistent throughout. In every case -- deon (obligation, properly dion), ophelimon (advantageous), lusiteloun (profitable), kerdaleon (gainful), agathon (good), sumpheron (expedient), euporon (plenteous) -- the same conception runs through: praise for the ordering, all-pervading principle of motion, and censure for the restraining, binding principle that opposes it.

Zemiodes (hurtful) confirms this. Change the zeta back to delta (restoring the ancient form) and it becomes demiodes -- "that which binds motion" (dounti ion). The hurtful is whatever binds and stops the flow.

With this, the entire Heraclitean vocabulary of good and evil resolves into a single principle: what promotes flux is good; what binds and restrains it is bad.

Hermogenes then asks about the emotions: pleasure, pain, desire.
elegant: Restore deon to its ancient form dion and the paradox vanishes -- it means good, not binding. Zemiodes becomes demiodes (binding motion). The entire moral vocabulary resolves: what promotes flux is praised; what binds it is censured. One principle, many names.
summary: Socrates resolves the deon paradox by restoring the ancient form dion (good), and reveals zemiodes as demiodes (binding motion). The entire moral vocabulary -- good, profitable, expedient -- praises motion, while harmful, hurtful terms censure binding.
distilled: Restore deon to ancient dion and it means good, not binding -- the entire moral vocabulary praises motion and censures stagnation: one Heraclitean principle, many names.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Deon/Dion (obligation/good -- ancient dion with iota = good; modern deon with epsilon = binding/chain), Zemiodes/Demiodes (hurtful -- from dounti ion, binding motion; zeta restored to delta). This passage completes the systematic Heraclitean moral vocabulary: every term of praise encodes the flowing principle, every term of censure encodes the binding principle. The resolution of deon's apparent contradiction through phonetic restoration is one of Socrates' most elegant etymological moves. Cross-references: The general Heraclitean framework (2.45-2.46), Republic (on the good as an ordering principle).

narrative:
"I do not think, Hermogenes, that there is any great difficulty about them -- edone is e (eta) onesis, the action which tends to advantage; and the original form may be supposed to have been eone, but this has been altered by the insertion of the delta. Lupe appears to be derived from the relaxation (luein) which the body feels when in sorrow; ania (trouble) is the hindrance of motion (alpha and ienai); algedon (distress), if I am not mistaken, is a foreign word, which is derived from aleinos (grievous); odune (grief) is called from the putting on (endusis) sorrow; in achthedon (vexation) 'the word too labours,' as any one may see; chara (joy) is the very expression of the fluency and diffusion of the soul (cheo); terpsis (delight) is so called from the pleasure creeping (erpon) through the soul, which may be likened to a breath (pnoe) and is properly erpnoun, but has been altered by time into terpnon; eupherosune (cheerfulness) and epithumia explain themselves; the former, which ought to be eupherosune and has been changed euphrosune, is named, as every one may see, from the soul moving (pheresthai) in harmony with nature; epithumia is really e epi ton thumon iousa dunamis, the power which enters into the soul; thumos (passion) is called from the rushing (thuseos) and boiling of the soul; imeros (desire) denotes the stream (rous) which most draws the soul dia ten esin tes roes -- because flowing with desire (iemenos), and expresses a longing after things and violent attraction of the soul to them, and is termed imeros from possessing this power; pothos (longing) is expressive of the desire of that which is not present but absent, and in another place (pou); this is the reason why the name pothos is applied to things absent, as imeros is to things present; eros (love) is so called because flowing in (esron) from without; the stream is not inherent, but is an influence introduced through the eyes, and from flowing in was called esros (influx) in the old time when they used omicron for omega, and is called eros, now that omega is substituted for omicron. But why do you not give me another word?"

"What do you think of doxa (opinion), and that class of words?"
refashioned:
Socrates tackles emotions and desires in rapid succession, finding no great difficulty:

Pleasure (hedone) is he onesis -- the action tending toward advantage. Originally eone, the delta was inserted later.

Pain (lupe) comes from luein -- the body's relaxation or loosening in sorrow.

Trouble (ania) is the hindrance of motion (alpha privative + ienai, to go).

Distress (algedon) is probably foreign, from aleinos (grievous).

Grief (odune) comes from endusis -- the putting on of sorrow, like donning a garment.

Vexation (achthedon) -- "the word itself labors," as anyone can hear in its heavy sound.

Joy (chara) expresses the fluency and diffusion of the soul (from cheo, to pour/flow).

Delight (terpsis) comes from pleasure creeping (erpon) through the soul like a breath (pnoe) -- originally erpnoun, altered to terpnon.

Cheerfulness (euphrosune, properly eupherosune) names the soul moving (pheresthai) in harmony with nature.

Desire (epithumia) is the power (dunamis) that enters into (epi) the thumos (soul/spirit).

Passion (thumos) comes from the rushing (thuseos) and boiling of the soul.

Desire/yearning (himeros) denotes the stream (rous) that most draws the soul, flowing with longing (hiemenos).

Longing (pothos) is for what is absent -- in another place (pou). Pothos applies to absent things; himeros to present ones.

Love (eros) was anciently esros -- something flowing in from outside, an influence entering through the eyes. When omicron was replaced by omega, esros became eros.

Hermogenes asks about doxa (opinion).
elegant: The emotions all encode flux: pleasure tends toward advantage, pain is loosening, joy is the soul's fluency, desire enters the soul as a power, passion boils, yearning is a stream, longing reaches toward the absent, and love flows in through the eyes from without. Every feeling is motion.
summary: Socrates etymologizes the entire emotional vocabulary as variations of flux: pleasure as advantage-seeking, pain as loosening, joy as flowing, desire as entering power, passion as boiling, yearning as a drawing stream, longing as reaching toward the absent, and love as an external stream flowing in through the eyes.
distilled: Every emotion is motion: pleasure seeks advantage, pain loosens, joy flows, passion boils, yearning is a stream, and love flows in from outside through the eyes.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Hedone (pleasure -- he onesis), Lupe (pain -- luein, loosening), Ania (trouble -- a + ienai, not going), Algedon (distress -- aleinos, grievous; foreign), Odune (grief -- endusis, putting on), Achthedon (vexation -- onomatopoeic heaviness), Chara (joy -- cheo, pouring/flowing), Terpsis (delight -- erpon + pnoe, creeping breath), Euphrosune (cheerfulness -- pheresthai, soul moving with nature), Epithumia (desire -- epi + thumos, power entering spirit), Thumos (passion -- thuseos, rushing/boiling), Himeros (yearning -- hiemenos + rous, longing stream), Pothos (longing -- pou, absent/elsewhere), Eros (love -- esros, flowing in from outside through the eyes). This is the most comprehensive single etymological passage, covering the entire emotional vocabulary. The eros etymology -- love as external influx through the eyes -- anticipates Phaedrus' theory of erotic vision. Cross-references: Phaedrus 251-252 (love entering through the eyes), Symposium (on eros), Republic (on thumos as the spirited part of the soul).

narrative:
"Doxa is either derived from dioxis (pursuit), and expresses the march of the soul in the pursuit of knowledge, or from the shooting of a bow (toxon); the latter is more likely, and is confirmed by oiesis (thinking), which is only oisis (moving), and implies the movement of the soul to the essential nature of each thing -- just as boule (counsel) has to do with shooting (bole); and boulesthai (to wish) combines the notion of aiming and deliberating -- all these words seem to follow doxa, and all involve the idea of shooting, just as aboulia, absence of counsel, on the other hand, is a mishap, or missing, or mistaking of the mark, or aim, or proposal, or object."

"You are quickening your pace now, Socrates."
refashioned:
Opinion (doxa) has two possible derivations: from dioxis (pursuit) -- the soul's march in pursuit of knowledge -- or from toxon (bow-shooting). Socrates prefers the archery metaphor.

This reading is confirmed by related words. Oiesis (thinking) is really oisis (moving), implying the soul's movement toward the essential nature of each thing -- thinking is the soul's arrow aimed at truth. Boule (counsel/deliberation) connects to bole (a shot/throw). Boulesthai (to wish) combines aiming and deliberating -- wishing is taking aim.

All these words follow from doxa and involve the idea of shooting: opinion, thinking, counsel, and wishing are all forms of aiming at a target. Conversely, aboulia (absence of counsel) is a mishap -- missing the mark, mistaking the aim, failing to hit the target.

The archery metaphor for thinking is striking: opinion is an arrow shot at truth, and bad judgment is simply missing the target.

Hermogenes notes that Socrates is picking up speed.
elegant: Opinion (doxa) is archery -- the soul shooting at truth. Thinking (oiesis) is movement toward essence. Counsel (boule) is a shot (bole). Wishing is taking aim. Folly (aboulia) is missing the mark. All cognition is aiming and shooting.
summary: Socrates connects doxa (opinion) to archery: thinking is the soul shooting at truth, counsel is a shot, wishing is aiming, and folly is missing the mark. All cognitive terms encode the metaphor of aiming at a target.
distilled: Opinion (doxa) is the soul's archery -- thinking aims at truth, counsel is a shot, wishing is taking aim, and folly is missing the mark.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words etymologized: Doxa (opinion -- from dioxis, pursuit, or toxon, bow-shooting), Oiesis (thinking -- from oisis, moving), Boule (counsel -- from bole, a shot), Boulesthai (to wish -- aiming and deliberating), Aboulia (folly -- missing the mark). The archery metaphor for cognition is philosophically rich -- it anticipates Aristotle's metaphor of hamartia (error/sin as missing the mark). The soul shooting at truth combines the Heraclitean motion theme with a new directional emphasis: motion toward a target. Cross-references: Republic (on opinion vs. knowledge), Theaetetus (on doxa), Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics (hamartia).

narrative:
"Why yes, the end I now dedicate to God, not, however, until I have explained anagke (necessity), which ought to come next, and ekousion (the voluntary). Ekousion is certainly the yielding (eikon) and unresisting -- the notion implied is yielding and not opposing, yielding, as I was just now saying, to that motion which is in accordance with our will; but the necessary and resistant being contrary to our will, implies error and ignorance; the idea is taken from walking through a ravine which is impassable, and rugged, and overgrown, and impedes motion -- and this is the derivation of the word anagkaion (necessary) an agke ion, going through a ravine. But while my strength lasts let us persevere, and I hope that you will persevere with your questions."

"Well, then, let me ask about the greatest and noblest, such as aletheia (truth) and pseudos (falsehood) and on (being), not forgetting to enquire why the word onoma (name), which is the theme of our discussion, has this name of onoma."

"You know the word maiesthai (to seek)?"

"Yes; -- meaning the same as zetein (to enquire)."
refashioned:
Socrates dedicates the final stretch to God but has two more terms first.

The voluntary (ekousion) means yielding (eikon) without resistance -- going along with motion that accords with our will. It implies consent and non-opposition to natural flow.

Necessity (anagke/anagkaion) is the opposite: resistance, contrariety to will, implying error and ignorance. The image is of walking through a ravine (agke) -- impassable, rugged, overgrown, impeding motion. Anagkaion means an agke ion -- "going through a ravine." Necessity is the experience of being forced through difficult terrain against your will.

Socrates promises to persevere while his strength lasts. Hermogenes then asks the biggest questions: truth (aletheia), falsehood (pseudos), being (on), and -- most fitting for this dialogue's theme -- the word name (onoma) itself.

Socrates begins with a preliminary question: do you know maiesthai (to seek), which means the same as zetein (to inquire)?
elegant: The voluntary (ekousion) is yielding to natural motion. Necessity (anagkaion) is going through a ravine (an agke ion) -- forced passage through rugged, impassable terrain. Now come the greatest questions: truth, falsehood, being, and name itself.
summary: Socrates etymologizes the voluntary as yielding to natural motion and necessity as going through a ravine -- forced, difficult passage. Hermogenes then asks for the greatest terms: truth, falsehood, being, and the word name itself.
distilled: The voluntary (ekousion) yields to natural flow; necessity (anagkaion) is forced passage through a ravine -- and now come the ultimate questions: truth, falsehood, being, and name.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes transitioning. Greek words etymologized: Ekousion (voluntary -- from eikon, yielding), Anagkaion/Anagke (necessity -- from an agke ion, going through a ravine). The ravine metaphor for necessity is vivid and philosophically suggestive -- necessity is experienced as constraint, not as logical entailment. The transition to aletheia, pseudos, on, and onoma marks the climax of Part 2's etymological investigation. Cross-references: Republic (on necessity and the Allegory of the Cave), Timaeus (on ananke as cosmic principle), Phaedo (on philosophical inquiry).

narrative:
"The word onoma seems to be a compressed sentence, signifying on ou zetema (being for which there is a search); as is still more obvious in onomaston (notable), which states in so many words that real existence is that for which there is a seeking (on ou masma); aletheia is also an agglomeration of theia ale (divine wandering), implying the divine motion of existence; pseudos (falsehood) is the opposite of motion; here is another ill name given by the legislator to stagnation and forced inaction, which he compares to sleep (eudein); but the original meaning of the word is disguised by the addition of psi; on and ousia are ion with an iota broken off; this agrees with the true principle, for being (on) is also moving (ion), and the same may be said of not being, which is likewise called not going (oukion or ouki on = ouk ion)."

"You have hammered away at them manfully; but suppose that some one were to say to you, what is the word ion, and what are reon and doun? -- show me their fitness."

"You mean to say, how should I answer him?"

"Yes."

"One way of giving the appearance of an answer has been already suggested."

"What way?"
refashioned:
Socrates reaches the summit -- the words that define the investigation itself.

Onoma (name) is a compressed sentence: on hou zetema -- "being for which there is a search." This is even clearer in onomaston (notable/famous), which transparently states that real existence (on) is what is sought (masma). A name is the search for being.

Aletheia (truth) is an agglomeration of theia ale -- "divine wandering." Truth is the divine motion of existence itself.

Pseudos (falsehood) is the opposite of motion -- another ill name the legislator gave to stagnation and forced inaction, comparing it to sleep (eudein). The addition of psi disguises the original meaning.

On (being) and ousia (essence) are really ion with an iota broken off. Being (on) IS going (ion). Not-being is likewise not-going (ouk ion = oukion).

This is the deepest level of the Heraclitean system: being itself is motion. To exist is to move.

Hermogenes presses further: what about the words ion (going), reon (flowing), and doun (binding) themselves? Can their fitness be shown? This challenges Socrates to etymologize his own foundational terms.
elegant: Name (onoma) is "being for which there is a search." Truth (aletheia) is "divine wandering." Falsehood (pseudos) is stagnation compared to sleep. Being (on) IS going (ion). At the deepest level: existence is motion, and naming is the search for being.
summary: Socrates etymologizes the foundational terms: name (onoma) as the search for being, truth (aletheia) as divine wandering, falsehood (pseudos) as stagnation, and being (on) as going (ion). Existence itself is motion. Hermogenes then challenges him to explain the basic terms of motion themselves.
distilled: Name is the search for being (on hou zetema); truth is divine wandering (theia ale); falsehood is stagnation; and being IS going (on = ion) -- existence itself is motion.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes pressing further. Greek words etymologized: Onoma (name -- on hou zetema, being for which there is search), Onomaston (notable -- on hou masma, being which is sought), Aletheia (truth -- theia ale, divine wandering), Pseudos (falsehood -- stagnation/sleep, from eudein; psi added as disguise), On/Ousia (being/essence -- from ion, going; iota broken off). The onoma etymology is the dialogue's most self-referential moment: the theme of the entire dialogue (names) is itself etymologized as the search for being. The equation on = ion (being = going) is the purest expression of the Heraclitean interpretation. Cross-references: Parmenides (on being and non-being), Theaetetus 152-160 (flux theory), Sophist (on being and non-being).

narrative: "To say that names which we do not understand are of foreign origin; and this is very likely the right answer, and something of this kind may be true of them; but also the original forms of words may have been lost in the lapse of ages; names have been so twisted in all manner of ways, that I should not be surprised if the old language when compared with that now in use would appear to us to be a barbarous tongue."
refashioned:
Socrates offers two explanations for words that resist further analysis. First, the foreign-origin escape: words we can't understand in Greek may have come from other languages, and this may genuinely be the case.

Second, and more interesting: the original forms of words may have been lost entirely through the passage of time. Names have been so thoroughly twisted, corrupted, and transformed over the ages that the ancient language, if we could hear it, might sound to modern Greek ears like a barbarous tongue -- completely foreign and incomprehensible.

This is a remarkably modern insight: languages change so drastically over centuries that ancestral forms become unrecognizable. The etymologist eventually hits bedrock -- a point where no further analysis is possible, not because meaning doesn't exist, but because the traces have been erased by time.
elegant: Intractable words may be foreign or may have been so corrupted by time that the ancient language would sound barbarous to modern ears. The etymologist eventually hits bedrock where time has erased all traces.
summary: Socrates acknowledges that some words resist analysis either because they're foreign or because time has corrupted them beyond recognition. The ancient language might sound completely barbarous to modern speakers.
distilled: Some words resist etymology because they're foreign or because time has corrupted them beyond recognition -- the ancient language might sound barbarous to modern ears.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this is a methodological concession. The acknowledgment that ancient language might be unrecognizable anticipates modern historical linguistics' discovery that proto-languages differ dramatically from their descendants. The two escape routes (foreign origin and temporal corruption) set honest limits on the etymological project. Cross-references: This reprises the foreign-origin device from 2.41 and 2.54, and the temporal corruption theme from 2.51 and 2.58-2.59.

narrative:
"Very likely."

"Yes, very likely. But still the enquiry demands our earnest attention and we must not flinch. For we should remember, that if a person go on analysing names into words, and enquiring also into the elements out of which the words are formed, and keeps on always repeating this process, he who has to answer him must at last give up the enquiry in despair."

"Very true."
refashioned:
Despite the limitations, Socrates insists they must not flinch from the inquiry. But he acknowledges a fundamental problem with recursive analysis: if you keep breaking names into words, and words into their component elements, and those elements into further elements, the process is infinite. Eventually the person trying to answer must give up in despair.

This is the regress problem of analysis: every explanation requires further explanation, and the chain of decomposition never ends on its own. There must be some point where analysis reaches irreducible elements -- primary names that cannot be broken down further. This problem will be addressed in the next entry.
elegant: The inquiry must continue despite its limits. But recursive analysis creates an infinite regress -- breaking names into words into elements ad infinitum. Eventually the analyst must give up. There must be irreducible primary elements.
summary: Socrates acknowledges the regress problem: endlessly analyzing names into words into elements produces despair. There must be irreducible primary elements where analysis stops.
distilled: Recursive analysis creates an infinite regress -- names broken into words into elements endlessly -- demanding the existence of irreducible primary elements where analysis must stop.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this addresses the theoretical problem of infinite regress in analysis. The issue is fundamental to Platonic epistemology: if knowledge requires giving an account (logos), and every account requires further accounts, when do we reach foundations? This connects to the discussion of primary elements (stoicheia) in Theaetetus. Cross-references: Theaetetus 201-202 (the dream theory of primary elements), Sophist (on division and analysis), Meno (on the regress of inquiry).

narrative: "And at what point ought he to lose heart and give up the enquiry? Must he not stop when he comes to the names which are the elements of all other names and sentences; for these cannot be supposed to be made up of other names? The word agathon (good), for example, is, as we were saying, a compound of agastos (admirable) and thoos (swift). And probably thoos is made up of other elements, and these again of others. But if we take a word which is incapable of further resolution, then we shall be right in saying that we have at last reached a primary element, which need not be resolved any further."
refashioned:
Socrates proposes a solution to the regress: analysis should stop when you reach the primary names -- the elemental building blocks from which all other names and sentences are composed. These primary names cannot themselves be made of other names.

Take agathon (good): it's a compound of agastos (admirable) and thoos (swift). Thoos is probably composed of still other elements, and those of further ones. But at some point you reach a word incapable of further resolution -- a truly primary element. At that point, you've hit bottom and need not decompose any further.

The question then becomes: how do we evaluate the correctness of these irreducible primary names? Compound names derive their meaning from their components, but primary names have no components. They must derive their fitness from some other principle entirely.
elegant: Analysis stops at primary names -- elements that cannot be further decomposed. Agathon breaks into agastos and thoos, which break further, but eventually you reach irreducible atoms of language. Their correctness requires a new principle.
summary: Socrates identifies the stopping point for analysis: primary names that cannot be decomposed further. Like atoms in physics, these linguistic elements form the foundation from which all compound names are built, but they require a different principle for evaluating their correctness.
distilled: Analysis stops at primary names -- irreducible linguistic atoms from which all compound names are built, requiring a new principle to evaluate their correctness.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek words discussed: Agathon (good -- compound of agastos + thoos), Thoos (swift -- further decomposable). This passage introduces the concept of stoicheia (primary elements) in language, paralleling the atomistic theories in physics. The distinction between compound and primary names maps onto the distinction between derived and foundational knowledge. The question of how to evaluate primary elements will lead to the theory of sound-symbolism (2.75-2.76). Cross-references: Theaetetus 201e-202c (dream theory of elements), Timaeus (on physical elements), Sophist (on combining forms).

narrative:
"I believe you to be in the right."

"And suppose the names about which you are now asking should turn out to be primary elements, must not their truth or law be examined according to some new method?"

"Very likely."

"Quite so, Hermogenes; all that has preceded would lead to this conclusion. And if, as I think, the conclusion is true, then I shall again say to you, come and help me, that I may not fall into some absurdity in stating the principle of primary names."

"Let me hear, and I will do my best to assist you."

"I think that you will acknowledge with me, that one principle is applicable to all names, primary as well as secondary -- when they are regarded simply as names, there is no difference in them."

"Certainly not."

"All the names that we have been explaining were intended to indicate the nature of things."

"Of course."

"And that this is true of the primary quite as much as of the secondary names, is implied in their being names."

"Surely."

"But the secondary, as I conceive, derive their significance from the primary."

"That is evident."

"Very good; but then how do the primary names which precede analysis show the natures of things, as far as they can be shown; which they must do, if they are to be real names? And here I will ask you a question: Suppose that we had no voice or tongue, and wanted to communicate with one another, should we not, like the deaf and dumb, make signs with the hands and head and the rest of the body?"

"There would be no choice, Socrates."

"We should imitate the nature of the thing; the elevation of our hands to heaven would mean lightness and upwardness; heaviness and downwardness would be expressed by letting them drop to the ground; if we were describing the running of a horse, or any other animal, we should make our bodies and their gestures as like as we could to them."

"I do not see that we could do anything else."

"We could not; for by bodily imitation only can the body ever express anything."

"Very true."

"And when we want to express ourselves, either with the voice, or tongue, or mouth, the expression is simply their imitation of that which we want to express."

"It must be so, I think."
refashioned:
Hermogenes agrees that primary names require a new evaluative method. Socrates asks for his help navigating this territory.

First, a shared principle: all names -- primary and secondary alike -- are intended to indicate the nature of things. This is what makes them names. Secondary names derive significance from primary ones. But how do primary names, which have no components to derive from, indicate natures?

Socrates introduces the concept of imitation through a thought experiment. If we had no voice or tongue and needed to communicate, we would make bodily signs -- like deaf people do. We would imitate the nature of things: raising hands to heaven for lightness and upwardness, dropping them for heaviness, mimicking a horse's gait to describe running.

The body can only express things through imitation. And the same principle applies to voice and speech: when we use voice, tongue, and mouth to express something, the expression is simply vocal imitation of what we want to convey.

This is the breakthrough: if primary names can't derive meaning from components, they must derive it from direct imitation -- the sounds themselves must somehow resemble the natures they name.
elegant: Primary names can't derive meaning from components, so they must imitate directly. Like bodily gesture imitates nature (hands raised for lightness, dropped for heaviness), vocal sounds must imitate the natures of things. Primary names are sound-pictures.
summary: Socrates argues that since primary names have no components, they must indicate natures through direct vocal imitation -- just as bodily gestures imitate what they express. This lays the groundwork for sound-symbolism: primary names are sonic imitations of essences.
distilled: Primary names work through direct imitation -- just as gestures imitate what they express (raised hands for lightness), vocal sounds must imitate the natures they name.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this is the theoretical foundation for sound-symbolism. The gesture analogy (raising hands for lightness, etc.) anticipates modern studies of iconic gesture in sign languages. The principle that primary names imitate essences through sound is the dialogue's most original linguistic theory. It will be developed into specific claims about individual letters (2.75-2.76). Cross-references: Republic (mimesis in art), Sophist (on images and imitation), Phaedrus (on writing as imitation of speech).

narrative:
"Then a name is a vocal imitation of that which the vocal imitator names or imitates?"

"I think so."

"Nay, my friend, I am disposed to think that we have not reached the truth as yet."

"Why not?"

"Because if we have we shall be obliged to admit that the people who imitate sheep, or cocks, or other animals, name that which they imitate."

"Quite true."

"Then could I have been right in what I was saying?"

"In my opinion, no. But I wish that you would tell me, Socrates, what sort of an imitation is a name?"

"In the first place, I should reply, not a musical imitation, although that is also vocal; nor, again, an imitation of what music imitates; these, in my judgment, would not be naming. Let me put the matter as follows: All objects have sound and figure, and many have colour?"

"Certainly."

"But the art of naming appears not to be concerned with imitations of this kind; the arts which have to do with them are music and drawing?"

"True."

"Again, is there not an essence of each thing, just as there is a colour, or sound? And is there not an essence of colour and sound as well as of anything else which may be said to have an essence?"

"I should think so."

"Well, and if any one could express the essence of each thing in letters and syllables, would he not express the nature of each thing?"

"Quite so."

"The musician and the painter were the two names which you gave to the two other imitators. What will this imitator be called?"

"I imagine, Socrates, that he must be the namer, or name-giver, of whom we are in search."

"If this is true, then I think that we are in a condition to consider the names ron (stream), ienai (to go), schesis (retention), about which you were asking; and we may see whether the namer has grasped the nature of them in letters and syllables in such a manner as to imitate the essence or not."

"Very good."

"But are these the only primary names, or are there others?"

"There must be others."
refashioned:
Socrates immediately qualifies his imitation theory. If names are simply vocal imitations, then anyone who imitates a sheep's bleating or a cock's crowing would be naming those animals -- which is absurd. So what kind of imitation IS a name?

Not musical imitation, which also uses voice but imitates sounds and rhythms. Not drawing, which imitates visual appearances. These arts deal with the sound, figure, and color of things. But naming deals with something deeper.

Every thing has an essence -- just as it has color and sound. And essences themselves have essences. If someone could express the essence of each thing in letters and syllables, he would express the nature of that thing. This person is neither musician nor painter but the namer -- the name-giver.

The namer imitates not the sound or appearance of things but their essential nature, using letters and syllables as his medium. Just as the musician works with tones and the painter with colors, the namer works with phonetic elements to create representations of essences.

With this principle established, they can examine primary names like rhon (stream), ienai (to go), and schesis (retention) to see whether the namer successfully captured their essences in sound.
elegant: Names don't imitate sounds or appearances -- imitating a sheep's cry doesn't name it. Names imitate essences, using letters and syllables as their medium. The namer is a third kind of artist alongside the musician and painter, expressing the nature of things through sound.
summary: Socrates refines the imitation theory: names don't imitate sounds or appearances (that's music and painting) but essences. The namer is a distinct artist who expresses the nature of things in letters and syllables, imitating not what things look or sound like but what they fundamentally are.
distilled: Names imitate not sounds or appearances but essences -- the namer is a unique artist who expresses the nature of things through letters and syllables.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Hermogenes. Greek words mentioned: Rhon (stream), Ienai (to go), Schesis (retention) -- cited as primary names to be analyzed. The distinction between imitating sounds/appearances and imitating essences is philosophically crucial. It parallels the Republic's distinction between art that copies appearances and philosophy that grasps reality. The namer as a third type of artist (alongside musician and painter) positions language-making as a fundamental human activity. Cross-references: Republic 596-598 (on mimesis and levels of imitation), Sophist (on images), Phaedrus (on the art of rhetoric).

narrative:
"So I should expect. But how shall we further analyse them, and where does the imitator begin? Imitation of the essence is made by syllables and letters; ought we not, therefore, first to separate the letters, just as those who are beginning rhythm first distinguish the powers of elementary, and then of compound sounds, and when they have done so, but not before, they proceed to the consideration of rhythms?"

"Yes."
refashioned:
Socrates asks: how should we analyze primary names further, and where does the imitative process begin? Since imitation of essence is accomplished through syllables and letters, they should start by separating and classifying the individual letters -- just as students of rhythm first learn to distinguish elementary sounds, then compound sounds, and only then proceed to rhythmic patterns.

The analogy to musical education is precise: you must understand the individual notes before you can understand melodies, and you must understand individual letters before you can understand how names imitate essences. This establishes a bottom-up analytical method: start with the smallest phonetic units and work upward to syllables, words, and sentences.
elegant: Analyze primary names from the bottom up: first classify individual letters, then syllables, then words -- just as music students learn elementary sounds before rhythms. Understanding names requires understanding their phonetic atoms.
summary: Socrates proposes a bottom-up analysis: classify individual letters first, then see how they combine into syllables and names, just as music students learn individual sounds before rhythms.
distilled: Analyze names from the bottom up -- classify individual letters first, then syllables, then words, just as music students master single sounds before rhythms.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this establishes the method for the sound-symbolism analysis (2.75-2.76). The analogy to musical education reflects the Greek grammatike tradition, which began with letters (grammata). The approach is structuralist: understand the system of elements before analyzing their combinations. Cross-references: Republic 402a-c (on learning letters as an analogy for philosophy), Theaetetus 202-206 (on elements and compounds), Philebus 17-18 (on the method of division).

narrative: "Must we not begin in the same way with letters; first separating the vowels, and then the consonants and mutes (letters which are neither vowels nor semivowels), into classes, according to the received distinctions of the learned; also the semivowels, which are neither vowels, nor yet mutes; and distinguishing into classes the vowels themselves? And when we have perfected the classification of things, we shall give them names, and see whether, as in the case of letters, there are any classes to which they may be all referred (cf. Phaedrus); and hence we shall see their natures, and see, too, whether they have in them classes as there are in the letters; and when we have well considered all this, we shall know how to apply them to what they resemble -- whether one letter is used to denote one thing, or whether there is to be an admixture of several of them; just, as in painting, the painter who wants to depict anything sometimes uses purple only, or any other colour, and sometimes mixes up several colours, as his method is when he has to paint flesh colour or anything of that kind -- he uses his colours as his figures appear to require them; and so, too, we shall apply letters to the expression of objects, either single letters when required, or several letters; and so we shall form syllables, as they are called, and from syllables make nouns and verbs; and thus, at last, from the combinations of nouns and verbs arrive at language, large and fair and whole; and as the painter made a figure, even so shall we make speech by the art of the namer or the rhetorician, or by some other art. Not that I am literally speaking of ourselves, but I was carried away -- meaning to say that this was the way in which (not we but) the ancients formed language, and what they put together we must take to pieces in like manner, if we are to attain a scientific view of the whole subject, and we must see whether the primary, and also whether the secondary elements are rightly given or not, for if they are not, the composition of them, my dear Hermogenes, will be a sorry piece of work, and in the wrong direction."
refashioned:
Socrates lays out a comprehensive program for analyzing language from its smallest units. First, classify the letters: separate vowels from consonants and mutes (letters that are neither vowels nor semivowels), following the accepted scholarly distinctions. Then classify the semivowels, and subdivide the vowels themselves into classes.

Once things are properly classified, see which letters correspond to which classes of things. Sometimes a single letter represents a single quality; sometimes several must be mixed together -- just as a painter sometimes uses a single pure color (purple alone) but other times mixes multiple pigments to achieve flesh tones or other complex hues. Each figure requires its own palette.

Letters are applied to objects the same way: singly when sufficient, in combination when needed. From letters come syllables, from syllables nouns and verbs, and from combinations of nouns and verbs comes language itself -- large, fair, and whole. Just as the painter creates a figure, so the namer creates speech.

Socrates catches himself -- he's speaking as if they're the ones creating language. He means that this is how the ancients formed language. Their task is the reverse: to take apart what the ancients put together, in order to reach a scientific understanding. If the primary elements are incorrectly assigned, everything built on them will be, as he tells Hermogenes, a sorry piece of work aimed in the wrong direction.
elegant: Classify letters as painters classify colors. Single letters represent single qualities; combinations create complex meanings, like mixing pigments for flesh tones. From letters come syllables, from syllables words, from words language -- large, fair, and whole. Our task is to reverse-engineer what the ancients built.
summary: Socrates presents language as built like a painting: letters are colors, syllables are mixed pigments, and the whole composition is language. The ancients assembled it; the task now is scientific disassembly to verify that the primary elements were correctly assigned.
distilled: Language is built like a painting -- letters are colors applied singly or mixed into syllables, words, and ultimately speech; our task is to reverse-engineer the ancients' composition and verify its correctness.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this is the most ambitious theoretical statement about language construction in the dialogue. The painter analogy extends the earlier comparison: musician, painter, and namer each imitate through their respective media (tones, colors, letters). The systematic classification of vowels, consonants, semivowels, and mutes reflects Greek grammatical tradition. The cf. Phaedrus reference connects to the method of collection and division. Cross-references: Phaedrus 265-266 (collection and division), Republic 402a-c (letters analogy), Theaetetus 202-206 (elements and compounds), Sophist (on combining forms).

narrative:
"That, Socrates, I can quite believe."

"Well, but do you suppose that you will be able to analyse them in this way? for I am certain that I should not."

"Much less am I likely to be able."
refashioned:
Hermogenes believes Socrates' theoretical framework but doubts the practical execution. Socrates himself confesses that he certainly couldn't carry out such a systematic analysis of all letters and their correspondences to things. Hermogenes adds that he's even less capable.

This brief exchange is a moment of genuine intellectual humility. The program Socrates has outlined -- a complete phonemic analysis mapping sounds to essences -- is enormously ambitious. Both speakers recognize they lack the capability to execute it fully. Yet the theoretical framework remains valuable even if complete implementation is beyond them.
elegant: Both Socrates and Hermogenes admit they cannot actually carry out the systematic letter-by-letter analysis. The program is theoretically sound but practically overwhelming.
summary: Both speakers admit they cannot execute the comprehensive letter-analysis program Socrates has outlined. The theory is sound but the practical task is beyond their abilities.
distilled: Both Socrates and Hermogenes confess they cannot carry out the systematic phonemic analysis -- the program is theoretically compelling but practically overwhelming.
notes: Speakers: Hermogenes and Socrates. No etymologies -- this is a moment of intellectual humility. The admission of incapacity is characteristically Socratic: knowing the limits of one's knowledge is itself a form of wisdom. The gap between theoretical program and practical execution anticipates modern linguistics' ongoing struggle to systematically map sound to meaning. Cross-references: Apology (on Socratic ignorance), Theaetetus (on the limits of analysis).

narrative:
"Shall we leave them, then? or shall we seek to discover, if we can, something about them, according to the measure of our ability, saying by way of preface, as I said before of the Gods, that of the truth about them we know nothing, and do but entertain human notions of them. And in this present enquiry, let us say to ourselves, before we proceed, that the higher method is the one which we or others who would analyse language to any good purpose must follow; but under the circumstances, as men say, we must do as well as we can. What do you think?"

"I very much approve."
refashioned:
Rather than abandon the inquiry, Socrates proposes a compromise. They should attempt what they can within the limits of their ability, with a clear preface -- the same disclaimer used for the gods: we know nothing of the ultimate truth, and our ideas are merely human notions.

The ideal method would be the systematic one he just described -- a complete scientific analysis of language. But under the circumstances, they must do the best they can with what they have. As the saying goes: "we must do as well as we can."

This is philosophical modesty paired with intellectual courage: acknowledging that the ideal standard is beyond reach while refusing to abandon the inquiry entirely. Better an imperfect attempt guided by the right principles than no attempt at all. Hermogenes fully approves this approach.
elegant: Rather than abandon the inquiry, do what we can within human limits. The ideal method is beyond us, but imperfect attempts guided by right principles surpass abandonment. We know nothing of the ultimate truth -- our ideas are merely human.
summary: Socrates proposes continuing with acknowledged limitations: the ideal systematic analysis is beyond them, but they should attempt what they can, prefacing that their ideas are merely human notions about ultimate truth.
distilled: Rather than abandon the inquiry because the ideal method is beyond reach, do the best you can within human limits -- imperfect attempts guided by right principles are better than nothing.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Hermogenes approving. No etymologies -- this is a methodological compromise. The preface echoes the divine disclaimer at 2.23: just as they couldn't claim knowledge of divine nature, they can't claim knowledge of ultimate linguistic truth. The phrase "do as well as we can" (prattein hosa dunatoi esmen) embodies Socratic practical wisdom. Cross-references: Apology (on the examined life within human limits), Timaeus 29c-d (the "likely story" as the best humans can achieve).

narrative:
"That objects should be imitated in letters and syllables, and so find expression, may appear ridiculous, Hermogenes, but it cannot be avoided -- there is no better principle to which we can look for the truth of first names. Deprived of this, we must have recourse to divine help, like the tragic poets, who in any perplexity have their gods waiting in the air; and must get out of our difficulty in like fashion, by saying that 'the Gods gave the first names, and therefore they are right.' This will be the best contrivance, or perhaps that other notion may be even better still, of deriving them from some barbarous people, for the barbarians are older than we are; or we may say that antiquity has cast a veil over them, which is the same sort of excuse as the last; for all these are not reasons but only ingenious excuses for having no reasons concerning the truth of words. And yet any sort of ignorance of first or primitive names involves an ignorance of secondary words; for they can only be explained by the primary. Clearly then the professor of languages should be able to give a very lucid explanation of first names, or let him be assured he will only talk nonsense about the rest. Do you not suppose this to be true?"

"Certainly, Socrates."

"My first notions of original names are truly wild and ridiculous, though I have no objection to impart them to you if you desire, and I hope that you will communicate to me in return anything better which you may have."

"Fear not; I will do my best."
refashioned:
Socrates acknowledges that the idea of imitating objects in letters and syllables may seem ridiculous. But there's no better principle available for explaining primary names. Without it, they'd have to resort to deus ex machina -- like tragic poets who, in any perplexity, have gods descend from above to resolve the plot. They'd simply declare that the gods gave the first names, so they must be right.

Or they could invoke the other escape routes: the words are foreign (from barbarian peoples who are older), or antiquity has cast a veil over them. But Socrates identifies all of these as ingenious excuses for having no real reasons -- evasions, not explanations.

The stakes are high: ignorance of primary names guarantees ignorance of secondary ones, since the latter are explained by the former. Anyone who claims expertise in language must explain first names clearly, or their knowledge of everything else is mere nonsense.

Socrates warns that his own theories about primary names are wild and ridiculous, but he'll share them if Hermogenes wants to hear. Hermogenes eagerly agrees.
elegant: Sound-imitation may seem ridiculous, but it's the only honest principle for primary names. The alternatives -- divine origin, foreign origin, antiquity's veil -- are all excuses, not explanations. Ignorance of primary names means ignorance of all words. The true linguist must explain first names or talk nonsense.
summary: Socrates defends sound-imitation as the only honest principle for primary names, dismissing divine origin, foreign borrowing, and antiquity's veil as mere excuses. Ignorance of primary names invalidates all secondary analysis. He warns his own theories will seem wild.
distilled: Sound-imitation is the only honest principle for primary names -- divine origin, foreign borrowing, and antiquity's veil are ingenious excuses, not real explanations.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. No specific etymologies -- this is a final defense of the sound-symbolism principle before its application. The deus ex machina analogy is pointed: Socrates refuses the lazy intellectual move of attributing language to gods. The three escape routes (divine, foreign, temporal) are systematically rejected as non-explanations. The insistence that primary names ground all secondary analysis echoes foundationalist epistemology. Cross-references: The escape routes were introduced at 2.41 (foreign), 2.65 (temporal), and various points (divine); the deus ex machina allusion references Greek tragedy; Republic (on the foundations of knowledge).

narrative: "In the first place, the letter rho appears to me to be the general instrument expressing all motion (kinesis). But I have not yet explained the meaning of this latter word, which is just iesis (going); for the letter eta was not in use among the ancients, who only employed epsilon; and the root is kiein, which is a foreign form, the same as ienai. And the old word kinesis will be correctly given as iesis in corresponding modern letters. Assuming this foreign root kiein, and allowing for the change of the eta and the insertion of the nu, we have kinesis, which should have been kieinsis or eisis; and stasis is the negative of ienai (or eisis), and has been improved into stasis. Now the letter rho, as I was saying, appeared to the imposer of names an excellent instrument for the expression of motion; and he frequently uses the letter for this purpose: for example, in the actual words rein and roe he represents motion by rho; also in the words tromos (trembling), trachus (rugged); and again, in words such as krouein (strike), thrauein (crush), ereikein (bruise), thruptein (break), kermatixein (crumble), rumbein (whirl): of all these sorts of movements he generally finds an expression in the letter R, because, as I imagine, he had observed that the tongue was most agitated and least at rest in the pronunciation of this letter, which he therefore used in order to express motion, just as by the letter iota he expresses the subtle elements which pass through all things."
refashioned:
Socrates begins his analysis of how individual letters imitate natural qualities.

Rho (R) is the general instrument for expressing motion. The tongue is most agitated and least at rest when pronouncing rho -- it vibrates and rolls -- which is why the name-giver used it to represent movement. Consider the evidence: rein (to flow), rhoe (stream), tromos (trembling), trachus (rugged), krouein (to strike), thrauein (to crush), ereikein (to bruise), thruptein (to break), kermatizein (to crumble), rhumbein (to whirl). In every word describing vigorous movement, rho is present.

Socrates also notes that kinesis (motion) itself derives from the foreign root kiein (same as ienai, to go). The ancients used epsilon where moderns use eta, so kinesis should really be kieinsis or eisis. And stasis (rest/stillness) is simply the negation of ienai/eisis -- the absence of going.

Iota expresses the subtle elements that pass through all things -- thin, penetrating qualities conveyed by a thin, penetrating sound.

The principle is sound-symbolism: the physical sensation of producing a sound corresponds to the quality it represents.
elegant: Rho (R) expresses motion because the tongue vibrates most when pronouncing it -- hence rein (flow), tromos (trembling), krouein (strike), rhumbein (whirl). Iota expresses subtle penetrating elements. The physical sensation of producing a sound corresponds to the quality it represents.
summary: Socrates assigns rho to motion (the tongue vibrates most when pronouncing it) and iota to subtle penetration. Words like rein, tromos, krouein, and rhumbein all contain rho because they describe movement. Sound-symbolism maps the physics of articulation to natural qualities.
distilled: Rho represents motion because the tongue vibrates when pronouncing it; iota represents subtle penetration -- the physical act of producing a sound maps onto the quality it expresses.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek letters and words analyzed: Rho (R -- instrument of motion; examples: rein, rhoe, tromos, trachus, krouein, thrauein, ereikein, thruptein, kermatizein, rhumbein), Iota (I -- instrument of subtlety/penetration), Kinesis (motion -- from kiein/ienai, going), Stasis (rest -- negation of ienai). This is the first systematic application of sound-symbolism. The phonetic observation about rho is genuinely insightful -- rho is a trill or flap in Greek, involving rapid tongue vibration. Modern phonosemantics has found some statistical tendencies supporting such claims. Cross-references: This is the practical application of the theory developed in 2.67-2.74. The analysis continues in 2.76.

narrative: "This is why he uses the letter iota as imitative of motion, ienai, iesthai. And there is another class of letters, phi, psi, sigma, and xi, of which the pronunciation is accompanied by great expenditure of breath; these are used in the imitation of such notions as psuchron (shivering), xeon (seething), seiesthai, (to be shaken), seismos (shock), and are always introduced by the giver of names when he wants to imitate what is phusodes (windy). He seems to have thought that the closing and pressure of the tongue in the utterance of delta and tau was expressive of binding and rest in a place: he further observed the liquid movement of lambda, in the pronunciation of which the tongue slips, and in this he found the expression of smoothness, as in leios (level), and in the word oliothanein (to slip) itself, liparon (sleek), in the word kollodes (gluey), and the like: the heavier sound of gamma detained the slipping tongue, and the union of the two gave the notion of a glutinous clammy nature, as in glischros, glukus, gloiodes. The nu he observed to be sounded from within, and therefore to have a notion of inwardness; hence he introduced the sound in endos and entos: alpha he assigned to the expression of size, and nu of length, because they are great letters: omicron was the sign of roundness, and therefore there is plenty of omicron mixed up in the word goggulon (round). Thus did the legislator, reducing all things into letters and syllables, and impressing on them names and signs, and out of them by imitation compounding other signs. That is my view, Hermogenes, of the truth of names; but I should like to hear what Cratylus has more to say."
refashioned:
Socrates completes his comprehensive sound-symbolism system, mapping each type of letter to the quality it naturally expresses:

Iota: subtle motion and penetration (ienai, iesthai -- going, moving).

Phi, psi, sigma, xi: these are breathy letters requiring great expenditure of air. They imitate windy, blowing, shaking phenomena -- psuchron (shivering/cold), xeon (seething), seiesthai (being shaken), seismos (earthquake/shock), phusodes (windy).

Delta and tau: the tongue closes and presses in their pronunciation, expressing binding and rest. These stops halt motion.

Lambda: a liquid letter where the tongue slips and slides, expressing smoothness -- leios (level/smooth), olisthanein (to slip), liparon (sleek), kollodes (gluey).

Gamma: heavier than lambda, it detains the slipping tongue. Combined with lambda, it creates the sensation of sticky, glutinous clinging -- glischros (viscous), glukus (sweet/sticky), gloiodes (gluey).

Nu: sounded from within the nasal cavity, expressing inwardness -- endos (within), entos (inside).

Alpha: large and open, expressing greatness and size.

Eta: also large, expressing length.

Omicron: round in the mouth's formation, expressing roundness -- hence goggulon (round) is full of omicrons.

Thus the original legislator reduced all things to letters and syllables, imprinted them with names and signs, and from these imitative units compounded further signs. This is Socrates' complete theory of names.

He concludes by inviting Cratylus to speak.
elegant: Each letter type maps to a natural quality: rho = motion, phi/psi/sigma/xi = breath and wind, delta/tau = binding and rest, lambda = smoothness, gamma+lambda = viscosity, nu = inwardness, alpha = size, eta = length, omicron = roundness. The legislator reduced all things to imitative phonetic atoms.
summary: Socrates completes his sound-symbolism: breathy letters for wind and shaking, stops for binding, lambda for smoothness, gamma-lambda for viscosity, nu for inwardness, alpha for size, omicron for roundness. The legislator built language by mapping phonetic qualities to natural qualities and compounding them into names.
distilled: The complete sound-symbolism: rho = motion, breathy letters = wind, stops = binding, lambda = smoothness, gamma = stickiness, nu = inwardness, alpha = size, omicron = roundness -- the legislator built language from these imitative phonetic atoms.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. Greek letters and words analyzed: Iota (subtle motion -- ienai), Phi/Psi/Sigma/Xi (breath/wind -- psuchron, xeon, seiesthai, seismos, phusodes), Delta/Tau (binding/rest -- closure of tongue), Lambda (smoothness -- leios, olisthanein, liparon, kollodes), Gamma (heaviness/detention -- combined with lambda for viscosity: glischros, glukus, gloiodes), Nu (inwardness -- endos, entos), Alpha (size/greatness), Eta (length), Omicron (roundness -- goggulon). This is the culmination of the dialogue's linguistic theory. While modern linguistics rejects systematic sound-symbolism, some phonosemantic tendencies (e.g., the bouba-kiki effect) have been experimentally confirmed. The comprehensive mapping of articulatory phonetics to semantic domains is remarkably systematic for ancient thought. Cross-references: This completes the program outlined in 2.67-2.74. The transition to Cratylus' response marks the end of Part 2.

narrative: "But, Socrates, as I was telling you before, Cratylus mystifies me; he says that there is a fitness of names, but he never explains what is this fitness, so that I cannot tell whether his obscurity is intended or not. Tell me now, Cratylus, here in the presence of Socrates, do you agree in what Socrates has been saying about names, or have you something better of your own? and if you have, tell me what your view is, and then you will either learn of Socrates, or Socrates and I will learn of you."
refashioned:
Hermogenes turns to Cratylus, bringing the long-silent third participant into the conversation. He repeats his earlier frustration: Cratylus insists there is a natural fitness of names but never explains what this fitness consists of. Hermogenes can't even tell whether the obscurity is deliberate mystification or genuine inability to articulate his view.

He issues a direct challenge: here, in Socrates' presence, Cratylus must declare himself. Does he agree with what Socrates has been saying about names? Or does he have something better to offer? If so, let him speak up. The result will be mutual education: either Cratylus will learn from Socrates, or both Socrates and Hermogenes will learn from Cratylus.

This marks the dramatic transition from Part 2 (Socrates' extended etymological investigation with Hermogenes) to Part 3 (the dialectical exchange with Cratylus).
elegant: Hermogenes challenges Cratylus directly: you claim names have natural fitness but never explain what that means. Here before Socrates, agree with his theory or offer something better. This marks the transition from etymological investigation to philosophical debate.
summary: Hermogenes challenges the long-silent Cratylus to either agree with Socrates' theory of names or present a superior alternative. This marks the dramatic transition from Part 2's etymological investigation to Part 3's philosophical debate.
distilled: Hermogenes challenges Cratylus: agree with Socrates' theory of natural names or offer something better -- marking the transition from etymology to philosophical debate.
notes: Speaker: Hermogenes. No etymologies -- this is the pivotal dramatic transition. Cratylus has been silent throughout Part 2, listening to Socrates' elaborate etymological investigation. His naturalist position (names have an inherent fitness) has been argued throughout by Socrates but with increasing ironic distance. Now Cratylus must respond to a theory that supports his view but with qualifications and self-awareness he may not share. Cross-references: The dialogue's opening (1.2-1.3, where Hermogenes first reported Cratylus' mysterious claims), Part 3 (where Socrates will critique Cratylus' rigid naturalism).

narrative:
"Well, but surely, Hermogenes, you do not suppose that you can learn, or I explain, any subject of importance all in a moment; at any rate, not such a subject as language, which is, perhaps, the very greatest of all."

"No, indeed; but, as Hesiod says, and I agree with him, 'to add little to little' is worth while. And, therefore, if you think that you can add anything at all, however small, to our knowledge, take a little trouble and oblige Socrates, and me too, who certainly have a claim upon you."
refashioned:
Cratylus finally speaks, but characteristically resists direct engagement. He protests that no one can learn or explain a subject as important as language in a single conversation. Language may be the very greatest subject of all.

Hermogenes counters with Hesiod's wisdom: "to add little to little" is worthwhile. Even a small contribution to their understanding would be valuable. If Cratylus can add anything at all, however modest, he should take the trouble -- he owes it to both Socrates and Hermogenes, who have a legitimate claim on his participation.

This exchange establishes the dynamic for Part 3: Cratylus reluctant and guarded, Hermogenes pressing for engagement, and Socrates waiting to apply his dialectical method to whatever Cratylus reveals. The massive etymological investigation of Part 2 has prepared the ground; now the philosophical harvest must begin.
elegant: Cratylus protests that language is too vast for one conversation. Hermogenes quotes Hesiod: "to add little to little" is worthwhile. Even small contributions matter. The etymological investigation has prepared the ground; now philosophical harvest begins.
summary: Cratylus resists quick explanation, saying language may be the greatest subject. Hermogenes counters with Hesiod: even small additions to knowledge matter. This final Part 2 exchange sets up the dynamic for Part 3's philosophical debate.
distilled: Cratylus protests that language is too vast to explain quickly, but Hermogenes quotes Hesiod: adding little to little is worthwhile -- even small contributions to understanding matter.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Hermogenes. The Hesiod quotation ("to add little to little") comes from Works and Days 361. Cratylus' reluctance to explain himself has been a theme since the dialogue's opening (1.2). His claim that language is "perhaps the very greatest of all" subjects elevates the investigation's stakes. The contrast between Socrates' prolific etymologizing and Cratylus' reticence is dramatic -- the philosopher who believes most strongly in natural names says least about them. Cross-references: Works and Days 361 (Hesiod), the opening exchange (1.2) where Hermogenes first described Cratylus' obscurity, Part 3 (the philosophical examination of Cratylus' views).

narrative:
"I am by no means positive, Cratylus, in the view which Hermogenes and myself have worked out; and therefore do not hesitate to say what you think, which if it be better than my own view I shall gladly accept. And I should not be at all surprised to find that you have found some better notion. For you have evidently reflected on these matters and have had teachers, and if you have really a better theory of the truth of names, you may count me in the number of your disciples."

"You are right, Socrates, in saying that I have made a study of these matters, and I might possibly convert you into a disciple. But I fear that the opposite is more probable, and I already find myself moved to say to you what Achilles in the 'Prayers' says to Ajax."
refashioned:
"I am far from certain, Cratylus, about the conclusions Hermogenes and I have reached. So do not hesitate to share your own view -- if it is better than mine, I will gladly accept it. It would not surprise me at all to learn that you have arrived at something superior. You have clearly given these questions serious thought and studied with teachers, and if you truly have a better theory about the correctness of names, consider me your student."

"You are right that I have studied these matters carefully, Socrates, and I might well convert you into a disciple. But I suspect the opposite is more likely, and I already find myself wanting to quote what Achilles says to Ajax in the 'Prayers.'"
elegant: Socrates invites Cratylus to challenge his provisional conclusions about names, offering to become his student if Cratylus has a better theory. Cratylus acknowledges his own study of the subject but suspects Socrates will end up persuading him instead, comparing the situation to Achilles addressing Ajax.
summary: Socrates humbly invites Cratylus into the dialogue, expressing uncertainty about the conclusions reached with Hermogenes and offering to become Cratylus's student if he has a better theory of names.
distilled: Socrates opens the dialogue with Cratylus by professing uncertainty and inviting correction, while Cratylus anticipates being persuaded rather than persuading.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Cratylus. -- This marks the transition to the third and final section of the Cratylus, where Socrates engages directly with the historical Cratylus, who was reportedly an early influence on Plato and a follower of Heraclitus. The reference to Achilles in the "Prayers" (Litai) alludes to Iliad IX, where Achilles responds to Ajax's embassy -- the implication being that Cratylus, like Achilles, already feels the force of the argument pressing against him. Socrates' characteristic profession of ignorance here mirrors his method throughout the early dialogues, particularly the Meno and Theaetetus, where intellectual humility serves as the starting point for genuine inquiry.
