narrative: "Excellent Cratylus, I have long been wondering at my own wisdom; I cannot trust myself. And I think that I ought to stop and ask myself, 'What am I saying?' for there is nothing worse than self-deception -- when the deceiver is always at home and always with you -- it is quite terrible, and therefore I ought often to retrace my steps and endeavour to 'look fore and aft,' in the words of the aforesaid Homer. And now let me see; where are we? Have we not been saying that the correct name indicates the nature of the thing? -- has this proposition been sufficiently proven?"
refashioned: "My dear Cratylus, I have long been amazed at my own supposed wisdom, and I cannot bring myself to trust it. I think I should stop and ask myself: what exactly am I saying? There is nothing worse than self-deception -- the deceiver is always at home, always right there with you -- and that makes it truly terrible. So I must constantly retrace my steps and, as Homer says, 'look fore and aft.' Now, where do we stand? Have we not been arguing that a correct name reveals the nature of the thing it names? Has that proposition been adequately established?"
elegant: Socrates warns against self-deception -- the worst kind, since the deceiver is always present. He proposes they review their conclusions: have they truly established that a correct name reveals the nature of what it names?
summary: Socrates expresses distrust of his own conclusions and warns against self-deception, then proposes reviewing the central thesis that correct names indicate the nature of things.
distilled: Socrates warns that self-deception is the worst deception because it is inescapable, and proposes critically reexamining whether names truly reveal the nature of things.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The warning against self-deception echoes the Delphic injunction "know thyself" that animates Socratic philosophy throughout the dialogues. The Homer quotation ("look fore and aft") comes from Iliad I.343 and III.109. This moment of self-critical review is characteristic of Socratic method -- the willingness to overturn one's own conclusions -- and anticipates the dramatic reversal that will unfold in Part 3, where Socrates systematically undermines the naturalist theory of names he appeared to champion in Part 2. Compare Theaetetus 150c-d, where Socrates describes his role as intellectual midwife who must test whether conclusions are genuine or merely wind-eggs.

narrative:
"Yes, Socrates, what you say, as I am disposed to think, is quite true."

"Names, then, are given in order to instruct?"

"Certainly."

"And naming is an art, and has artificers?"

"Yes."

"And who are they?"

"The legislators, of whom you spoke at first."

"And does this art grow up among men like other arts? Let me explain what I mean: of painters, some are better and some worse?"

"Yes."

"The better painters execute their works, I mean their figures, better, and the worse execute them worse; and of builders also, the better sort build fairer houses, and the worse build them worse."

"True."

"And among legislators, there are some who do their work better and some worse?"

"No; there I do not agree with you."

"Then you do not think that some laws are better and others worse?"

"No, indeed."

"Or that one name is better than another?"

"Certainly not."

"Then all names are rightly imposed?"

"Yes, if they are names at all."

"Well, what do you say to the name of our friend Hermogenes, which was mentioned before? -- assuming that he has nothing of the nature of Hermes in him, shall we say that this is a wrong name, or not his name at all?"

"I should reply that Hermogenes is not his name at all, but only appears to be his, and is really the name of somebody else, who has the nature which corresponds to it."

"And if a man were to call him Hermogenes, would he not be even speaking falsely? For there may be a doubt whether you can call him Hermogenes, if he is not."

"What do you mean?"

"Are you maintaining that falsehood is impossible? For if this is your meaning I should answer, that there have been plenty of liars in all ages."

"Why, Socrates, how can a man say that which is not? -- say something and yet say nothing? For is not falsehood saying the thing which is not?"

"Your argument, friend, is too subtle for a man of my age. But I should like to know whether you are one of those philosophers who think that falsehood may be spoken but not said?"

"Neither spoken nor said."
refashioned:
Cratylus agrees that names are given to instruct, that naming is an art practiced by legislators. Socrates then draws an analogy: among painters and builders, some are better and some worse at their craft. Surely the same applies to legislators?

Cratylus disagrees. He denies that some laws or names are better than others. If something is a name at all, it is rightly given.

Socrates presses him: what about Hermogenes, who has nothing of the nature of Hermes in him? Is his name wrong?

Cratylus replies that "Hermogenes" is simply not his name at all -- it only appears to be. It truly belongs to someone who possesses the nature it describes.

Socrates pushes further: if someone calls him Hermogenes, are they speaking falsely? Can you even call someone by a name that does not belong to them?

Cratylus reveals his radical position: falsehood is impossible. How can anyone say what is not -- say something and yet say nothing? Falsehood is saying what does not exist, which cannot be done.

Socrates asks whether Cratylus is one of those philosophers who think falsehood can be spoken but not stated as a proposition. Cratylus replies: neither spoken nor said.
elegant: Cratylus accepts that names instruct and that legislators create them, but denies any legislator can do the job badly. If something is a name at all, it is correct; if it fails to match its object's nature, it is simply not a name. Pressed on Hermogenes -- who lacks the nature of Hermes -- Cratylus says the name merely appears to be his but truly belongs to someone else. This leads to his most radical claim: falsehood is impossible. One cannot say what is not, because saying what does not exist means saying nothing at all.
summary: Socrates elicits Cratylus's extreme naturalist position: every genuine name is correct, incorrectly applied names are not names at all, and falsehood is impossible since one cannot say what is not.
distilled: Cratylus reveals his radical thesis that all genuine names are inherently correct, misapplied names are not names at all, and false speech is literally impossible.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- Cratylus's denial that falsehood is possible directly echoes the Eleatic paradox attributed to Parmenides: "what is not" cannot be thought or spoken. This same problem is central to the Sophist (236d-264b), where the Eleatic Stranger commits "parricide" against Parmenides by showing how non-being can participate in being, thereby making false statement possible. Cratylus's position also recalls Antisthenes (founder of Cynicism), who reportedly held that contradiction is impossible -- if two people seem to disagree, one of them is simply not talking about the same thing. The example of Hermogenes recurs from Part 1, where Hermogenes' name ("born of Hermes," god of commerce) was ironic given his poverty.

narrative:
"Nor uttered nor addressed? For example: If a person, saluting you in a foreign country, were to take your hand and say, 'Hail, Athenian stranger, Hermogenes, son of Smicrion' -- these words, whether spoken, said, uttered, or addressed, would have no application to you but only to our friend Hermogenes, or perhaps to nobody at all?"

"In my opinion, Socrates, the speaker would only be talking nonsense."

"Well, but that will be quite enough for me, if you will tell me whether the nonsense would be true or false, or partly true and partly false -- which is all that I want to know."

"I should say that he would be putting himself in motion to no purpose; and that his words would be an unmeaning sound like the noise of hammering at a brazen pot."

"But let us see, Cratylus, whether we cannot find a meeting-point, for you would admit that the name is not the same with the thing named?"

"I should."

"And would you further acknowledge that the name is an imitation of the thing?"

"Certainly."

"And you would say that pictures are also imitations of things, but in another way?"

"Yes."

"I believe you may be right, but I do not rightly understand you. Please to say, then, whether both sorts of imitation (I mean both pictures or words) are not equally attributable and applicable to the things of which they are the imitation."

"They are."

"First look at the matter thus: you may attribute the likeness of the man to the man, and of the woman to the woman; and so on?"

"Certainly."

"And conversely you may attribute the likeness of the man to the woman, and of the woman to the man?"

"Very true."

"And are both modes of assigning them right, or only the first?"

"Only the first."

"That is to say, the mode of assignment which attributes to each that which belongs to them and is like them?"

"That is my view."
refashioned:
Socrates tests the claim further: if a stranger in a foreign country took Cratylus by the hand and greeted him as "Hermogenes, son of Smicrion" -- would those words have no application to Cratylus, only to the real Hermogenes, or perhaps to nobody at all?

Cratylus insists the speaker would simply be talking nonsense -- putting himself in motion to no purpose, producing meaningless noise like hammering on a bronze pot.

Socrates seeks common ground. Cratylus agrees that a name is not the same as the thing it names, that a name is an imitation of a thing, and that pictures are also imitations in a different mode. Both kinds of imitation -- visual and verbal -- can be attributed to the things they represent.

Socrates then establishes a crucial point through analogy: you can assign a man's portrait to the man, or a woman's portrait to the woman -- that is correct attribution. You can also assign a man's portrait to a woman or a woman's to a man -- but only the first mode of assignment is right. Cratylus agrees: the correct mode attributes to each thing what belongs to it and is like it.
elegant: Socrates illustrates with a scenario: if a stranger called Cratylus "Hermogenes," Cratylus would dismiss it as meaningless noise. But Socrates finds common ground -- names are imitations of things, as pictures are imitations in a different mode. Both can be correctly or incorrectly assigned. A man's portrait belongs to the man, a woman's to the woman. Only attribution that matches likeness to its proper object is correct.
summary: Socrates establishes that names, like pictures, are imitations that can be correctly or incorrectly assigned to objects. Correct assignment matches the imitation to what it resembles.
distilled: Socrates establishes through the analogy of portraits that names are imitations which can be correctly or incorrectly attributed, since only assigning a likeness to its proper object counts as right.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Cratylus. -- The analogy between names and pictures is pivotal for the argument that will follow. By getting Cratylus to concede that imitations can be misassigned, Socrates opens the door to showing that names can be wrong -- which Cratylus had denied. The "brazen pot" image is characteristically Platonic -- compare Republic 531a where similar dismissive metaphors appear. The distinction between correct and incorrect attribution of imitations anticipates the Sophist's discussion of images (eidola) and the difference between faithful likenesses (eikones) and deceptive semblances (phantasmata) at 235d-236c. The mention of Smicrion identifies Hermogenes' father, placing the dialogue in specific Athenian social context.

narrative: "Now then, as I am desirous that we being friends should have a good understanding about the argument, let me state my view to you: the first mode of assignment, whether applied to figures or to names, I call right, and when applied to names only, true as well as right; and the other mode of giving and assigning the name which is unlike, I call wrong, and in the case of names, false as well as wrong."
refashioned: "Since I want us, as friends, to understand each other clearly on this point, let me state my view directly. The first mode of assignment -- matching an imitation to what it truly resembles -- I call right, whether we are dealing with pictures or names. When applied specifically to names, I call it not only right but true. The other mode -- assigning a name that does not resemble its object -- I call wrong, and in the case of names, false as well as wrong."
elegant: Socrates defines his terms: assigning an imitation to what it resembles is right, and in the case of names, true. Assigning one that does not resemble is wrong, and in the case of names, false.
summary: Socrates formulates the principle that correct assignment of names is both right and true, while incorrect assignment is both wrong and false -- establishing that falsehood in naming is possible.
distilled: Socrates defines correct naming as assigning imitations to what they resemble, making such assignment both right and true, while misassignment is both wrong and false.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This formulation directly challenges Cratylus's claim that falsehood is impossible. By establishing that names can be wrongly assigned (like misattributed portraits), Socrates creates logical space for false statements. The distinction between "right" (orthos) and "true" (alethes) is significant -- rightness applies to both pictures and names as imitations, but truth applies specifically to linguistic imitations. This argument is developed more rigorously in the Sophist (261d-263d), where the Eleatic Stranger shows that false statements combine names and verbs in ways that do not correspond to reality. The friendly tone ("as friends") reflects Socrates' characteristic method of making philosophical opponents feel like collaborative partners.

narrative:
"That may be true, Socrates, in the case of pictures; they may be wrongly assigned; but not in the case of names -- they must be always right."

"Why, what is the difference? May I not go to a man and say to him, 'This is your picture,' showing him his own likeness, or perhaps the likeness of a woman; and when I say 'show,' I mean bring before the sense of sight."

"Certainly."

"And may I not go to him again, and say, 'This is your name'? -- for the name, like the picture, is an imitation. May I not say to him -- 'This is your name'? and may I not then bring to his sense of hearing the imitation of himself, when I say, 'This is a man'; or of a female of the human species, when I say, 'This is a woman,' as the case may be? Is not all that quite possible?"

"I would fain agree with you, Socrates; and therefore I say, Granted."
refashioned:
Cratylus concedes that pictures can be wrongly assigned but insists names must always be correct. Socrates challenges the distinction: just as you can show a man his own portrait or mistakenly show him a woman's portrait -- bringing an imitation before his sense of sight -- can you not also bring a verbal imitation before his sense of hearing? You can say "this is your name" and then present the word for "man" or the word for "woman," correctly or incorrectly. Is that not equally possible?

Cratylus reluctantly agrees: "I would gladly agree with you, Socrates; and therefore I say, granted."
elegant: Cratylus insists names cannot be wrongly assigned, only pictures. Socrates dismantles the distinction: just as you can show a man the wrong portrait, you can present him the wrong name. Pictures are imitations brought before sight; names are imitations brought before hearing. The possibility of misassignment applies equally to both. Cratylus reluctantly concedes.
summary: Socrates argues that if pictures can be wrongly assigned, so can names, since both are imitations presented to different senses. Cratylus reluctantly concedes the point.
distilled: Socrates demonstrates that names and pictures are both imitations assignable to the wrong objects, collapsing Cratylus's distinction between the two and forcing a reluctant concession.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- Cratylus's reluctance ("I would fain agree") signals that Socrates is pushing him beyond his comfort zone. The parallel between visual and auditory imitation is a powerful argument that exploits Cratylus's own concession that names are imitations. This sensory framing -- sight for pictures, hearing for names -- anticipates discussions in the Republic (507c-509d) about the different media through which Forms are apprehended. The argument also connects to the Sophist's analysis of how images relate to their originals, and the crucial distinction between an image that faithfully represents and one that merely appears to represent.

narrative: "That is very good of you, if I am right, which need hardly be disputed at present. But if I can assign names as well as pictures to objects, the right assignment of them we may call truth, and the wrong assignment of them falsehood. Now if there be such a wrong assignment of names, there may also be a wrong or inappropriate assignment of verbs; and if of names and verbs then of the sentences, which are made up of them. What do you say, Cratylus?"
refashioned: "That is generous of you, and I think my point need hardly be disputed further. But if names, like pictures, can be assigned to objects both rightly and wrongly, then right assignment is truth and wrong assignment is falsehood. And if names can be wrongly assigned, then so can verbs. And if both names and verbs can be wrongly assigned, then entire sentences -- which are composed of names and verbs -- can be false as well. What do you say, Cratylus?"
elegant: If names can be wrongly assigned, the wrong assignment is falsehood. If names can be false, so can verbs. And if both names and verbs can be false, then entire sentences composed of them can be false as well.
summary: Socrates extends the argument from individual names to verbs and entire sentences: if names can be wrongly assigned, then falsehood extends to all levels of language.
distilled: Socrates scales up the possibility of falsehood from individual names to verbs to complete sentences, establishing that false speech is possible at every level of language.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This stepwise argument -- from names (onomata) to verbs (rhemata) to sentences (logoi) -- is the same logical structure used by the Eleatic Stranger in the Sophist (261d-263d) to demonstrate how false statements are possible. In the Sophist, the Stranger shows that a logos is the minimum unit of truth or falsehood, composed of a name and a verb (e.g., "Theaetetus sits"). The argument here in the Cratylus represents an earlier, less developed version of the same insight. Having established that falsehood is possible -- contra Cratylus's earlier denial -- Socrates can now examine what makes names more or less accurate imitations.

narrative:
"I agree; and think that what you say is very true."

"And further, primitive nouns may be compared to pictures, and in pictures you may either give all the appropriate colours and figures, or you may not give them all -- some may be wanting; or there may be too many or too much of them -- may there not?"

"Very true."

"And he who gives all gives a perfect picture or figure; and he who takes away or adds also gives a picture or figure, but not a good one."

"Yes."

"In like manner, he who by syllables and letters imitates the nature of things, if he gives all that is appropriate will produce a good image, or in other words a name; but if he subtracts or perhaps adds a little, he will make an image but not a good one; whence I infer that some names are well and others ill made."

"That is true."

"Then the artist of names may be sometimes good, or he may be bad?"

"Yes."

"And this artist of names is called the legislator?"

"Yes."

"Then like other artists the legislator may be good or he may be bad; it must surely be so if our former admissions hold good?"

"Very true, Socrates; but the case of language, you see, is different; for when by the help of grammar we assign the letters alpha or beta, or any other letters to a certain name, then, if we add, or subtract, or misplace a letter, the name which is written is not only written wrongly, but not written at all; and in any of these cases becomes other than a name."

"But I doubt whether your view is altogether correct, Cratylus."

"How so?"
refashioned:
Cratylus agrees. Socrates extends the analogy: primitive nouns can be compared to pictures. In a painting, you may include all the appropriate colors and shapes, or you may leave some out, or add too many. The artist who includes everything produces a perfect image; the one who adds or subtracts produces an image, but not a good one.

Similarly, someone who imitates the nature of things through syllables and letters will produce a good image -- a well-made name -- if everything appropriate is included. If something is subtracted or added, the result is still an image, but not a good one. Therefore some names are well made and others poorly made. The name-maker, the legislator, can be skilled or unskilled, just like any other artist.

Cratylus resists: language is different, he argues. When we spell a name using specific letters, any addition, subtraction, or rearrangement of letters means the name is not merely written badly -- it is not written at all. It becomes something entirely other than a name.

Socrates expresses doubt: "I am not sure your view is altogether correct."
elegant: Names, like paintings, can include all appropriate elements or fall short. A painter who includes everything produces a perfect image; one who adds or subtracts produces an inferior one. Likewise, a name-maker who captures the nature of things in syllables and letters may do so well or poorly -- making the legislator, like any artist, capable of better or worse work. Cratylus resists: in language, adding, removing, or misplacing even one letter does not make a name worse -- it makes it no name at all.
summary: Socrates argues that names, like paintings, can be better or worse imitations. Cratylus counters that language is different from painting: any alteration to a name's letters destroys it entirely rather than merely degrading it.
distilled: Socrates argues name-makers can be more or less skilled like painters, but Cratylus insists that any alteration to a name's letters does not degrade it but annihilates it entirely.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- The painting analogy is central to Plato's theory of mimesis (imitation), developed extensively in Republic X (595a-608b). Cratylus's counterargument introduces an important distinction between quantitative and qualitative imitation -- he treats names as functioning like numbers (where changing any element changes the identity entirely) rather than like paintings (where imperfections degrade quality without destroying identity). Socrates will address this distinction directly in 3.9. The term "legislator" (nomothetēs) for the name-maker was introduced in Part 1 and connects to the idea that language is a social institution, a theme explored in the Statesman (303e-305e) regarding the legislator's art.

narrative: "I believe that what you say may be true about numbers, which must be just what they are, or not be at all; for example, the number ten at once becomes other than ten if a unit be added or subtracted, and so of any other number: but this does not apply to that which is qualitative or to anything which is represented under an image. I should say rather that the image, if expressing in every point the entire reality, would no longer be an image. Let us suppose the existence of two objects: one of them shall be Cratylus, and the other the image of Cratylus; and we will suppose, further, that some God makes not only a representation such as a painter would make of your outward form and colour, but also creates an inward organization like yours, having the same warmth and softness; and into this infuses motion, and soul, and mind, such as you have, and in a word copies all your qualities, and places them by you in another form; would you say that this was Cratylus and the image of Cratylus, or that there were two Cratyluses?"
refashioned:
"What you say may be true of numbers -- a number must be exactly what it is or not exist at all. Add or subtract a unit from ten and it instantly becomes something other than ten. But this does not apply to things that are qualitative, or to anything represented as an image. In fact, I would argue the opposite: if an image expressed every single aspect of the reality it represents, it would no longer be an image at all.

Consider this thought experiment. Suppose there are two objects: one is Cratylus, and the other is an image of Cratylus. Now suppose some god creates not merely a painter's likeness of your outward appearance and coloring, but also replicates your entire inner constitution -- the same warmth and softness of your body -- and then breathes into it motion, soul, and mind identical to yours, copying every one of your qualities and placing this duplicate right beside you. Would you say this was Cratylus and the image of Cratylus, or that there were two Cratyluses?"
elegant: Numbers must be exactly what they are or cease to exist -- change one unit of ten and it is no longer ten. But images work differently. If an image replicated every aspect of reality, it would cease to be an image and become the thing itself. Imagine a god who copies not just your appearance but your inner nature -- warmth, motion, soul, mind -- and places this perfect duplicate beside you. Would that be Cratylus and his image, or two Cratyluses?
summary: Socrates distinguishes numbers (which must be exact) from images (which must differ from their originals). A perfect copy of Cratylus would not be an image of Cratylus but a second Cratylus -- proving that images necessarily fall short of complete replication.
distilled: Socrates argues that a perfect image would cease to be an image and become the thing itself, proving that all genuine imitation requires some degree of incompleteness.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This is one of the most philosophically rich passages in the Cratylus. The argument that a perfect copy would be a duplicate rather than an image anticipates modern discussions of identity and indiscernibles (Leibniz's principle). The thought experiment of a god creating a perfect copy of Cratylus resonates with the distinction between Forms and particulars in the Phaedo (74a-75d) and Republic (596a-597e) -- a Form is not merely a perfect copy of particulars but something ontologically different. The argument also connects to Parmenides 132d-133a, where the "Third Man" argument shows that positing perfect likenesses leads to infinite regress. The key insight -- that representation requires difference from what is represented -- is foundational for Plato's theory of mimesis.

narrative:
"I should say that there were two Cratyluses."

"Then you see, my friend, that we must find some other principle of truth in images, and also in names; and not insist that an image is no longer an image when something is added or subtracted. Do you not perceive that images are very far from having qualities which are the exact counterpart of the realities which they represent?"

"Yes, I see."

"But then how ridiculous would be the effect of names on things, if they were exactly the same with them! For they would be the doubles of them, and no one would be able to determine which were the names and which were the realities."

"Quite true."
refashioned:
Cratylus admits there would be two Cratyluses, not a Cratylus and his image.

"Then you see, my friend, that we need a different principle of truth for images and for names. We cannot insist that an image ceases to be an image whenever something is added or subtracted. Surely you can see that images are very far from being exact counterparts of the realities they represent?"

Cratylus agrees.

"And consider how absurd it would be if names were exactly the same as the things they name! They would be mere duplicates, and no one could tell which were the names and which were the realities."

"Quite true."
elegant: Cratylus concedes: a perfect copy would be a second Cratylus, not an image. Therefore images need not -- and should not -- be exact counterparts of their originals. If names were identical to the things they name, they would be duplicates, and no one could distinguish names from realities.
summary: Cratylus admits a perfect replica would be a duplicate, not an image. Socrates concludes that images and names must necessarily differ from what they represent, or they would be indistinguishable from reality itself.
distilled: Since a perfect copy is a duplicate rather than an image, names and images must necessarily fall short of the realities they represent -- otherwise no one could tell names from things.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- Cratylus's concession is a turning point: by admitting that perfect replication creates duplicates rather than images, he implicitly accepts that imperfect resemblance is not a defect of names but their essential nature. This undermines his earlier insistence that any alteration to a name destroys it entirely. The argument that names identical to things would be "doubles" connects to the Republic's discussion of art as twice removed from reality (597e) -- the painting of a bed is not a bed, and this distance is what makes it a representation rather than the thing itself. The insight also prefigures Nelson Goodman's distinction between representation and duplication in Languages of Art.

narrative:
"Then fear not, but have the courage to admit that one name may be correctly and another incorrectly given; and do not insist that the name shall be exactly the same with the thing; but allow the occasional substitution of a wrong letter, and if of a letter also of a noun in a sentence, and if of a noun in a sentence also of a sentence which is not appropriate to the matter, and acknowledge that the thing may be named, and described, so long as the general character of the thing which you are describing is retained; and this, as you will remember, was remarked by Hermogenes and myself in the particular instance of the names of the letters."

"Yes, I remember."
refashioned:
"Then take courage and admit that one name may be correctly given and another incorrectly. Do not insist that a name must be identical to the thing it names. Allow for the occasional wrong letter. And if a wrong letter is permissible, then so is a wrong noun in a sentence. And if a wrong noun, then an entire sentence that does not perfectly fit the subject. Acknowledge that a thing can be named and described so long as the general character of what you are describing is preserved. Hermogenes and I made this very observation when we discussed the names of the letters."

"Yes, I remember."
elegant: Have the courage to admit that names can be correctly or incorrectly given without being identical to their objects. Allow for wrong letters, and if wrong letters, then wrong nouns, and if wrong nouns, then imperfect sentences -- so long as the general character of the thing described is preserved.
summary: Socrates urges Cratylus to accept that names can vary in accuracy without ceasing to be names, as long as they preserve the general character of what they describe.
distilled: Socrates establishes that names need not be perfect copies to function -- they remain valid so long as they preserve the general character of their objects.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Cratylus (brief reply). -- The principle of "general character" (typos) being sufficient for naming is crucial for Plato's broader philosophy. It anticipates the concept of participation (methexis) in the Forms -- particulars need not perfectly replicate a Form to participate in it. Compare Phaedo 74d-75a, where equal sticks "fall short" of Equality itself yet still participate in it. The scaling argument -- from letters to nouns to sentences -- mirrors the compositional structure of language that the Sophist will analyze more formally. The reference back to the discussion with Hermogenes grounds the argument in the earlier parts of the dialogue, maintaining continuity.

narrative: "Good; and when the general character is preserved, even if some of the proper letters are wanting, still the thing is signified; -- well, if all the letters are given; not well, when only a few of them are given. I think that we had better admit this, lest we be punished like travellers in Aegina who wander about the street late at night: and be likewise told by truth herself that we have arrived too late; or if not, you must find out some new notion of correctness of names, and no longer maintain that a name is the expression of a thing in letters or syllables; for if you say both, you will be inconsistent with yourself."
refashioned: "Good. When the general character is preserved, the thing is signified -- well, if all the proper letters are present; less well, if only a few are given. But the thing is still signified. We had better admit this, or we will be like travelers in Aegina who wander the streets after dark and are told they have arrived too late. Truth herself will say the same to us. Otherwise, you must come up with an entirely new theory of the correctness of names and stop maintaining that a name expresses a thing through letters and syllables -- because if you insist on both perfect replication and the image theory, you will contradict yourself."
elegant: When the general character is preserved, a thing is signified -- well with all proper letters, less well with fewer, but signified nonetheless. Accept this or find a new theory of names entirely, because insisting on both perfect letter-accuracy and the image theory produces a contradiction.
summary: Socrates argues that names signify their objects even with imperfect letters, as long as general character is preserved. Cratylus must either accept this or abandon his theory entirely.
distilled: Names signify well with all proper letters and less well with fewer, but they signify nonetheless -- and Cratylus must accept this or face self-contradiction.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The reference to Aegina's curfew laws is a vivid Socratic touch -- travelers who arrive after dark face penalties, just as those who arrive at philosophical truth too late face the penalty of self-contradiction. Aegina, an island near Athens, was known for strict civic regulations. The logical point is sharp: Cratylus cannot simultaneously hold that (1) names are imitations of things and (2) any imperfection in the imitation destroys the name entirely. Since he has already conceded that perfect imitation produces a duplicate rather than an image (3.9-3.10), he must accept that names function as imperfect representations. This argument closely parallels the structure of Parmenides 132d-133a.

narrative:
"I quite acknowledge, Socrates, what you say to be very reasonable."

"Then as we are agreed thus far, let us ask ourselves whether a name rightly imposed ought not to have the proper letters."

"Yes."

"And the proper letters are those which are like the things?"

"Yes."

"Enough then of names which are rightly given. And in names which are incorrectly given, the greater part may be supposed to be made up of proper and similar letters, or there would be no likeness; but there will be likewise a part which is improper and spoils the beauty and formation of the word: you would admit that?"

"There would be no use, Socrates, in my quarrelling with you, since I cannot be satisfied that a name which is incorrectly given is a name at all."

"Do you admit a name to be the representation of a thing?"

"Yes, I do."

"But do you not allow that some nouns are primitive, and some derived?"

"Yes, I do."
refashioned:
Cratylus acknowledges Socrates' reasoning is sound. They agree that a rightly imposed name should contain the proper letters -- those that resemble the things being named.

Socrates then addresses incorrectly given names: even these must be mostly composed of proper, fitting letters, or there would be no likeness at all. But they also contain improper elements that mar the word's formation. Can Cratylus accept this?

Cratylus remains uneasy: there is no point in quarreling, he says, but he still cannot accept that an incorrectly given name is truly a name at all.

Socrates redirects: does Cratylus at least admit that a name is a representation of a thing? Yes. And that some nouns are primitive while others are derived? Yes.
elegant: Correctly given names contain letters resembling the things named. Incorrectly given names still contain mostly fitting letters -- otherwise there would be no likeness at all -- but include improper elements that mar the word. Cratylus remains uneasy, unable to accept that an incorrect name is still a name. But he concedes that names are representations and that some nouns are primitive while others are derived.
summary: Socrates argues that even incorrect names contain mostly fitting letters, and Cratylus, though still uncomfortable with the idea of incorrect names, concedes that names are representations and that nouns can be primitive or derived.
distilled: Even imperfect names must contain mostly appropriate letters to function as representations, and Cratylus grudgingly concedes the distinction between primitive and derived nouns.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- Cratylus's persistent discomfort reveals the tension between his intellectual concessions and his deep-seated naturalist commitments. His position -- that an incorrect name is no name at all -- is logically analogous to the Parmenidean claim that what is not cannot be spoken. The distinction between primitive and derived nouns is important for the argument to come: if derived nouns get their meaning from primitive ones, then the accuracy of the entire naming system depends on how well the primitive nouns imitate reality. This sets up Socrates' argument about convention in 3.15-3.17. Compare Republic 533d-534a on the importance of getting first principles right.

narrative: "Then if you admit that primitive or first nouns are representations of things, is there any better way of framing representations than by assimilating them to the objects as much as you can; or do you prefer the notion of Hermogenes and of many others, who say that names are conventional, and have a meaning to those who have agreed about them, and who have previous knowledge of the things intended by them, and that convention is the only principle; and whether you abide by our present convention, or make a new and opposite one, according to which you call small great and great small -- that, they would say, makes no difference, if you are only agreed. Which of these two notions do you prefer?"
refashioned: "If you agree that primitive nouns are representations of things, then is there any better way to construct representations than by making them resemble their objects as closely as possible? Or do you prefer the view of Hermogenes and many others -- that names are purely conventional, meaningful only to those who have agreed upon them and already know the things referred to? On that view, convention is the only principle at work, and it makes no difference whether you call small things 'great' and great things 'small,' so long as everyone agrees. Which of these two positions do you prefer?"
elegant: If primitive nouns are representations, is the best method to make them resemble their objects as closely as possible? Or is Hermogenes right that names are purely conventional -- that it makes no difference what sounds we assign, so long as everyone agrees?
summary: Socrates presents the core choice: is natural resemblance or pure convention the basis of naming? He frames this as a decision between his own theory and Hermogenes' conventionalism.
distilled: Socrates forces a choice between naturalism -- names should resemble their objects -- and conventionalism -- names mean whatever people agree they mean.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage crystallizes the central opposition of the entire Cratylus: naturalism (Cratylus's view that names inherently fit their objects) versus conventionalism (Hermogenes' view that names are arbitrary agreements). Socrates is about to show that both positions contain partial truth. The conventionalist position echoes the sophistic tradition, particularly Protagoras's relativism in the Theaetetus (152a): if man is the measure of all things, then naming conventions are as valid as any other human construction. The example of calling small things "great" and vice versa highlights the apparent absurdity of pure conventionalism while also pointing to its logical coherence -- if everyone switches, communication proceeds unimpeded.

narrative:
"Representation by likeness, Socrates, is infinitely better than representation by any chance sign."

"Very good: but if the name is to be like the thing, the letters out of which the first names are composed must also be like things. Returning to the image of the picture, I would ask, How could any one ever compose a picture which would be like anything at all, if there were not pigments in nature which resembled the things imitated, and out of which the picture is composed?"

"Impossible."

"No more could names ever resemble any actually existing thing, unless the original elements of which they are compounded bore some degree of resemblance to the objects of which the names are the imitation: And the original elements are letters?"

"Yes."

"Let me now invite you to consider what Hermogenes and I were saying about sounds. Do you agree with me that the letter rho is expressive of rapidity, motion, and hardness? Were we right or wrong in saying so?"

"I should say that you were right."

"And that lambda was expressive of smoothness, and softness, and the like?"

"There again you were right."

"And yet, as you are aware, that which is called by us sklerotes, is by the Eretrians called skleroter."

"Very true."

"But are the letters rho and sigma equivalents; and is there the same significance to them in the termination rho, which there is to us in sigma, or is there no significance to one of us?"

"Nay, surely there is a significance to both of us."

"In as far as they are like, or in as far as they are unlike?"

"In as far as they are like."

"Are they altogether alike?"

"Yes; for the purpose of expressing motion."

"And what do you say of the insertion of the lambda? for that is expressive not of hardness but of softness."

"Why, perhaps the letter lambda is wrongly inserted, Socrates, and should be altered into rho, as you were saying to Hermogenes and in my opinion rightly, when you spoke of adding and subtracting letters upon occasion."

"Good. But still the word is intelligible to both of us; when I say skleros (hard), you know what I mean."

"Yes, my dear friend, and the explanation of that is custom."
refashioned:
Cratylus declares that representation by likeness is infinitely superior to representation by arbitrary signs.

Socrates agrees, but presses the implication: if names are to resemble things, then the letters composing them must also resemble things. Just as a painter cannot create a likeness without pigments that naturally resemble what is being depicted, names cannot resemble existing things unless their basic elements -- letters -- bear some resemblance to the objects they imitate.

Cratylus concurs and agrees that rho expresses rapidity, motion, and hardness, while lambda expresses smoothness and softness. But Socrates introduces a complication: the Athenians say "sklerotes" (hardness), while the Eretrians say "skleroter." Both rho and sigma serve the same function in the two dialects. Are these equivalent letters alike or unlike?

Cratylus says they are alike -- at least for expressing motion. But Socrates points out that "sklerotes" contains lambda, which expresses softness, not hardness. Cratylus suggests the lambda is simply a mistake that should be replaced by rho.

Socrates concedes the point but observes: the word remains intelligible to both of them. When Socrates says "skleros," Cratylus knows exactly what he means.

"Yes," Cratylus replies, "and the explanation for that is custom."
elegant: Cratylus prefers representation by likeness over arbitrary signs. But Socrates shows the problem: the word for "hardness" (sklerotes) contains lambda, which expresses softness -- the opposite of what it names. Different dialects use different letters for the same meaning. Cratylus suggests the lambda is simply a mistake. Socrates counters: regardless of imperfect letters, the word is still intelligible. Cratylus attributes this intelligibility to custom -- inadvertently conceding a role for convention.
summary: Through the example of dialectal variations in the Greek word for 'hardness,' Socrates shows that names can be intelligible even when their component letters do not perfectly match the qualities they represent. Cratylus attributes this to custom, inadvertently conceding a role for convention.
distilled: Socrates demonstrates that words remain intelligible despite containing letters that contradict the qualities they name, forcing Cratylus to attribute this intelligibility to custom -- which is convention by another name.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- This passage is a masterful dialectical trap. By getting Cratylus to invoke "custom" to explain intelligibility despite imperfect letter-resemblance, Socrates sets up the argument in 3.16-3.17 that custom is simply convention under a different name. The Eretrian dialect variant (skleroter vs. sklerotes) provides empirical evidence that different sound-patterns can convey the same meaning -- a direct challenge to strict sound-symbolism. Eretria was a city on the island of Euboea. The letter-sound associations (rho for motion, lambda for smoothness) were developed in Part 2 of the dialogue, where Socrates proposed that individual phonemes have intrinsic expressive properties -- a theory he is now subtly undermining.

narrative:
"And what is custom but convention? I utter a sound which I understand, and you know that I understand the meaning of the sound: this is what you are saying?"

"Yes."

"And if when I speak you know my meaning, there is an indication given by me to you?"

"Yes."
refashioned:
"And what is custom but convention? When I utter a sound that I understand, and you know that I understand the meaning of that sound -- that is what you are describing, is it not?"

"Yes."

"And if you grasp my meaning when I speak, then I have successfully communicated an indication to you?"

"Yes."
elegant: What is custom but convention? If I utter a sound and you understand my meaning, I have successfully communicated -- and that is precisely what convention accomplishes.
summary: Socrates identifies custom with convention: if a speaker utters a sound and a listener grasps the meaning, communication has occurred through shared agreement rather than natural resemblance.
distilled: Socrates collapses the distinction between custom and convention, showing that intelligible communication depends on shared agreement about what sounds mean.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Cratylus. -- This is the pivotal moment where Socrates reveals that Cratylus's appeal to "custom" (ethos) is functionally identical to Hermogenes' appeal to "convention" (syntheke). The equation of the two terms undermines Cratylus's attempt to maintain a distinction between his naturalist position and the conventionalist one. This dialectical move -- showing that an opponent's position, when pressed, reduces to the very position they reject -- is characteristic of Socratic elenchus. Compare Theaetetus 171a-c, where Protagoras's relativism is shown to be self-refuting. The argument also anticipates modern philosophy of language debates between causal/natural theories of reference (Kripke, Putnam) and use-based theories (Wittgenstein).

narrative: "This indication of my meaning may proceed from unlike as well as from like, for example in the lambda of sklerotes. But if this is true, then you have made a convention with yourself, and the correctness of a name turns out to be convention, since letters which are unlike are indicative equally with those which are like, if they are sanctioned by custom and convention. And even supposing that you distinguish custom from convention ever so much, still you must say that the signification of words is given by custom and not by likeness, for custom may indicate by the unlike as well as by the like. But as we are agreed thus far, Cratylus (for I shall assume that your silence gives consent), then custom and convention must be supposed to contribute to the indication of our thoughts; for suppose we take the instance of number, how can you ever imagine, my good friend, that you will find names resembling every individual number, unless you allow that which you term convention and agreement to have authority in determining the correctness of names? I quite agree with you that words should as far as possible resemble things; but I fear that this dragging in of resemblance, as Hermogenes says, is a shabby thing, which has to be supplemented by the mechanical aid of convention with a view to correctness; for I believe that if we could always, or almost always, use likenesses, which are perfectly appropriate, this would be the most perfect state of language; as the opposite is the most imperfect. But let me ask you, what is the force of names, and what is the use of them?"
refashioned:
"This indication of meaning can proceed from letters that are unlike the thing as readily as from letters that are like it -- as we saw with the lambda in 'sklerotes.' If that is true, then you have effectively established a convention with yourself, and the correctness of a name turns out to depend on convention, since unlike letters can indicate meaning just as well as like ones, provided they are sanctioned by custom.

Even if you insist on distinguishing custom from convention as strongly as you please, you must still admit that the signification of words depends on custom rather than likeness, since custom can indicate through unlike sounds as well as like ones.

Since we are agreed on this -- I will take your silence as consent -- we must accept that custom and convention contribute to the expression of our thoughts. Consider numbers: how could you possibly find names that resemble every individual number unless you grant convention and agreement authority in determining the correctness of names?

I agree with you entirely that words should resemble things as much as possible. But I fear that insisting on resemblance alone is, as Hermogenes put it, a 'shabby' approach, which must be supplemented by the practical aid of convention. If we could always use perfectly appropriate likenesses, that would be the ideal state of language; the opposite would be the most imperfect. But let me ask you: what is the purpose and use of names?"
elegant: Convention contributes to naming whether Cratylus admits it or not, because unlike letters indicate meaning just as effectively as like ones, provided custom sanctions them. Words should resemble things as far as possible -- that is the ideal -- but pure resemblance alone cannot account for all of language. Convention must supplement likeness. The question then becomes: what is the actual purpose of names?
summary: Socrates demonstrates that convention necessarily supplements natural resemblance in language, since unlike sounds convey meaning through custom. He accepts the ideal of resemblance but argues it must be supplemented by convention, then turns to the fundamental question of what names are for.
distilled: Socrates concludes that language necessarily combines natural resemblance with convention, since unlike sounds communicate meaning through custom, and then pivots to ask what purpose names ultimately serve.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage represents Socrates' synthesis of the naturalist and conventionalist positions -- neither pure resemblance nor pure convention suffices, and actual language requires both. This synthetic conclusion is characteristically Platonic, reflecting the method of the Statesman (where the Stranger weaves together seemingly opposed elements) and anticipating Aristotle's famous declaration in De Interpretatione (16a) that words signify "by convention" (kata syntheken). The reference to numbers as an example of where resemblance fails is particularly effective -- there is nothing about the sound "seven" that resembles the quantity seven. Socrates' phrase about "dragging in resemblance" being "shabby" is self-deprecating, acknowledging the limits of the theory he spent Part 2 elaborating. The transition to asking about the use of names opens the final movement of the dialogue.

narrative:
"The use of names, Socrates, as I should imagine, is to inform: the simple truth is, that he who knows names knows also the things which are expressed by them."

"I suppose you mean to say, Cratylus, that as the name is, so also is the thing; and that he who knows the one will also know the other, because they are similars, and all similars fall under the same art or science; and therefore you would say that he who knows names will also know things."

"That is precisely what I mean."

"But let us consider what is the nature of this information about things which, according to you, is given us by names. Is it the best sort of information? or is there any other? What do you say?"

"I believe that to be both the only and the best sort of information about them; there can be no other."

"But do you believe that in the discovery of them, he who discovers the names discovers also the things; or is this only the method of instruction, and is there some other method of enquiry and discovery."

"I certainly believe that the methods of enquiry and discovery are of the same nature as instruction."

"Well, but do you not see, Cratylus, that he who follows names in the search after things, and analyses their meaning, is in great danger of being deceived?"

"How so?"

"Why clearly he who first gave names gave them according to his conception of the things which they signified -- did he not?"

"True."

"And if his conception was erroneous, and he gave names according to his conception, in what position shall we who are his followers find ourselves? Shall we not be deceived by him?"
refashioned:
Cratylus says the use of names is to inform. Whoever knows names knows the things expressed by them. Socrates clarifies: Cratylus means that since names resemble things, knowledge of one yields knowledge of the other -- all similar things fall under the same art or science.

Cratylus confirms this is precisely his meaning and further claims that names provide the only and best information about things -- there is no other kind.

Socrates probes deeper: is this true for discovery as well as instruction? Does the person who discovers the names also discover the things? Cratylus says yes -- inquiry and discovery work the same way as instruction.

Socrates objects: anyone who follows names in the search for things, analyzing their etymologies, is in great danger of being deceived. The original name-giver assigned names according to his own understanding of things. If that understanding was wrong, and he gave names based on a flawed conception, what happens to those of us who follow his lead? Will we not be deceived?
elegant: Cratylus claims names are the only and best source of knowledge about things -- knowing names means knowing the things themselves. Socrates sees the danger: the original name-giver assigned names based on his own understanding. If that understanding was flawed, everyone who learns from those names inherits the error.
summary: Cratylus claims that knowing names is the only way to know things. Socrates raises a critical objection: if the original name-giver had a flawed understanding, all subsequent users inherit his errors.
distilled: Socrates undermines the claim that names are the primary route to knowledge by arguing that the original name-giver's errors would be inherited by all who learn from his names.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- This passage initiates the epistemological climax of the Cratylus. Cratylus's claim that names are the sole path to knowledge represents the strongest form of linguistic determinism -- the idea that our understanding of reality is entirely mediated by language. Socrates' objection anticipates the modern concept of the "Sapir-Whorf hypothesis" and its critique: if language shapes thought, what happens when language is wrong? The argument about inheriting the name-giver's errors parallels Socrates' methodological principle in Republic 510c-511d about the importance of examining first principles rather than building upon unexamined assumptions. It also connects to Theaetetus 201c-210b, where Socrates examines whether knowledge can be reduced to true belief plus an account (logos) -- and finds that the account itself requires prior knowledge.

narrative: "But, Socrates, am I not right in thinking that he must surely have known; or else, as I was saying, his names would not be names at all? And you have a clear proof that he has not missed the truth, and the proof is -- that he is perfectly consistent. Did you ever observe in speaking that all the words which you utter have a common character and purpose?"
refashioned: "But Socrates, surely the original name-giver must have known the truth. Otherwise, as I have been saying, his names would not be names at all. And there is clear proof that he did not miss the truth: he is perfectly consistent. Have you not noticed in speaking that all the words you use share a common character and purpose?"
elegant: Cratylus insists the original name-giver must have known the truth, since otherwise his names would not be names at all. His proof: the system is perfectly consistent -- all words share a common character and purpose.
summary: Cratylus defends the original name-giver's accuracy by arguing that the internal consistency of language proves its truth -- all words share a common direction and purpose.
distilled: Cratylus argues that the systematic consistency of language proves the original name-giver must have known the truth.
notes: Speaker: Cratylus. -- Cratylus's appeal to internal consistency as proof of truth is a sophisticated but ultimately fallacious argument that Socrates will dismantle in 3.20. The "common character and purpose" Cratylus detects in all words is the Heraclitean doctrine of universal flux -- he believes all names express motion and change, proving that reality itself is in constant flux. This is a form of coherentism: the theory is validated by its internal consistency rather than by correspondence to an independent reality. Socrates will show (3.20) that internal consistency is compatible with systematic error, just as a geometric proof can be consistently wrong if its first premises are flawed. Compare Theaetetus 171a-c, where the internal consistency of Protagoras's relativism is shown to undermine itself.

narrative:
"But that, friend Cratylus, is no answer. For if he did begin in error, he may have forced the remainder into agreement with the original error and with himself; there would be nothing strange in this, any more than in geometrical diagrams, which have often a slight and invisible flaw in the first part of the process, and are consistently mistaken in the long deductions which follow. And this is the reason why every man should expend his chief thought and attention on the consideration of his first principles: -- are they or are they not rightly laid down? and when he has duly sifted them, all the rest will follow. Now I should be astonished to find that names are really consistent. And here let us revert to our former discussion: Were we not saying that all things are in motion and progress and flux, and that this idea of motion is expressed by names? Do you not conceive that to be the meaning of them?"

"Yes; that is assuredly their meaning, and the true meaning."
refashioned:
"But that is no real answer, Cratylus. If the name-giver began with an error, he may well have forced everything else into alignment with that original mistake. There would be nothing surprising about that -- it happens all the time in geometry, where a slight, invisible flaw in the first step of a proof leads to consistently wrong conclusions all the way through. This is exactly why every person should devote their greatest attention to examining first principles: are they rightly established or not? Once you have properly tested your foundations, everything else will follow.

I would actually be astonished if names turned out to be truly consistent. But let us return to our earlier discussion. Were we not saying that all things are in motion, progress, and flux, and that this idea of motion is what names express? Is that not how you understand them?"

"Yes, that is certainly their meaning -- and the true meaning."
elegant: Internal consistency proves nothing if the starting point is wrong. In geometry, a small error in the first step produces consistently wrong conclusions throughout. This is why first principles demand the greatest scrutiny. If the name-giver began with a flawed premise -- say, that everything is in flux -- all his names would consistently reflect that error. Cratylus confirms that he does indeed see all names as expressing motion and flux.
summary: Socrates argues that internal consistency does not prove truth -- a system can be consistently wrong if built on a flawed first principle, like a geometric proof with an invisible error in step one. He then identifies the specific first principle in question: the Heraclitean doctrine that all things are in flux.
distilled: Socrates demolishes the argument from consistency by showing that a system built on a single error can be coherently and comprehensively wrong -- like a geometric proof flawed at its foundation.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Cratylus. -- This is one of the most important methodological passages in all of Plato. The geometric analogy -- where a small initial error propagates consistently through all subsequent deductions -- is a foundational argument for the priority of examining first principles, developed at length in Republic VI-VII (510c-511e), where Socrates distinguishes mathematical reasoning (which proceeds from unexamined hypotheses) from dialectic (which ascends to unhypothetical first principles). The identification of Heraclitean flux as the name-giver's potentially flawed first principle sets up the climactic argument of the dialogue (3.26-3.28), where Socrates will argue that knowledge requires stable, unchanging objects -- the Forms.

narrative: "Let us revert to episteme (knowledge) and observe how ambiguous this word is, seeming rather to signify stopping the soul at things than going round with them; and therefore we should leave the beginning as at present, and not reject the epsilon, but make an insertion of an iota instead of an epsilon (not pioteme, but epiisteme). Take another example: bebaion (sure) is clearly the expression of station and position, and not of motion. Again, the word istoria (enquiry) bears upon the face of it the stopping (istanai) of the stream; and the word piston (faithful) certainly indicates cessation of motion; then, again, mneme (memory), as any one may see, expresses rest in the soul, and not motion. Moreover, words such as amartia and sumphora, which have a bad sense, viewed in the light of their etymologies will be the same as sunesis and episteme and other words which have a good sense (compare omartein, sunienai, epesthai, sumpheresthai); and much the same may be said of amathia and akolasia, for amathia may be explained as e ama theo iontos poreia, and akolasia as e akolouthia tois pragmasin. Thus the names which in these instances we find to have the worst sense, will turn out to be framed on the same principle as those which have the best. And any one I believe who would take the trouble might find many other examples in which the giver of names indicates, not that things are in motion or progress, but that they are at rest; which is the opposite of motion."
refashioned:
"Let us look again at 'episteme' (knowledge) and notice how ambiguous it is. It seems to signify stopping the soul at things rather than moving along with them. We should keep its opening letters as they are but read the epsilon as expressing 'epi-histemi' -- standing upon -- rather than motion.

Consider other examples: 'bebaion' (sure, certain) clearly expresses stability and fixed position, not motion. 'Istoria' (inquiry) bears on its face the idea of stopping (histanai) the stream of experience. 'Piston' (faithful) indicates the cessation of motion. 'Mneme' (memory) expresses rest in the soul, not movement.

Furthermore, words with negative meanings, when analyzed etymologically, turn out to be structured on the same principle as positive words. 'Amartia' (error) and 'sumphora' (misfortune) can be parsed to express the same ideas of following and accompanying as 'sunesis' (understanding) and 'episteme' (knowledge). Similarly, 'amathia' (ignorance) can be read as 'the journey of one who travels with god,' and 'akolasia' (licentiousness) as 'following along with things.'

So the names we consider worst in meaning are constructed on the same principle as those with the best meanings. And anyone who took the trouble could find many more examples where the name-giver expresses not motion and progress but rest -- the very opposite of motion."
elegant: Many Greek words suggest rest rather than motion. 'Episteme' (knowledge) implies the soul standing firm. 'Bebaion' (certainty) expresses stability. 'Istoria' (inquiry) suggests stopping the stream. 'Mneme' (memory) expresses rest in the soul. Even words with negative meanings, analyzed etymologically, follow the same structural principles as positive ones. The name-giver did not consistently express motion -- many names point toward the opposite.
summary: Socrates provides counter-examples showing that many Greek words etymologically suggest rest and stability rather than motion, undermining the claim that all names express Heraclitean flux.
distilled: Socrates demonstrates through etymological analysis that many fundamental Greek words -- knowledge, certainty, inquiry, memory -- express rest and stability rather than Heraclitean motion.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage systematically dismantles the Heraclitean interpretation of language that Socrates himself developed in Part 2. The etymological analyses here deliberately mirror and contradict the earlier analyses, showing that the same method can yield opposite conclusions depending on one's starting assumptions -- which is precisely the point about examining first principles from 3.20. The words analyzed are philosophically significant: episteme (knowledge), bebaion (certainty), istoria (inquiry), piston (faithfulness), mneme (memory) -- all central concepts in Greek epistemology. The argument that words with bad meanings can be parsed to have good meanings (and vice versa) further demonstrates the unreliability of etymological analysis. This connects to Phaedo 96a-99d, where Socrates describes his disillusionment with natural philosophy and his turn to examining logoi (accounts/arguments) instead.

narrative:
"Yes, Socrates, but observe; the greater number express motion."

"What of that, Cratylus? Are we to count them like votes? and is correctness of names the voice of the majority? Are we to say of whichever sort there are most, those are the true ones?"

"No; that is not reasonable."

"Certainly not. But let us have done with this question and proceed to another, about which I should like to know whether you think with me. Were we not lately acknowledging that the first givers of names in states, both Hellenic and barbarous, were the legislators, and that the art which gave names was the art of the legislator?"

"Quite true."

"Tell me, then, did the first legislators, who were the givers of the first names, know or not know the things which they named?"

"They must have known, Socrates."

"Why, yes, friend Cratylus, they could hardly have been ignorant."

"I should say not."

"Let us return to the point from which we digressed. You were saying, if you remember, that he who gave names must have known the things which he named; are you still of that opinion?"

"I am."

"And would you say that the giver of the first names had also a knowledge of the things which he named?"

"I should."

"But how could he have learned or discovered things from names if the primitive names were not yet given? For, if we are correct in our view, the only way of learning and discovering things, is either to discover names for ourselves or to learn them from others."

"I think that there is a good deal in what you say, Socrates."

"But if things are only to be known through names, how can we suppose that the givers of names had knowledge, or were legislators before there were names at all, and therefore before they could have known them?"

"I believe, Socrates, the true account of the matter to be, that a power more than human gave things their first names, and that the names which are thus given are necessarily their true names."

"Then how came the giver of the names, if he was an inspired being or God, to contradict himself? For were we not saying just now that he made some names expressive of rest and others of motion? Were we mistaken?"
refashioned:
Cratylus objects that the majority of names express motion. Socrates dismisses this: "Are we going to count names like votes? Is correctness determined by majority rule?" Cratylus concedes this is unreasonable.

Socrates shifts to a deeper problem. The original legislators who gave names must have known the things they were naming. Cratylus agrees. But Socrates asks: how could the first name-giver have learned about things through names, if no names yet existed? If names are the only route to knowledge, the first name-giver could not have had knowledge before creating names.

Cratylus, pressed into a corner, appeals to the divine: a power greater than human gave things their first names, and those names are necessarily true.

Socrates sees the escape attempt and closes the door: if the name-giver was divine, how did he contradict himself? We have just shown that some names express rest and others express motion. Was the god inconsistent?
elegant: Cratylus objects that most names express motion. Socrates dismisses majority rule as a criterion for truth. He then raises a deeper problem: if the first name-giver learned things through names, how did he know things before names existed? Cratylus appeals to divine origin. Socrates counters: if a god gave the names, why do some express rest and others motion? The divine name-giver contradicted himself.
summary: Socrates dismisses the argument from majority, then poses a dilemma: if knowledge comes only through names, the first name-giver could not have known things before names existed. Cratylus appeals to divine origin, but Socrates notes that even a divine name-giver contradicted himself by creating names expressing both motion and rest.
distilled: Socrates traps Cratylus in a dilemma -- the first name-giver could not have learned from names that did not yet exist -- and when Cratylus appeals to divine origin, Socrates asks why a god would contradict himself with names expressing both motion and rest.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- The argument about the first name-giver is a regress problem: if knowledge requires names, but creating names requires knowledge, then no one could ever have created the first names. Cratylus's appeal to divine origin is a deus ex machina that Socrates immediately turns against him. The rejection of majority rule as a criterion for truth echoes Republic 493a-c, where Socrates criticizes the democratic tendency to equate popular opinion with wisdom. The broader logical structure -- showing that a theory's own premises prevent it from getting started -- appears in similar form in the Theaetetus (171a-c) against Protagorean relativism and in the Meno (80d-e) in Meno's paradox about the impossibility of inquiry. Cratylus's appeal to divine naming also connects to the Phaedrus (244b-245c), where Socrates discusses the value and limits of divine inspiration.

narrative:
"But I suppose one of the two not to be names at all."

"And which, then, did he make, my good friend; those which are expressive of rest, or those which are expressive of motion? This is a point which, as I said before, cannot be determined by counting them."

"No; not in that way, Socrates."
refashioned:
Cratylus retreats: he supposes that one of the two groups -- names expressing rest or names expressing motion -- are not really names at all.

Socrates presses: which ones did the divine name-giver actually create? The ones expressing rest, or the ones expressing motion? As established before, this cannot be settled by counting.

Cratylus concedes: "No, not in that way."
elegant: Cratylus tries to dismiss one group of names as not truly being names. Socrates asks: which group -- the ones expressing rest or motion? This cannot be determined by counting, and Cratylus has no other criterion to offer.
summary: Cratylus attempts to dismiss either the rest-names or motion-names as not genuine, but Socrates points out he has no criterion for deciding which group is authentic.
distilled: Cratylus's attempt to dismiss one group of names as inauthentic fails because he has no non-circular criterion for deciding which group the divine name-giver truly created.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- Cratylus's retreat here mirrors his earlier move with Hermogenes' name: when a name does not fit his theory, he declares it is not a name at all. This is an unfalsifiable strategy -- any counterevidence is simply defined away. Socrates' insistence that the question cannot be settled by counting reiterates his rejection of majority rule from 3.22. The impasse Cratylus faces -- unable to determine which names are genuine without an external standard -- sets up Socrates' crucial argument in 3.24 that we need a standard of truth independent of names altogether.

narrative: "But if this is a battle of names, some of them asserting that they are like the truth, others contending that THEY are, how or by what criterion are we to decide between them? For there are no other names to which appeal can be made, but obviously recourse must be had to another standard which, without employing names, will make clear which of the two are right; and this must be a standard which shows the truth of things."
refashioned: "If we are caught in a battle of names -- some asserting they are like the truth, others insisting that they are -- how do we decide between them? We cannot appeal to other names, since those would face the same challenge. Obviously we need a different standard altogether -- one that does not rely on names at all -- which can show us the truth of things directly and settle which set of names is right."
elegant: When names contradict each other, we cannot settle the dispute by appealing to more names. We need a standard independent of language altogether -- one that reveals the truth of things directly.
summary: Socrates argues that when names conflict, no appeal to further names can settle the dispute. A standard of truth independent of names is required to determine which names are correct.
distilled: Socrates establishes that conflicting names cannot be adjudicated by more names -- we need a standard of truth that is entirely independent of language.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This is the philosophical crux of the Cratylus. Socrates argues that language cannot be its own ultimate standard of truth -- there must be something beyond language against which language is measured. This "standard which shows the truth of things" points directly toward the Theory of Forms, which Plato develops in the Phaedo (74a-76e), Republic (474b-480a, 505a-509c), and Symposium (210a-212a). The argument has the structure of a transcendental argument: if names can be true or false, there must be something in virtue of which they are true or false, and that something cannot itself be a name. This insight connects to Aristotle's Metaphysics (1006a-1012a) on the principle of non-contradiction as the most certain principle, and to Wittgenstein's Tractatus (2.172) on the claim that a picture cannot depict its own form of representation.

narrative:
"I agree."

"But if that is true, Cratylus, then I suppose that things may be known without names?"

"Clearly."

"But how would you expect to know them? What other way can there be of knowing them, except the true and natural way, through their affinities, when they are akin to each other, and through themselves? For that which is other and different from them must signify something other and different from them."

"What you are saying is, I think, true."

"Well, but reflect; have we not several times acknowledged that names rightly given are the likenesses and images of the things which they name?"

"Yes."

"Let us suppose that to any extent you please you can learn things through the medium of names, and suppose also that you can learn them from the things themselves -- which is likely to be the nobler and clearer way; to learn of the image, whether the image and the truth of which the image is the expression have been rightly conceived, or to learn of the truth whether the truth and the image of it have been duly executed?"

"I should say that we must learn of the truth."

"How real existence is to be studied or discovered is, I suspect, beyond you and me. But we may admit so much, that the knowledge of things is not to be derived from names. No; they must be studied and investigated in themselves."

"Clearly, Socrates."
refashioned:
Cratylus agrees that an independent standard is needed, and therefore concedes that things can be known without names.

Socrates asks: how? The natural way is through the things' own affinities -- through their kinship with one another and through themselves. What is different from them can only signify something different.

Cratylus agrees. Socrates then poses the decisive question: granted that names are likenesses and images of things, and granted that you can learn either from the image or from the original -- which is the nobler and clearer path? Should you study the image to determine whether it accurately represents the truth, or study the truth to determine whether the image has been well executed?

Cratylus says: we must learn from the truth.

Socrates acknowledges that how real existence is to be studied may be beyond both of them. But they can at least agree on this: knowledge of things is not to be derived from names. Things must be studied and investigated in themselves.

Cratylus agrees.
elegant: If a standard beyond names is needed, then things can be known without names -- through their own natures and affinities.
