Given a choice between learning from the image or from the original, we should always learn from the truth itself. The knowledge of things cannot be derived from names; things must be studied in themselves.
summary: Socrates and Cratylus agree that things should be known through direct investigation rather than through names, since it is better to learn from the original than from the image. Knowledge cannot be derived from names alone.
distilled: Socrates establishes that genuine knowledge comes from studying things in themselves rather than through their names, since it is always better to learn from the original than from the copy.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- Socrates' conclusion that things must be "studied and investigated in themselves" points toward the method of dialectic described in Republic VII (531d-534e), where the philosopher ascends from images and hypotheses to the direct apprehension of Forms. The hierarchy -- truth is epistemically prior to images of truth -- recapitulates the Divided Line of Republic VI (509d-511e), where knowledge of Forms (noesis) is superior to knowledge of sensible particulars (pistis), which is in turn superior to knowledge of images (eikasia). Socrates' humble admission that "how real existence is to be studied is beyond you and me" is characteristic Socratic modesty, but also signals that the Cratylus is pointing toward questions that other dialogues will address more fully -- particularly the Phaedo, Symposium, and Republic.

narrative: "There is another point. I should not like us to be imposed upon by the appearance of such a multitude of names, all tending in the same direction. I myself do not deny that the givers of names did really give them under the idea that all things were in motion and flux; which was their sincere but, I think, mistaken opinion. And having fallen into a kind of whirlpool themselves, they are carried round, and want to drag us in after them. There is a matter, master Cratylus, about which I often dream, and should like to ask your opinion: Tell me, whether there is or is not any absolute beauty or good, or any other absolute existence?"
refashioned:
"There is another point I want to raise. We should not be misled by the sheer number of names that all seem to point in the same direction. I do not deny that the original name-givers truly believed that all things are in motion and flux -- that was their sincere conviction, but I think it was mistaken. Having fallen into a kind of whirlpool, they were swept along by it, and now they want to drag us in after them.

But there is something I often dream about, Cratylus, and I would like your opinion on it: tell me, is there or is there not such a thing as absolute beauty, or absolute good, or any other absolute existence?"
elegant: We should not be misled by the multitude of names pointing toward flux. The original name-givers sincerely believed everything was in motion, but I think they were wrong -- caught in a whirlpool, they want to drag us in after them. Here is my real question: is there such a thing as absolute beauty, absolute good, or any other absolute existence?
summary: Socrates warns against being swept up in the Heraclitean assumption embedded in language. He then poses the fundamental question: does absolute beauty, absolute good, or any other unchanging reality exist?
distilled: Socrates warns against the Heraclitean whirlpool encoded in language and poses the question that will determine everything: do absolute, unchanging realities exist?
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The "whirlpool" metaphor vividly captures how a pervasive assumption can become self-reinforcing -- if all your names presuppose flux, your language constantly confirms the doctrine. The phrase "I often dream" (pollakis oneirotto) uses the same language Socrates employs elsewhere for his deepest philosophical intuitions. The question about "absolute beauty" (auto to kalon) and "absolute good" (auto to agathon) introduces the Theory of Forms explicitly for the first time in this dialogue. "Auto to" -- "the thing itself" -- is Plato's standard formula for referring to Forms, used throughout the Phaedo (65d, 74a, 78d), Symposium (211a-b), and Republic (476a, 507b). This is the moment where the Cratylus connects to the central metaphysical project of Plato's middle dialogues. The claim that the name-givers were Heracliteans reflects Aristotle's later report (Metaphysics 987a) that the young Plato was influenced by the Heraclitean Cratylus before turning to Socratic philosophy.

narrative:
"Certainly, Socrates, I think so."

"Then let us seek the true beauty: not asking whether a face is fair, or anything of that sort, for all such things appear to be in a flux; but let us ask whether the true beauty is not always beautiful."

"Certainly."

"And can we rightly speak of a beauty which is always passing away, and is first this and then that; must not the same thing be born and retire and vanish while the word is in our mouths?"

"Undoubtedly."

"Then how can that be a real thing which is never in the same state? for obviously things which are the same cannot change while they remain the same; and if they are always the same and in the same state, and never depart from their original form, they can never change or be moved."

"Certainly they cannot."

"Nor yet can they be known by any one; for at the moment that the observer approaches, then they become other and of another nature, so that you cannot get any further in knowing their nature or state, for you cannot know that which has no state."

"True."
refashioned:
Cratylus agrees that absolute realities exist. Socrates then sets the terms of inquiry: we should not ask whether some particular face is beautiful -- such things are in flux. Instead, we should ask whether true beauty, beauty itself, is not always beautiful.

Cratylus agrees. Socrates continues: can we meaningfully speak of a beauty that is constantly changing, that is first one thing and then another? Such a thing would come into being, pass away, and vanish while we are still speaking about it.

He then draws out the implications: how can something be real if it is never in the same state? Things that remain the same cannot change while they remain the same. And if they are always the same, always in the same condition, never departing from their original form, they can never change or be moved.

But there is an even deeper problem: things in constant flux cannot even be known. The moment the observer approaches, they become something else entirely. You cannot know the nature or condition of something that has no stable condition to know.
elegant: True beauty must always be beautiful -- not a face that appears fair and then fades. If beauty were in constant flux, it would vanish while we speak of it. What is never in the same state cannot be real, since remaining the same excludes change. And what is always changing cannot even be known: the moment you approach it, it becomes something else. You cannot know what has no stable state.
summary: Socrates argues that if reality were in constant flux, nothing could be truly real or knowable. True beauty must be permanently beautiful, and anything in perpetual change would transform even as we try to apprehend it, making knowledge impossible.
distilled: Socrates argues that universal flux makes both reality and knowledge impossible -- what is always changing can neither truly exist nor be known, since it transforms the moment the observer approaches.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- This passage presents Plato's most direct argument against Heraclitean flux in the Cratylus, paralleling the fuller treatment in the Theaetetus (179c-183c), where Socrates shows that Protagorean-Heraclitean relativism ("everything is in motion") collapses into incoherence. The distinction between beautiful things (which change) and Beauty itself (which is eternal) is the core of the Theory of Forms as presented in the Symposium (210e-211b), where Diotima describes Beauty itself as "always being, neither coming to be nor perishing." The epistemological argument -- that knowledge requires a stable object -- is developed in Republic V (476e-480a), where Socrates distinguishes knowledge (of what is) from opinion (of what becomes). The claim that things in flux "become other" the moment the observer approaches anticipates modern discussions of the observer effect in quantum mechanics, though the philosophical point is quite different.

narrative: "Nor can we reasonably say, Cratylus, that there is knowledge at all, if everything is in a state of transition and there is nothing abiding; for knowledge too cannot continue to be knowledge unless continuing always to abide and exist. But if the very nature of knowledge changes, at the time when the change occurs there will be no knowledge; and if the transition is always going on, there will always be no knowledge, and, according to this view, there will be no one to know and nothing to be known: but if that which knows and that which is known exists ever, and the beautiful and the good and every other thing also exist, then I do not think that they can resemble a process or flux, as we were just now supposing. Whether there is this eternal nature in things, or whether the truth is what Heracleitus and his followers and many others say, is a question hard to determine; and no man of sense will like to put himself or the education of his mind in the power of names: neither will he so far trust names or the givers of names as to be confident in any knowledge which condemns himself and other existences to an unhealthy state of unreality; he will not believe that all things leak like a pot, or imagine that the world is a man who has a running at the nose. This may be true, Cratylus, but is also very likely to be untrue; and therefore I would not have you be too easily persuaded of it. Reflect well and like a man, and do not easily accept such a doctrine; for you are young and of an age to learn. And when you have found the truth, come and tell me."
refashioned:
"Nor can we reasonably say there is knowledge at all if everything is in transition and nothing abides. Knowledge itself cannot remain knowledge unless it continues to exist without change. If the very nature of knowledge changes, then at the moment of change there is no knowledge. And if change is perpetual, there is perpetually no knowledge -- no one to know, and nothing to be known.

But if that which knows and that which is known do exist permanently -- if beauty and goodness and every other such reality truly endure -- then they cannot resemble a process or a flux, as we were supposing.

Whether things have this eternal nature, or whether Heraclitus and his followers are right, is genuinely hard to determine. But no person of good sense will surrender themselves or the education of their mind to the authority of names. No one should trust names or their creators so far as to become confident in a worldview that condemns all existence to an unhealthy state of unreality -- believing that all things leak like a cracked pot, or imagining the world is a man with a runny nose.

Perhaps this is true, Cratylus, but it is also very likely to be untrue. So do not be too easily persuaded. Reflect seriously, and do not accept such a doctrine lightly. You are young and at the age for learning. When you have found the truth, come and tell me."
elegant: If everything is in flux, knowledge itself cannot persist -- it changes into non-knowledge at every moment, leaving no knower and nothing known. But if the knower, the known, beauty, goodness, and every other reality truly endure, they cannot be processes or flux. Whether things have an eternal nature or Heraclitus is right is genuinely hard to determine. But no person of sense will place their mind at the mercy of names, or trust a worldview that reduces all existence to unreality -- as if everything leaks like a cracked pot. Do not accept this too easily. You are young. Think carefully, and when you find the truth, come tell me.
summary: Socrates argues that universal flux destroys knowledge itself, since knowledge requires stability. He urges Cratylus not to surrender his mind to the authority of names or accept the Heraclitean doctrine uncritically, since he is young enough to discover the truth for himself.
distilled: Socrates argues that if everything is in flux then knowledge itself is impossible, urges Cratylus not to place his mind at the mercy of names, and counsels him to think independently while he is still young enough to learn.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This is the philosophical climax of the Cratylus and one of the most important passages for understanding Plato's motivation for the Theory of Forms. The argument is: (1) knowledge requires stable objects; (2) if everything is in flux, nothing is stable; (3) therefore, if everything is in flux, there is no knowledge; (4) but there is knowledge; (5) therefore, not everything is in flux -- there must be eternal, unchanging realities. This argument is restated in Theaetetus 179c-183c and undergirds the metaphysics of the Phaedo (78c-79d) and Republic V-VII. The colorful images -- things leaking like pots, the world as a man with a runny nose -- are vivid Socratic mockery of Heraclitean doctrine. Socrates' counsel to the young Cratylus is poignant given the tradition (Aristotle, Metaphysics 987a32) that the historical Cratylus eventually took Heracliteanism to its extreme, refusing to speak at all and only pointing with his finger, since he believed words could not capture constantly changing reality. The closing exhortation to independent inquiry is quintessentially Socratic -- compare the closing of the Meno and the Theaetetus.

narrative:
"I will do as you say, though I can assure you, Socrates, that I have been considering the matter already, and the result of a great deal of trouble and consideration is that I incline to Heracleitus."

"Then, another day, my friend, when you come back, you shall give me a lesson; but at present, go into the country, as you are intending, and Hermogenes shall set you on your way."

"Very good, Socrates; I hope, however, that you will continue to think about these things yourself."
refashioned:
"I will do as you suggest, though I assure you, Socrates, I have been thinking about this for a long time already. After much trouble and reflection, I still incline toward Heraclitus."

"Then another day, my friend, when you return, you shall teach me. But for now, go to the country as you planned, and Hermogenes will see you on your way."

"Very good, Socrates. I hope, however, that you will keep thinking about these things yourself."
elegant: Cratylus admits that despite Socrates' arguments, he still inclines toward Heraclitus. Socrates invites him to return and teach him someday, sends him off to the country with Hermogenes, and receives a parting hope that he too will keep thinking.
summary: The dialogue closes with Cratylus admitting he still favors Heraclitus despite Socrates' arguments, and Socrates graciously sending him on his way with an invitation to continue the conversation another day.
distilled: Cratylus departs still inclined toward Heraclitus, and Socrates sends him off with the open-ended hope that both will continue thinking about the nature of names and reality.
notes: Speakers: Cratylus and Socrates. -- The dialogue ends without resolution, which is typical of Plato's early and transitional dialogues (compare the Euthyphro, Lysis, Charmides, and Theaetetus). Cratylus's persistence in favoring Heraclitus despite Socrates' arguments is dramatically significant: according to Aristotle (Metaphysics 987a32-b1), the historical Cratylus was a Heraclitean who influenced the young Plato, and his extreme commitment to flux eventually led him to abandon speech entirely. The casual closing -- Hermogenes accompanying Cratylus to the country -- returns us to the social world of Athens after the philosophical heights. Socrates' final gesture of mutual inquiry ("I hope you will continue to think about these things yourself") embodies the Socratic conviction that philosophy is an ongoing, cooperative pursuit rather than a body of finished doctrine. The unresolved questions about names, convention, nature, flux, and Forms point forward to the Theaetetus (on knowledge), the Sophist (on being, non-being, and false statement), and the Parmenides (on the problems of participation in Forms).
